## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Visual Angle for a Warning Light

**Problem Setup (KTU Typical):**
A circular warning light of diameter $h = 50$ mm is mounted on a control panel. An operator views it from a distance of $d = 2$ m. Calculate the visual angle subtended at the eye in:
(a) radians
(b) degrees
(c) arc-minutes

**Step-by-Step Solution:**

**Step 1: Identify the geometric relationship.**

A circular object of height $h$ at distance $d$ from the eye subtends a visual angle $\theta$ at the nodal point of the eye. By the small-angle approximation:

$$\theta = \arctan\!\left(\dfrac{h}{d}\right)$$

**Step 2: Substitute values and check the small-angle validity.**

The argument of arctangent:

$$\dfrac{h}{d} = \dfrac{0.050 \text{ m}}{2.0 \text{ m}} = 0.025$$

This is small (much less than 0.1), so the small-angle approximation holds:

$$\theta \approx \dfrac{h}{d} = 0.025 \text{ rad}$$

**Step 3: Convert radians to degrees.**

Using the conversion factor $1 \text{ rad} = 57.2958°$:

$$\theta_{deg} = 0.025 \times 57.2958 = 1.4324°$$

**Step 4: Convert degrees to arc-minutes.**

Since $1° = 60$ arc-minutes:

$$\theta_{arcmin} = 1.4324 \times 60 = 85.94 \text{ arc-min}$$

**Step 5: Engineering judgment.**

The minimum legible visual angle for a warning light symbol is typically **5 to 20 arc-minutes** for normal vision (Snellen 20/20, i.e., 1 arc-minute resolution). Our result of **85.94 arc-min** vastly exceeds this minimum, so the warning light is **highly legible** at the given viewing distance.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Valuation Tip:** Always show the small-angle check (ratio < 0.1). Examiners award 1 mark for correctly identifying and justifying the approximation.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Luminance Contrast for a Signal Light

**Problem Setup:**
A green "system safe" indicator light has a luminance of $L_s = 80$ cd/m² against a dark control panel background of luminance $L_b = 5$ cd/m². Calculate:
(a) Weber contrast ratio
(b) Comment on legibility per ISO 9241-303 standards

**Step-by-Step Solution:**

**Step 1: Recall the Weber contrast formula.**

$$C = \dfrac{\vert L_s - L_b \vert}{L_b}$$

**Step 2: Substitute the numerical values.**

$$C = \dfrac{\vert 80 - 5 \vert}{5} = \dfrac{75}{5} = 15$$

**Step 3: Interpret the result.**

The contrast ratio is **15:1** (or 15, dimensionless). According to the ISO 9241-303 standard:

- $C \geq 1$ (1:1) — minimum, marginal legibility
- $C \geq 3$ (3:1) — minimum acceptable for text
- $C \geq 7$ (7:1) — preferred for body text
- $C \geq 12$ — preferred for warning signs

A contrast of **15:1** is **excellent** and easily meets the warning-light threshold.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Weber contrast uses $L_b$ in the denominator?** This is the **Weber-Fechner psychophysical model**: the human perception of brightness difference is proportional to the *relative* change, not the absolute change. The denominator normalizes by the ambient adaptation level of the eye.

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Reaction Time Using Hick's Law

**Problem Setup:**
An operator is presented with a control panel with $n = 7$ different colored warning lights, each indicating a different fault condition. The system constants are $a = 0.15$ s and $b = 0.12$ s. Calculate the reaction time.

**Step-by-Step Solution:**

**Step 1: Apply Hick's Law.**

$$RT = a + b \cdot \log_2(n + 1)$$

**Step 2: Compute the information content term.**

$$\log_2(7 + 1) = \log_2(8) = 3 \text{ bits}$$

**Step 3: Substitute and calculate.**

$$RT = 0.15 + 0.12 \times 3 = 0.15 + 0.36 = 0.51 \text{ s}$$

**Step 4: Compare with simple reaction time benchmark.**

For a single warning light (n = 1):
$$RT_{simple} = 0.15 + 0.12 \cdot \log_2(2) = 0.15 + 0.12 = 0.27 \text{ s}$$

The 7-light panel **almost doubles** the reaction time compared to a single light, demonstrating the human-factors principle of **minimizing choice complexity** in warning system design.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Warning Light Modality Strategies

> [!NOTE]
> This analytical matrix is a high-yield KTU Module 2 comparison. Examiners frequently ask "compare and contrast" questions on this topic.

| Parameter | Steady Light | Flashing Light | Pulsed Coded Light | Color-Coded Light |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Conspicuity** | Low | **Very High** | High | Moderate–High |
| **Information Content** | 1 bit (on/off) | 1 bit (on/off) | **3+ bits** (pattern + on/off) | 2–3 bits (color + on/off) |
| **Detection Time** | Slow | **Fastest** | Fast | Fast |
| **Operator Cognitive Load** | Low | Low | Moderate | Low–Moderate |
| **False Alarm Risk** | Moderate | Low (attention-catching) | Low | Low |
| **Implementation Cost** | Lowest | Low | Moderate | Low–Moderate |
| **Failure Mode Visibility** | Poor (bulb-out undetected) | **Good** (failure obvious) | Good | Good |
| **Best Use Case** | Status indication | **Critical warnings** | Multi-state alerts | Categorical warnings |
| **Standard Reference** | Generic | FAA, ISO 11428 | IEC 60073 | ISO 3864, ANSI Z535 |

### 3.5 The Complete KTU Analytical Framework for Signal Design

The following multi-step engineering design flow is the **master analytical procedure** for designing a warning light system:

1. **Identify the hazard** and classify it by severity (catastrophic / critical / marginal / negligible per MIL-STD-882).
2. **Determine required response time** based on system dynamics.
3. **Select signal modality** (visual / auditory / tactile / multi-modal).
4. **Choose color code** per ISO 3864 / ANSI Z535.
5. **Select temporal pattern** (steady / flashing / coded) per criticality.
6. **Calculate required luminance** using the inverse square law and ambient conditions.
7. **Compute visual angle** at the maximum expected viewing distance.
8. **Verify contrast ratio** exceeds minimum threshold.
9. **Validate flash rate** is in the optimal 1–4 Hz range.
10. **Test for alarm desensitization** in operational conditions.
11. **Document operator training** requirements.
12. **Establish maintenance and testing** schedule.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 14-Mark Question Pattern:** Examiners frequently ask students to **apply this 12-step framework** to a scenario (e.g., "design a warning light system for a chemical reactor control panel"). A complete answer that follows these steps with proper formulas and justifications will score full marks.

### 4.6 Engineering Schematic: Standardized Stack Light (Tower Light)

A standard industrial stack light is a vertically arranged column of colored warning lights, where each tier conveys a different severity level. The functional architecture is shown below:

```mermaid
        BOT[Green Light — Tier 4: Normal Operation]

    MID1 --- MID2
    MID2 --- BOT

    CTRL --> MID1
    CTRL --> MID2
    CTRL --> BOT

```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### **PART A: Short-Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)**

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, PEEBT742]**
**Define the term "signal" in the context of human factors engineering. Distinguish between steady and flashing warning lights, citing one engineering application of each.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A **signal** is a deliberately engineered stimulus (visual, auditory, or tactile) that conveys specific information about system status to a human operator, prompting a predefined response.

- A **steady warning light** maintains continuous constant luminance, providing a single piece of information (ON/OFF state). *Application:* Power-on indicator on a control panel.
- A **flashing warning light** cycles periodically between ON and OFF states, providing enhanced conspicuity and attention capture due to the human visual system's sensitivity to temporal transients. *Application:* Master Warning light in an aircraft cockpit.

**Mark Distribution:** Definition (1M) + Distinction (1M) + Application (1M) = **3 Marks**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, PEEBT742]**
**State the international color code standards for warning lights. Why is "red" exclusively reserved for danger/emergency signals?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Color Code Standards (per ISO 3864-1 / ANSI Z535.1):**
- **Red** — Danger / Emergency / Stop
- **Amber/Yellow** — Warning / Caution
- **Green** — Safe / Normal operation
- **Blue** — Mandatory action / Notice
- **White** — Neutral / Status confirmation

**Why red is reserved for danger:**
Red is reserved exclusively for danger signals to **prevent alarm desensitization**. If red is used for non-critical signals, operators gradually learn to ignore red, which is catastrophic in genuine emergencies. Additionally, red light penetrates fog and smoke effectively (long wavelength) and has cross-cultural association with blood, fire, and danger, making it universally recognizable as a hazard cue. This convention is enforced in standards such as **IEC 60073** and **ISO 3864**.

**Mark Distribution:** Color codes (1M) + Reason for red reservation — alarm desensitization (1M) + Standard reference (1M) = **3 Marks**

---

### **PART B: Long-Answer Questions — Internal Choice (Choose ONE of TWO)**

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, PEEBT742, Module 2]**

**(a)** Explain the FIVE cardinal design principles of warning lights in human factors engineering. For each principle, give one real-world example. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A circular warning light of diameter 60 mm is mounted on a chemical plant control panel. The operator views it from a maximum distance of 2.5 m. The signal luminance is 200 cd/m² and the panel background luminance is 8 cd/m². Calculate:
   (i) The visual angle subtended at the eye in arc-minutes
   (ii) The Weber contrast ratio
   (iii) Comment on the legibility and conspicuity of the warning light with reference to ISO standards. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution — Question A (a) [7 Marks]**

The five cardinal principles are:

1. **Conspicuity (Detectability):** The signal must stand out from its background. *Example:* A flashing red beacon on a fire alarm pull station stands out against a beige office wall.

2. **Legibility (Readability):** The signal's color, label, or symbol must be interpretable at the required viewing distance. *Example:* Exit signs use a high-contrast red-on-white color scheme to remain legible at 30+ meters.

3. **Meaningfulness (Comprehensibility):** The signal must be interpretable as intended. *Example:* The green "go" traffic light is universally understood as permission to proceed.

4. **Distinctiveness (Non-Confusion):** Each signal must be uniquely distinguishable. *Example:* Aviation uses red for "DO NOT CROSS" runway lights, distinct from white runway edge lights.

5. **Compatibility (Expectation):** The signal's meaning must align with operator expectations. *Example:* Placing a red light on an emergency stop button is compatible with the global convention.

**Mark Distribution:** 5 principles × (1.0 M definition + 0.4 M example) = **7 Marks**

---

#### **Model Solution — Question A (b) [7 Marks]**

**Given Data:**
- Diameter $h = 60$ mm $= 0.060$ m
- Viewing distance $d = 2.5$ m
- Signal luminance $L_s = 200$ cd/m²
- Background luminance $L_b = 8$ cd/m²

---

**Sub-part (i): Visual Angle Calculation [3 Marks]**

The visual angle is given by the small-angle approximation:

$$\theta = \dfrac{h}{d} = \dfrac{0.060}{2.5} = 0.024 \text{ rad}$$

Convert to degrees:

$$\theta_{deg} = 0.024 \times 57.2958 = 1.3751°$$

Convert to arc-minutes:

$$\theta_{arcmin} = 1.3751 \times 60 = 82.51 \text{ arc-minutes}$$

---

**Sub-part (ii): Weber Contrast Ratio [2 Marks]**

$$C = \dfrac{\vert L_s - L_b \vert}{L_b} = \dfrac{\vert 200 - 8 \vert}{8} = \dfrac{192}{8} = 24$$

The contrast ratio is **24:1**.

---

**Sub-part (iii): Legibility & Conspicuity Comment [2 Marks]**

**Visual angle of 82.51 arc-minutes** is far above the minimum legible threshold of 5–20 arc-minutes for normal vision. The signal is **highly legible**.

**Contrast ratio of 24:1** exceeds the ISO 9241-303 preferred threshold of 7:1 and even surpasses the 12:1 warning-sign benchmark. The signal is **highly conspicuous**.

**Conclusion:** The warning light meets and exceeds ISO legibility and conspicuity requirements and is approved for deployment.

**Mark Distribution:** (i) 3M + (ii) 2M + (iii) 2M = **7 Marks**

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, PEEBT742, Module 2]**

**(a)** What is Critical Flicker Frequency (CFF)? Explain its significance in the design of flashing warning lights. A warning lamp has a luminance of 100 cd/m². Using the empirical relation $f_c = 12.5 \log(L) + 25$ Hz, calculate the CFF and recommend an appropriate flash rate for a critical alarm. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** With the help of a labeled block diagram, describe the four-tier industrial stack light (tower light) system. State the standard color code for each tier and justify the rationale for placing red at the top. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution — Question B (a) [7 Marks]**

**Definition of CFF [1 Mark]:**
The **Critical Flicker Frequency (CFF)** is the lowest flash frequency at which a flickering light source is perceived by the human eye as a continuous (steady) light. Below the CFF, the light is perceived as individual flashes; above it, the flicker fuses into a steady appearance.

**Significance in Warning Light Design [2 Marks]:**
CFF is significant because flashing warning lights must be **visibly flashing** to maximize conspicuity. If the flash rate exceeds CFF, the operator perceives the light as steady, **losing the attention-capturing benefit** of flashing. CFF depends on luminance: higher luminance → higher CFF. This relationship must guide flash-rate selection in the engineering design.

**Calculation [3 Marks]:**

$$f_c = 12.5 \log(100) + 25$$

Since $\log_{10}(100) = 2$:

$$f_c = 12.5 \times 2 + 25 = 25 + 25 = 50 \text{ Hz}$$

**Recommended Flash Rate [1 Mark]:**
For a critical alarm, the flash rate should be **well below 50 Hz** to ensure visible flashing. The recommended optimal range is **1 to 4 Hz (60–240 flashes per minute)** per FAA and ISO guidance. A flash rate of **2 Hz (120 fpm)** is recommended for this critical alarm application.

**Mark Distribution:** Definition (1M) + Significance (2M) + Calculation (3M) + Recommendation (1M) = **7 Marks**

---

#### **Model Solution — Question B (b) [7 Marks]**

**Block Diagram [2 Marks]:** (See the Mermaid schematic in Section 4.6.)

**Description of Four-Tier Stack Light [3 Marks]:**

A **stack light** (or **tower light / signal tower**) is a vertically mounted column of individually controlled colored lamps used in industrial automation to communicate machine status at a glance, even from a distance.

**Standard Tier Configuration (from top to bottom):**

| Tier | Color | Meaning (Per IEC/ISO Standards) |
|---|---|---|
| 1 (Top) | **Red** | Critical alarm / Emergency / Stop / Fault |
| 2 | **Amber** | Warning / Abnormal condition / Over-range |
| 3 | **Blue** | Mandatory action / Manual intervention required |
| 4 (Bottom) | **Green** | Normal operation / Running / Ready |

Often a 5th tier of **White** is included between Blue and Green for status confirmation.

**Rationale for Placing Red at the Top [2 Marks]:**
1. **Maximum visibility:** Operators instinctively look at the top of a column first, mirroring the natural eye-saccade pattern.
2. **Longest sight line:** In a vertically mounted tower, the topmost lamp is most often above eye-level, where the lamp's luminous intensity is least occluded by personnel, machinery, or other equipment.
3. **Universal convention:** Placing red on top matches the international convention (ISO 11428, IEC 60073, ANSI/NFPA 79) so operators can recognize the system without training.
4. **Conspicuity hierarchy:** The most severe warning is given the most visually prominent position.

**Mark Distribution:** Diagram (2M) + Tier description (3M) + Rationale (2M) = **7 Marks**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the small-angle justification** in visual angle problems → **1 mark deducted.**
> 2. **Using $L_s$ instead of $L_b$** in the contrast ratio denominator → **1–2 marks deducted.**
> 3. **Confusing CFF with optimal flash rate** — CFF is the *upper bound*; you should flash *below* CFF, not *at* CFF → **2 marks deducted.**
> 4. **Wrong color-tier order** in stack light (placing red at the bottom) → **2 marks deducted.**
> 5. **Not citing the standard** (ISO 3864, ANSI Z535, IEC 60073) — examiners expect at least one standard reference → **1 mark deducted.**
> 6. **Skipping units** in numerical answers → **0.5 mark per instance.**

---

### **Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember**

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist — KTU Module 2: Signals & Warning Lights**

- **Signal Definition:** A deliberately designed stimulus that communicates system status to a human operator.
- **Warning Light:** A visual signal using electromagnetic radiation (visible spectrum) to alert, caution, or inform.
- **5 Cardinal Principles:** Conspicuity, Legibility, Meaningfulness, Distinctiveness, Compatibility.
- **Color Codes (ISO 3864):** Red = Danger, Amber = Warning, Green = Safe, Blue = Notice, White = Neutral.
- **Red is exclusively for danger** — to prevent alarm desensitization (criterion: never use red for non-emergency).
- **Visual Angle Formula:** $\theta = h/d$ (radians), convert to degrees via $\times 57.296$, to arc-min via $\times 3438$.
- **Minimum legible visual angle:** 5–20 arc-minutes (normal vision, Snellen 20/20).
- **Weber Contrast Ratio:** $C = \vert L_s - L_b \vert / L_b$ (background in denominator, dimensionless).
- **Contrast thresholds:** 1:1 minimum, 3:1 text, 7:1 preferred, 12:1+ warning.
- **CFF (Critical Flicker Frequency):** ~30–60 Hz depending on luminance; flash rates must be *below* CFF.
- **Optimal flash rate for warnings:** 1–4 Hz (60–240 fpm) per FAA/ISO.
- **Hick's Law:** $RT = a + b \cdot \log_2(n+1)$ — more signal choices = slower response.
- **Simple visual reaction time:** ~0.25 s; auditory ~0.15 s; tactile ~0.13 s.
- **Inverse Square Law:** Light intensity falls off as $1/d^2$ — compensate by increasing source intensity or reducing distance.
- **Stevens' Power Law for Brightness:** $\psi = k \cdot \phi^{0.33}$.
- **Flashing > Steady for conspicuity** in cluttered environments.
- **Stack light convention (top to bottom):** Red → Amber → Blue → Green (+ optional White).
- **Standards to cite in exams:** ISO 3864-1, ANSI Z535.1, IEC 60073, ISO 11428, FAA AC 25.11.
- **Alarm desensitization** = a critical human-factors failure mode; mitigate via strict color hierarchy.
- **Redundancy principle:** Combine visual + auditory signals for critical warnings to maximize detection probability.
- **Detection threshold SNR ≥ 3** for reliable signal detection in noisy environments.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Representational Displays-Aircraft Bank Angle Displays-Principles of Aircraft Position Displays; Auditory Displays-Process of Hearing; Principles of Auditory Displays
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT742 - HUMAN FACTORS IN ENGINEERING AND DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Representational Displays-Aircraft Bank Angle Displays-Principles of Aircraft Position Displays; Auditory Displays-Process of Hearing; Principles of Auditory Displays

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 📘 MODULE 2 — REPRESENTATIONAL & AUDITORY DISPLAYS

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Representational Displays

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Scheme):**
> A **Representational Display** is a visual display device that depicts the *quantitative* or *qualitative* state of a system, variable, or process through a directly perceivable analog (or pictorial) mapping between the display element and the controlled variable. The display preserves a *one-to-one geometric, spatial, or functional correspondence* with the system being monitored (Wickens, Hollands — Engineering Psychology & Human Performance).

**Conceptual Analogy — The Speedometer of a Car:**
Imagine driving a car. The needle of the speedometer moves in a continuous arc that directly mirrors how fast the wheels are turning. The further right the needle moves, the faster the car moves. There is a *visual analogy* between needle position and speed. This is the essence of a **representational display** — the mapping is intuitive because the *form* of the display resembles the *function* of the variable.

| Type | Description | Aircraft Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Analog (Pictorial)** | Continuous physical scale | Attitude Indicator (AI) |
| **Quantitative (Numeric)** | Direct digital readout | Altimeter in feet |
| **Combined** | Pictorial + Numeric | HSI (Horizontal Situation Indicator) |

---

### 1.2 Aircraft Bank Angle Displays

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Aircraft Bank Angle** is the rotation of the aircraft about its *longitudinal (nose-to-tail) axis*. It is measured in **degrees of roll (φ)**, where **0° = wings level** and **±90° = knife-edge flight**.

A **Bank Angle Display** must communicate three things to the pilot *simultaneously*:
1. **Magnitude** of bank (how many degrees rolled).
2. **Direction** of bank (left wing down / right wing down).
3. **Rate of change** of bank (rolling speed).

The two principal flight instruments that perform this role are:

- **Attitude Indicator (AI)** — also called the *Artificial Horizon* — provides *both* pitch and bank.
- **Turn Coordinator (TC)** / **Turn-and-Slip Indicator** — supplements bank with rate information.

**Intuition:** Think of a pilot flying in clouds (IMC — Instrument Meteorological Conditions). He has *no* outside horizon. The Artificial Horizon acts as his "synthetic horizon" — a miniature airplane floating in a blue-sky/brown-earth box that tells him whether the real aircraft is wings-level, climbing, descending, banking left, or banking right.

---

### 1.3 Principles of Aircraft Position Displays

The classical Wickens / Boff & Lincoln / Human Factors literature identifies **six governing principles** for the design of aircraft position displays:

1. **Principle of the Moving Part** — The element that *moves* should represent the element that *moves* in the real world (e.g., a miniature aircraft symbol should remain stationary while the horizon bar rotates *behind* it — NOT the aircraft symbol moving relative to a fixed horizon, because this is more compatible with the pilot's vestibular frame of reference).
2. **Principle of Pictorial Realism** — The display should look like the real-world scene it represents.
3. **Principle of the Functional Whole** — Integrate related variables into one display rather than several discrete dials.
4. **Principle of Attention Capture / Preattentive Processing** — Critical information (e.g., bank > 35°) should be detectable in < 250 ms without serial search.
5. **Principle of Display-Centered vs. World-Centered Frames** — *World-centered* (outside-in, moving horizon) supports better spatial awareness; *display-centered* (inside-out, fixed aircraft symbol) supports better flight-control precision. The **modern PFD** compromises with a *world-centered* artificial horizon.
6. **Principle of Stereokinetic Cues / Perspective Compatibility** — 3-D perspective displays (e.g., Highway-in-the-Sky, SVS) reduce pilot workload for trajectory following.

---

### 1.4 Auditory Displays

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition:**
> An **Auditory Display** is a man-machine interface in which information is conveyed to the human operator through the *acoustic channel* — via sound pressure waves that stimulate the cochlea and are interpreted by the central nervous system.

**Intuition:** When your phone buzzes, the visual channel is busy (you are looking at a textbook). The phone instead *speaks* a short auditory message — "Message from John." The sound reaches you even when you cannot see the screen. This is an auditory display converting a *textual* variable (sender identity) into a *temporal acoustic pattern* (speech waveform).

---

### 1.5 The Process of Hearing (Anatomy & Physiology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hearing** is the transduction of *acoustic pressure fluctuations* in air (or other media) into *neural action potentials* that the brain interprets as pitch, loudness, and timbre.

**Anatomical chain** (outer → inner ear):

| Stage | Structure | Function |
|---|---|---|
| 1. Collection | **Pinna (Auricle)** | Collects sound; provides spectral cues for *localization* (front/back) |
| 2. Conduction | **Ear Canal (Meatus)** | Channels sound; resonance peaks at **~2.7 kHz** |
| 3. Impedance matching | **Tympanic Membrane + Ossicles (Malleus, Incus, Stapes)** | Convert air-borne pressure to fluid-borne pressure in cochlea; gain ≈ **22:1 (≈ 27 dB)** |
| 4. Transduction | **Cochlea (Organ of Corti, Hair Cells)** | Convert basilar-membrane motion into neural spikes via inner/outer hair cells |
| 5. Encoding | **Auditory Nerve (CN VIII) → Cochlear Nucleus → Superior Olivary Complex → Inferior Colliculus → Medial Geniculate → Auditory Cortex** | Frequency, intensity, and spatial encoding |

**Key psychoacoustic parameters for designers:**

- **Frequency range of human hearing:** **20 Hz – 20,000 Hz** (degrades with age — *presbycusis*).
- **Most sensitive band:** **1 kHz – 4 kHz** (matches human voice and danger signals).
- **Threshold of hearing (TOH):** $I_0 = 10^{-12} \, \text{W/m}^2$ (at 1 kHz reference).
- **Threshold of pain:** $I_{pain} \approx 10^0 \, \text{W/m}^2$ (12 orders of magnitude above threshold).

---

### 1.6 Principles of Auditory Displays

> [!NOTE]
> Auditory displays are governed by the **Absolute Identification Limit** (Miller, 1956) and the **Trade-off between Absolute & Relative Identification** (Poulton, 1956). The core principles are:

1. **Absolute Identification Limit:** A human can *unambiguously identify* a maximum of **~5 to 7 distinct auditory signals** (channels) at a glance (Miller's Magical Number). Beyond 7, errors explode.
2. **Relative Identification (Transduction):** An almost *unlimited* number of signals can be interpreted if the operator can *compare* each new signal to a *reference* (e.g., tuning a radio by ear).
3. **Matching the Display to the Auditory Dimension:** Use the auditory dimension that *best matches* the variable:
   - *Loudness* → urgency/distance/proximity
   - *Pitch* → rate/frequency/elevation
   - *Rhythm/Tempo* → speed/heart-rate
   - *Timbre* → category/source identification
   - *Spatial location* → spatial awareness
4. **Intelligibility vs. Annoyance Trade-off:** Louder ≠ better. The signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) should be **≥ 10–15 dB** above ambient for reliable detection.
5. **Earcons & Auditory Icons:** Structured non-verbal tones (earcons) and metaphorical everyday sounds (auditory icons, e.g., paper-shuffling for email) leverage memory primitives.
6. **No-Go for Silent Channels:** Auditory displays are excellent for *intermittent, alerting, or omnidirectional* information; poor for *continuous, quantitative, high-resolution* data (use a visual display instead).

> [!TIP]
> **A simple rule of thumb for KTU exams:** Use an *auditory* display when the operator's eyes are busy, the information is *alerting* or *spatial*, or the number of distinct signals is small. Use a *visual* display when information is *quantitative, continuous*, or requires more than ~7 distinct channels.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of a Bank Angle Display — The Attitude Indicator (AI)

The **Attitude Indicator** is the quintessential *world-centered, pictorial, integrated* display. It is a gyroscopic instrument whose miniature horizon bar is driven directly by the real aircraft's pitch and roll.

| Component | What it shows | Reading convention |
|---|---|---|
| Miniature aircraft symbol (fixed in display frame) | Ownship | Always fixed at center |
| Horizon bar (rotates & translates) | Real horizon line relative to ownship | Roll: rotation; Pitch: vertical offset |
| Bank index (at top, fixed) | Reference for roll angle | Tick marks every 10°, 20°, 30°, 45°, 60° |
| Pitch ladder | Pitch in 5° or 10° increments | Lines above (climb) / below (descend) horizon |

**Why the aircraft symbol stays fixed and the horizon moves** (Moving-Part Principle):
If the display mimicked the *pilot's view from the cockpit*, the horizon would be fixed and the aircraft symbol would rotate. But the *real-world frame of reference* (the outside world) is more useful for *spatial orientation* — the pilot sees "the world rotating around me" because that is what his vestibular system tells him. Therefore, in the AI, **the world (horizon bar) moves and the aircraft (symbol) stays still** — a *world-centered*, *outside-in* convention. This is the *Wickens Compatible Display* principle.

---

## 2.2 The Turn Coordinator (TC) — Rate vs. Position

The **Turn Coordinator** is a *rate* instrument — it shows the *rate of bank* and *coordination of the turn* (slip/skid). It complements the AI (which shows *position*).

- The miniature aircraft rolls/tilts left or right proportional to **turn rate**.
- The **slip ball** (in the inclinometer below) shows *coordination*.
- **Standard rate turn:** 3° per second = a 360° turn in **2 minutes** (a 180° turn in 1 minute).

**Standard-Rate formula:**

$$
\omega_{std} = \frac{360°}{120 \, \text{s}} = 3 \, \text{°/s}
$$

A 360° standard-rate turn takes exactly **2 minutes**. A 180° turn takes **1 minute**. This is heavily tested in KTU/Pilot exams.

---

## 2.3 The Six Principles of Aircraft Position Displays — Detailed Treatment

### P1 — Moving-Part Principle
The element that *moves* in the real world should move in the display. (e.g., The horizon — which is what *the world* does to a pilot — moves in the AI.)

### P2 — Pictorial Realism
The display geometry should resemble the geometry of the task. (e.g., A moving-map display for navigation literally shows a map.)

### P3 — Functional Whole
Combine variables used together onto one display. (e.g., The HSI combines heading, course, OBS, glideslope, and bearing in *one* instrument.)

### P4 — Preattentive Processing
Critical alerts should be perceptually "pop-out" (< 250 ms). Use color, large size, high contrast, motion. (e.g., Red 35°-bank-warning stripe.)

### P5 — Display- vs. World-Centered Reference Frame
| Reference Frame | Best for | Pilot Bias |
|---|---|---|
| **Display-Centered** (inside-out) | Precision flight control | Better for *trajectory* following |
| **World-Centered** (outside-in) | Spatial awareness | Better for *orientation* |

### P6 — Stereopsis / Perspective Compatibility
3-D perspective displays (SVS — Synthetic Vision System) reduce workload because the geometry is *isomorphic* with the pilot's egocentric frame.

---

## 2.4 The Process of Hearing — Deeper Mechanics

### 2.4.1 Sound Pressure & Intensity

A sound wave in air is a periodic variation in **pressure**, $p(t)$, around ambient atmospheric pressure, $P_0 \approx 101.3 \, \text{kPa}$.

The **root-mean-square (RMS) sound pressure**, $p_{rms}$, is the physically relevant quantity (not the peak).

**Sound Intensity** (power per unit area) is related to pressure by:

$$
I = \frac{p_{rms}^2}{\rho \, c}
$$

where $\rho$ is the air density (≈ **1.21 kg/m³** at 20 °C, sea level) and $c$ is the speed of sound in air (≈ **343 m/s**).

The **characteristic acoustic impedance of air** is therefore:

$$
Z_0 = \rho \, c \approx 1.21 \times 343 \approx 415 \, \text{Pa·s/m}
$$

(also called **Rayl**).

### 2.4.2 The Decibel Scale

Because the human ear responds *logarithmically*, sound is measured in **decibels (dB)**:

$$
L_p = 20 \, \log_{10} \!\left( \frac{p_{rms}}{p_{ref}} \right) \quad \text{[dB SPL]}
$$

where the reference pressure is $p_{ref} = 20 \, \mu\text{Pa} = 2 \times 10^{-5} \, \text{Pa}$ (the standard threshold of hearing at 1 kHz).

Equivalently, in **intensity units**:

$$
L_I = 10 \, \log_{10} \!\left( \frac{I}{I_{ref}} \right) \quad \text{[dB]}
$$

with $I_{ref} = 10^{-12} \, \text{W/m}^2$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **A 10 dB increase ≈ a perceived doubling of loudness** (Stevens' Power Law, exponent ≈ 0.6 for loudness). A 20 dB increase ≈ a 100× increase in sound *intensity* but only ≈ 2× increase in perceived loudness.

### 2.4.3 Equal-Loudness Contours (Fletcher-Munson)

Human hearing is **not equally sensitive** at all frequencies. The **Fletcher-Munson curves** (ISO 226:2003) show the SPL required to perceive a pure tone as equally loud at different frequencies.

| Frequency | Approx. SPL for 40-phon loudness |
|---|---|
| 100 Hz | ≈ 50 dB |
| 1 kHz | 40 dB (reference) |
| 4 kHz | ≈ 35 dB (most sensitive region) |
| 10 kHz | ≈ 50 dB |

**Design implication:** Auditory warning signals (alarms, earcons) should place their dominant energy in the **1–4 kHz** band where the ear is *most sensitive* and where ambient noise is *lower*.

### 2.4.4 Localization Cues — How We Know Where Sound Comes From

| Cue | Mechanism | Effective for |
|---|---|---|
| **ITD** (Interaural Time Difference) | Difference in arrival time between ears | Low frequencies < 1500 Hz (wavelength > head size) |
| **ILD** (Interaural Level Difference) | Head-shadow attenuation of far ear | High frequencies > 1500 Hz |
| **Pinna spectral notches** | Pinna filtering creates direction-specific comb-filtering pattern | Front/back & up/down discrimination |
| **Head motion** | Dynamic changes in ITD/ILD | Front/back disambiguation |

**Maximum ITD** across the head (≈ 18 cm path difference) gives ≈ **630 μs** delay. This is the **Precedence Effect / Haas Effect** window — sounds arriving within ~30 ms are fused.

---

## 2.5 Principles of Auditory Displays — Quantitative Limits

### 2.5.1 Miller's Law for Auditory Channels

The number of distinct, absolutely identifiable auditory channels is **n ≈ 5 ± 2** (Miller, 1956, *The Magical Number Seven, Plus or Minus Two*). For pure tones in noise, this drops to **≈ 3–4 distinct pitches**.

### 2.5.2 Channel Capacity & Bandwidth

Auditory *information throughput* is bounded by the **channel capacity** theorem. For speech intelligibility, the **articulation index (AI)** and **Speech Intelligibility Index (SII, ANSI S3.5-1997)** quantify the proportion of speech cues that survive the channel.

For a band of bandwidth $B$ Hz and SNR $\rho$:

$$
C = B \, \log_2 \!\left(1 + \rho\right) \quad \text{[bits/s]}
$$

### 2.5.3 Recommended Auditory Dimension Mapping

| Variable to display | Auditory dimension | Why |
|---|---|---|
| Urgency / Proximity | Loudness | Louder = more urgent (Zwicker) |
| Speed / Rate | Pitch | Higher pitch = faster |
| Categorical Source | Timbre | Identifies "who" or "what" |
| Spatial Position | Spatial location (3-D audio) | Direct mapping |
| Continuous progress | Tempo / Rhythm | Easy to track |
| Discrete alert | Duration / Number of beeps | E.g., 1 beep = caution; 2 = warning |

---

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Markdown Table)

| # | Concept | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Sound pressure level | $L_p = 20 \log_{10}(p_{rms}/p_{ref})$ | dB SPL; $p_{ref} = 20 \, \mu$Pa |
| 2 | Sound intensity level | $L_I = 10 \log_{10}(I/I_{ref})$ | dB; $I_{ref} = 10^{-12} \, \text{W/m}^2$ |
| 3 | Intensity ↔ Pressure | $I = p_{rms}^2 / (\rho c)$ | W/m²; $\rho c \approx 415$ Rayl |
| 4 | Channel capacity (Shannon) | $C = B \log_2(1+\rho)$ | bits/s |
| 5 | Standard-rate turn | $\omega_{std} = 3 \, \text{°/s}$ | 360° in 2 min |
| 6 | Bank angle for standard rate | $\tan \phi \approx \frac{V^2}{g \, R}$ | rad; $R = V/\omega$ |
| 7 | Steady-coordinated turn radius | $R = V^2 / (g \tan \phi)$ | meters |
| 8 | Loudness (Stevens) | $L = k \, I^{0.6}$ | Sone scale; doubling $\approx$ +10 dB |
| 9 | Pitch (Mel scale) | $M = 2595 \log_{10}(1+f/700)$ | Mels |
| 10 | Critical band (Fletcher) | $\Delta f \approx 1.6 + 4 \, f_c^2$ | Hz (Hz² up to ~500 Hz, then linear ~100 Hz) |
| 11 | Hearing threshold | $I_0 = 10^{-12} \, \text{W/m}^2$ | at 1 kHz |
| 12 | Pain threshold | $I_{pain} \approx 1 \, \text{W/m}^2$ |  |
| 13 | Hearing range | 20 Hz – 20 kHz | degrades with age |
| 14 | Pinna resonance | 2.5 – 4 kHz | aids localization |
| 15 | Speed of sound in air | $c \approx 343 \, \text{m/s}$ | at 20 °C |
| 16 | Characteristic impedance of air | $Z_0 = \rho c \approx 415 \, \text{Rayl}$ | Pa·s/m |
| 17 | Miller's limit | 5 ± 2 auditory channels | absolute ID |
| 18 | Recommended alarm SNR | ≥ 10–15 dB above ambient |  |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** The decibel scale is the universal language of acoustics — used in aircraft noise certification (ICAO Annex 16), hearing conservation (OSHA 90 dBA 8-hr limit), audio engineering, sonar, and ultrasound imaging. The bank-angle formulas are used in flight-management system (FMS) design, air-traffic-control trajectory prediction, and turn-and-bank indicator calibration.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Sound Pressure Level (SPL) from RMS Pressure

**Step 1 — Define the acoustic signal.**
A pure tone is $p(t) = P \, \sin(2\pi f t)$. The *peak* pressure is $P$, but the *RMS* pressure is:

$$
p_{rms} = \sqrt{\langle p^2(t) \rangle_T} = \frac{P}{\sqrt{2}}
$$

**Step 2 — Form the squared ratio.**
The human ear responds to *power-like* quantities (intensity, which is proportional to $p_{rms}^2$). Define the ratio:

$$
\frac{p_{rms}^2}{p_{ref}^2} = \frac{I}{I_{ref}}
$$

**Step 3 — Take the base-10 logarithm.**
A logarithmic scale compresses 12 orders of magnitude of human hearing into a 0–120 dB range:

$$
\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{I}{I_{ref}}\right) = \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{p_{rms}^2}{p_{ref}^2}\right) = 2 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{p_{rms}}{p_{ref}}\right)
$$

**Step 4 — Multiply by 10 to obtain the *bel*, then by 10 again for the *decibel*.**

$$
L_I = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{I}{I_{ref}}\right) = 10 \times 2 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{p_{rms}}{p_{ref}}\right) = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{p_{rms}}{p_{ref}}\right)
$$

This produces the celebrated equation:

$$
\boxed{L_p = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{p_{rms}}{p_{ref}}\right) \text{ dB SPL}}
$$

**Why a factor of 20 for pressure but 10 for intensity?**
Because $I \propto p^2$, when we go from intensity-ratio to pressure-ratio we pull a *factor of 2* out of the log: $\log_{10}(x^2) = 2 \log_{10}(x)$. So the multiplier *doubles* from 10 to 20.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Bank Angle for a Steady Coordinated Turn

**Step 1 — Set up the free-body diagram.**
In a *steady, coordinated, level turn*, the aircraft moves in a horizontal circle of radius $R$ at constant true airspeed $V$. The required centripetal acceleration is:

$$
a_c = \frac{V^2}{R}
$$

**Step 2 — Provide the centripetal force with the lift vector.**
In a banked turn, the lift vector $\vec{L}$ tilts inward by the bank angle $\phi$. The horizontal component of lift provides centripetal force; the vertical component supports weight:

$$
L \sin \phi = m \frac{V^2}{R}
$$
$$
L \cos \phi = m g
$$

**Step 3 — Divide the equations to eliminate $L$ and $m$.**

$$
\tan \phi = \frac{V^2}{g \, R}
$$

**Step 4 — Express the radius in terms of turn rate.**
For a *standard-rate turn*, $\omega = 3 \, \text{°/s} = \pi/60 \, \text{rad/s}$ and $V = \omega R$ ⟹ $R = V/\omega$.

**Step 5 — Substitute.**

$$
\tan \phi = \frac{V^2 \, \omega}{g \, V} = \frac{V \, \omega}{g}
$$

**Step 6 — Numerical example (KTU-style).**
Aircraft flies at $V = 150 \, \text{knots} = 77.17 \, \text{m/s}$, $\omega = 3 \, \text{°/s} = 0.0524 \, \text{rad/s}$, $g = 9.81 \, \text{m/s}^2$:

$$
\tan \phi = \frac{77.17 \times 0.0524}{9.81} = \frac{4.044}{9.81} \approx 0.4122
$$

$$
\phi = \arctan(0.4122) \approx 22.4°
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Rule of thumb:** For a *standard-rate (3°/s) turn*, bank angle ≈ *airspeed in knots / 10 + 7* — for 150 kts: 15 + 7 = 22°. Matches our derivation. KTU examiners love this shortcut.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Coordination of an Auditory Display (Channel Capacity)

**Step 1 — Define SNR.**
A pilot/engineer monitors a noisy signal. Let the signal power be $S$ and noise power be $N$. The signal-to-noise ratio is:

$$
\rho = \frac{S}{N}
$$

**Step 2 — Bandwidth limit.**
The auditory system can *resolve* spectral components down to the *critical band* of the cochlea, which has effective bandwidth $B$ (Hz).

**Step 3 — Apply Shannon-Hartley theorem.**

$$
C = B \, \log_2(1 + \rho) \quad \text{[bits/s]}
$$

**Step 4 — Numerical example.**
Aircraft cockpit voice channel: $B = 3400 \, \text{Hz}$, $\rho = 30$ (linear, i.e., 14.77 dB):

$$
C = 3400 \times \log_2(31) = 3400 \times 4.954 = 16{,}844 \, \text{bits/s} \approx 16.8 \, \text{kbps}
$$

This is exactly the bitrate of GSM cellular telephony (13 kbps) — which is why ATC radios are intelligible but not hi-fi.

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Minimum Audible Field & dB SPL Conversion

**Step 1 — Threshold of hearing.**
The faintest sound a young, healthy ear can detect at 1 kHz is $p_{ref} = 20 \, \mu$Pa (RMS). This is the **0 dB SPL** reference.

**Step 2 — Conversions to/from linear pressure.**

$$
p_{rms} = p_{ref} \times 10^{L_p/20}
$$

| $L_p$ (dB SPL) | $p_{rms}$ | Example |
|---|---|---|
| 0 | 20 μPa | Threshold of hearing |
| 20 | 200 μPa | Whisper at 1 m |
| 60 | 20 mPa | Normal conversation |
| 94 | 1 Pa | 1 Pascal reference (used in audio) |
| 120 | 20 Pa | Threshold of discomfort |
| 140 | 200 Pa | Threshold of pain |

**Step 3 — Energy check.**
At 140 dB SPL, $p_{rms} = 200 \, \text{Pa}$. Acoustic intensity:

$$
I = \frac{p_{rms}^2}{\rho c} = \frac{200^2}{415} = \frac{40{,}000}{415} \approx 96.4 \, \text{W/m}^2
$$

This is roughly the acoustic intensity of a jet-engine at close range — it is genuinely dangerous.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Display Selection Decision Tree

| Situation | Recommended Display | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| Pilot flying in clouds (IMC) — needs orientation | **Pictorial world-centered AI** | Vestibular compatibility, immediate |
| Pilot intercepting a precise radial | **HSI / CDI** | Quantitative + pictorial |
| Operator driving a car | **Auditory (speech) + Visual speedometer** | Multichannel redundancy |
| Cardiac monitor in ICU | **Auditory alarm (varying pitch/tempo)** | Eye-busy, alerting |
| Industrial control room — many parameters | **Visual SCADA + auditory alarms** | > 7 channels — auditory is for alerts only |
| 3-D navigation (Heli/SAR) | **SVS / Highway-in-the-Sky** | Perspective compatibility |
| Pilot must detect bank > 35° *instantly* | **Red stripe on AI** | Preattentive pop-out |
| Ambulatory firefighter (no visual channel free) | **Directional 3-D audio** | Spatial auditory localization |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 14-Mark Question Pattern:** Examiners often present a *scenario* (e.g., "an air traffic controller must identify 9 aircraft by audio only") and ask: *design an auditory display for this task and justify the dimension used.* The answer should cite Miller's 5 ± 2 limit and propose *hierarchical coding* (timbre for category, pitch for sub-category, earcon rhythm for ID).

---

## 3.6 Worked Example: Convert a Jet-Takeoff Noise Level to Linear Intensity

**Given:** $L_p = 130 \, \text{dB SPL}$ at 100 m from a military jet on takeoff.

**Find:** The corresponding $p_{rms}$ and $I$.

**Step 1 — Solve for $p_{rms}$.**

$$
L_p = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{p_{rms}}{p_{ref}}\right)
$$

$$
130 = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{p_{rms}}{20 \times 10^{-6}}\right)
$$

$$
\frac{130}{20} = 6.5 = \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{p_{rms}}{20 \times 10^{-6}}\right)
$$

$$
10^{6.5} = 3.162 \times 10^{6}
$$

$$
p_{rms} = 20 \times 10^{-6} \times 3.162 \times 10^{6} = 63.25 \, \text{Pa}
$$

**Step 2 — Solve for $I$.**

$$
I = \frac{p_{rms}^2}{\rho c} = \frac{63.25^2}{415} = \frac{4000.6}{415} \approx 9.64 \, \text{W/m}^2
$$

**Step 3 — Verify with the intensity-level formula.**

$$
L_I = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{9.64}{10^{-12}}\right) = 10 \log_{10}(9.64 \times 10^{12}) = 10 \times 12.984 = 129.84 \approx 130 \, \text{dB} \quad \checkmark
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The pressure and intensity decibel scales give the **same numerical value** (130 dB SPL = 130 dB IL) only when both reference values are used. They are not numerically equal in general — but for SPL we use the 20-multiplier and for SIL we use the 10-multiplier, and *both yield 130 dB* because the conversion is self-consistent.

---

## 3.7 Analytical Matrix — Bank Angle Limits (Aircraft Categories)

| Aircraft Category | Typical Bank Limit | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| Commercial airliner (FAR 25) | 30° in cruise, 35° in approach | Passenger comfort, structural limit |
| General Aviation (FAR 23) | 60° max in normal category | Aerobatic reserve |
| Military Fighter | 90°+ (knife-edge) | High-G, high-roll structural margin |
| Light helicopter | 30–45° | Stability augmentation limit |
| Aerobatic (FAR 23 acro) | 360°/s roll rate | Certified for full aerobatics |

**Display design rule:** The **bank index** on the AI should *always* highlight the 30° and 45° tick marks in a different color (typically white → green at 30°, green → amber at 45°), giving the pilot preattentive cues to the most common safety limits.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Aircraft Bank Angle Display System (Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Airframe Sensors: Gyros and Accelerometers] --> B[Signal Conditioner]
    B --> C[Attitude Indicator AI]
    B --> D[Turn Coordinator TC]
    B --> E[Heading Indicator HI]
    C --> F[Pilot Visual Channel]
    D --> F
    E --> F
    F --> G[Pilot Perception and Decision]
    G --> H[Control Inputs: Aileron, Elevator, Rudder]
    H --> I[Aircraft Motion Response]
    I --> A
    F -.Optional Auditory Channel.-> J[Voice Warning System GPWS TCAS]
    J --> G
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Read this as a closed-loop control system**: the pilot *observes* the AI/TC/HI (visual channel), *decides*, and *commands* a control input that *changes* the aircraft state — which is *re-sensed* by the gyros. The **GPWS / TCAS** is an *auditory* overlay that injects a third modality (sound) for time-critical alerts.

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Functional Architecture of the Human Auditory Display Chain

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Src[Source: System Variable] --> Enc[Encoder: Maps Variable to Acoustic Dimension]
    Enc --> Amp[Amplifier and SNR Conditioning]
    Amp --> Spk[Transducer: Speaker or Earphone]
    Spk --> Air[Acoustic Channel: Air Pressure Wave]
    Air --> Pinna[Pinna: Collects and Filters]
    Pinna --> Canal[Ear Canal: Conduction with Resonance]
    Canal --> TM[Tympanic Membrane: Vibration]
    TM --> Oss[Ossicles: Mechanical Impedance Matching]
    Oss --> Coch[Cochlea: Frequency Analysis on Basilar Membrane]
    Coch --> Nerve[Auditory Nerve: Neural Transduction]
    Nerve --> Brain[Auditory Cortex: Perception and Identification]
    Brain --> Resp[Operator Response]
    Resp --> Src
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Every block in this chain is a potential *failure mode*.** A defective cochlea (presbycusis), blocked canal (cerumen), stiff ossicles (otosclerosis), or noisy environment (low SNR) can break the link. Display designers must *guarantee* SNR ≥ 10–15 dB to overcome ambient cockpit/plant noise.

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Six Principles of Aircraft Position Displays (Cluster)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Six Principles of Aircraft Position Displays] --> P1[P1: Moving Part]
    P --> P2[P2: Pictorial Realism]
    P --> P3[P3: Functional Whole]
    P --> P4[P4: Preattentive Processing]
    P --> P5[P5: Reference Frame]
    P --> P6[P6: Stereopsis and Perspective]
    P5 --> P5a[Display Centered: Best for Precision]
    P5 --> P5b[World Centered: Best for Orientation]
    P1 --> Ex1[Example: AI Horizon Moves Not Aircraft Symbol]
    P2 --> Ex2[Example: HSI Looks Like a Map]
    P3 --> Ex3[Example: HSI Combines Heading Course and Glideslope]
    P4 --> Ex4[Example: Red 35 deg Bank Warning Stripe]
    P6 --> Ex6[Example: Highway in the Sky SVS]
```

---

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram — Ear Anatomy & Hearing Process

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    OuterEar[Outer Ear: Pinna and Ear Canal] --> MidEar[Middle Ear: Tympanic Membrane and Ossicles]
    MidEar --> InnerEar[Inner Ear: Cochlea and Organ of Corti]
    InnerEar --> CN8[Auditory Nerve CN VIII]
    CN8 --> CN[Cochlear Nucleus]
    CN --> SOC[Superior Olivary Complex: ITD and ILD Localization]
    SOC --> IC[Inferior Colliculus]
    IC --> MGN[Medial Geniculate Nucleus of Thalamus]
    MGN --> AC[Primary Auditory Cortex]
    AC --> Cog[Cognitive Processing: Identification Comprehension and Action]
```

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic for the auditory brainstem path:** "**C**ochlear → **S**OC → **I**C → **M**GN → **A**uditory cortex" = **CSIMA**. Frequently asked in KTU viva.

---

## 4.5 Mermaid Diagram — Auditory Display Dimension Mapping (Earcon Design)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Var[Variable to Display] --> Q1{Quantitative or Categorical?}
    Q1 -->|Quantitative Continuous| D1[Use Pitch or Loudness for Monotonic Mapping]
    Q1 -->|Categorical| D2[Use Timbre or Rhythm for Identification]
    Q1 -->|Spatial| D3[Use 3D Audio: ITD ILD Pinna Cues]
    D1 --> D1a[Pitch: Rate or Speed]
    D1 --> D1b[Loudness: Urgency or Proximity]
    D2 --> D2a[Timbre: Source Identity]
    D2 --> D2b[Rhythm: Status or Count]
    D3 --> D3a[ITD: Low Frequency Localization]
    D3 --> D3b[ILD: High Frequency Localization]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering takeaway:** Always match the *psychophysical dimension* of the auditory system to the *semantic* of the variable. Loudness for urgency, pitch for speed, timbre for source, location for direction.

---

## 4.6 Mermaid Diagram — Decibel Reference Levels (Lifestyle)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    DB0[0 dB SPL: Threshold of Hearing] --> DB10[10 dB: Rustling Leaves]
    DB10 --> DB20[20 dB: Whisper at 1 m]
    DB20 --> DB40[40 dB: Quiet Library]
    DB40 --> DB60[60 dB: Normal Conversation]
    DB60 --> DB80[80 dB: Busy Traffic]
    DB80 --> DB90[90 dB: OSHA 8 hr Limit]
    DB90 --> DB100[100 dB: Subway Train]
    DB100 --> DB120[120 dB: Threshold of Discomfort]
    DB120 --> DB140[140 dB: Threshold of Pain Jet Engine]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Confusing "display-centered" vs. "world-centered":** Students frequently swap the two. **Mnemonic: "D**isplay = **D**river inside the cockpit looking *out* → aircraft moves, world fixed. **W**orld = **W**orld outside moves because pilot is *tilted*." The **AI is world-centered** — the *aircraft symbol stays still* and the *horizon moves*.
> 2. **Forgetting the factor of 20 vs. 10 in dB:** SPL uses 20, SIL uses 10. Half the marks in numerical questions are lost here.
> 3. **Forgetting the reference values:** $p_{ref} = 20 \, \mu$Pa and $I_{ref} = 10^{-12} \, \text{W/m}^2$. Always state them.
> 4. **Pitch vs. Loudness mapping:** Students sometimes say "use loudness for speed" — it should be *pitch*.
> 5. **Forgetting the 5 ± 2 limit:** Auditory displays are *not* good for > 7 channels.
> 6. **Not justifying the design choice:** In 14-mark questions, simply stating "use an auditory display" loses 2–3 marks. You must justify using *at least* Miller's law, SNR, and a dimension-mapping table.

---

## 📝 PART A — 3-Mark Questions (Short Answer)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
**Define a representational display with a suitable aviation example.**

**Model Answer:**
A *representational display* is a visual interface in which the *form* of the display element is geometrically or pictorially analogous to the *form* of the controlled variable, allowing the operator to read the system state by direct perceptual mapping rather than by symbolic decoding. (1.5 marks)
**Aviation example:** The **Attitude Indicator (AI)** — its miniature aircraft symbol and horizon bar are *pictorial* analogues of the real aircraft and the real horizon, so the pilot can read pitch and bank by direct visual analogy without consciously translating symbolic codes. (1.5 marks)

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**State Miller's Law for auditory absolute identification and its design implication.**

**Model Answer:**
**Statement:** Miller (1956) found that the human operator can *unambiguously identify* a maximum of approximately **5 ± 2** distinct auditory signals (channels) presented in rapid succession without external reference. (2 marks)
**Design implication:** Auditory displays should be limited to ≤ 7 distinct channels for absolute identification; for more signals, use *relative* identification (a reference signal) or supplement with a *visual* display. (1 mark)

---

## 📝 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Choice 1**

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Module 2 Internal Choice A]**

> **(a)** Explain the **six principles of aircraft position displays** with one aviation example for each principle. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** Describe the **anatomy and physiology of hearing** from pinna to auditory cortex, highlighting the role of the basilar membrane in frequency analysis. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution — Part (a)**

| # | Principle | Definition | Aviation Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Moving-Part** | The element that moves in the real world should move in the display. | AI: the *horizon bar* rotates, not the aircraft symbol. |
| 2 | **Pictorial Realism** | The display geometry should resemble the task geometry. | HSI: looks like a *horizontal navigation chart*. |
| 3 | **Functional Whole** | Combine related variables on one instrument. | HSI: heading, course, bearing, glideslope on one dial. |
| 4 | **Preattentive Processing** | Critical info must "pop out" in < 250 ms. | Red stripe at > 35° bank on the AI. |
| 5 | **Reference Frame** | *Display-centered* = inside-out (precision); *World-centered* = outside-in (orientation). | AI is *world-centered* — world moves, aircraft symbol fixed. |
| 6 | **Stereopsis / Perspective** | 3-D perspective is *isomorphic* with egocentric frame. | Synthetic Vision System (SVS) / Highway-in-the-Sky. |

*(1 mark per principle + 0.5 mark per example = 6.5 marks; 0.5 mark for tabular summary.)*

#### **Model Solution — Part (b)**

The hearing pathway is anatomically and functionally divided into **outer, middle, and inner ear** plus the **central auditory nervous system**.

1. **Outer Ear (Pinna + Ear Canal):** The pinna collects sound and provides spectral cues for *front-back* and *elevation* localization. The ear canal amplifies frequencies near **2.7 kHz** by resonance, increasing sensitivity in the speech band. *(1.5 marks)*

2. **Middle Ear (Tympanic Membrane + Ossicles):** Acoustic pressure in air is transmitted to the *fluid-filled* cochlea. Because the cochlear fluid is much denser than air, an *impedance mismatch* would cause ~30 dB of reflection loss. The **ossicles (malleus, incus, stapes)** act as a mechanical transformer, providing a **22:1 area ratio + 1.3:1 lever advantage ≈ 27 dB gain** to match impedances. *(1.5 marks)*

3. **Inner Ear (Cochlea + Organ of Corti):** The cochlea is a fluid-filled, snail-shaped tube with the **basilar membrane (BM)** running its length. The BM is *tonotopic* — its **base** is narrow and stiff (resonant at high frequencies, ~20 kHz) while its **apex** is wide and floppy (resonant at low frequencies, ~20 Hz). A pure tone of frequency $f$ produces a *traveling wave* that peaks at the position on the BM tuned to $f$. This is **Békésy's place theory of pitch perception**. *(2.5 marks)*

4. **Central Auditory Pathway:** Hair cells at the BM peak transduce mechanical motion into neural spikes via CN VIII. The signal is processed sequentially by the **cochlear nucleus → superior olivary complex (first site of binaural ITD/ILD comparison) → inferior colliculus → medial geniculate nucleus → auditory cortex**. *(1.5 marks)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Choice 2**

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 2 Internal Choice B]**

> **(a)** Derive the **relationship between bank angle, true airspeed, and turn rate** for a steady coordinated turn. A small aircraft cruises at **150 knots** in a **standard-rate turn**. Compute the bank angle. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** A cockpit voice warning at **94 dB SPL** is played in an ambient cockpit noise of **78 dB SPL**. Compute the **SNR**, the **linear pressure ratio**, and discuss whether this is *adequate* for a safety-critical alert per Human-Factors standards. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution — Part (a) — Derivation**

**Step 1 — Centripetal Force Equation.** *(1 mark)*
In a steady, coordinated, level turn the aircraft follows a horizontal circular path. The centripetal acceleration is:

$$
a_c = \frac{V^2}{R}
$$

The horizontal component of *lift* supplies this:

$$
L \sin \phi = m \frac{V^2}{R}
$$

**Step 2 — Vertical Equilibrium.** *(1 mark)*
The vertical component of lift supports weight:

$$
L \cos \phi = m g
$$

**Step 3 — Divide the Equations.** *(1 mark)*

$$
\tan \phi = \frac{V^2}{g \, R}
$$

**Step 4 — Relate $R$ to $\omega$ and $V$.** *(1 mark)*

$$
R = \frac{V}{\omega} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \tan \phi = \frac{V \, \omega}{g}
$$

**Step 5 — Numerical Computation.** *(2.5 marks)*

- $V = 150 \, \text{knots} = 150 \times 0.5144 = 77.17 \, \text{m/s}$
- $\omega = 3 \, \text{°/s} = 3 \times \pi / 180 = 0.0524 \, \text{rad/s}$
- $g = 9.81 \, \text{m/s}^2$

$$
\tan \phi = \frac{77.17 \times 0.0524}{9.81} = \frac{4.044}{9.81} \approx 0.4122
$$

$$
\phi = \arctan(0.4122) \approx 22.4° \approx 22°
$$

**Step 6 — Engineering Interpretation.** *(0.5 mark)*
This 22° bank angle falls well within the airline standard 30° cruise limit, confirming the calculation is operationally reasonable.

#### **Model Solution — Part (b) — SNR & Adequacy**

**Step 1 — Compute SNR in dB.** *(1 mark)*

$$
\text{SNR (dB)} = L_{signal} - L_{noise} = 94 - 78 = 16 \, \text{dB}
$$

**Step 2 — Convert to Linear Pressure Ratio.** *(2 marks)*

$$
\text{SNR}_{linear} = 10^{\text{SNR (dB)} / 20} = 10^{16/20} = 10^{0.8} \approx 6.31
$$

So $p_{signal} \approx 6.31 \times p_{noise}$ (RMS). Equivalently:

$$
\frac{p_{rms, signal}}{p_{rms, noise}} = 10^{16/20} \approx 6.31
$$

**Step 3 — Compute RMS Pressures.** *(1.5 marks)*

$$
p_{rms, signal} = 20 \, \mu\text{Pa} \times 10^{94/20} = 20 \times 10^{-6} \times 10^{4.7} = 20 \times 10^{-6} \times 50{,}119 = 1.002 \, \text{Pa}
$$

$$
p_{rms, noise} = 20 \, \mu\text{Pa} \times 10^{78/20} = 20 \times 10^{-6} \times 10^{3.9} = 20 \times 10^{-6} \times 7943 = 0.1589 \, \text{Pa}
$$

Ratio = $1.002 / 0.1589 \approx 6.31$ ✓

**Step 4 — Adequacy Discussion.** *(2.5 marks)*

Per ICAO Doc 9683 and standard Human-Factors practice (Wickens, McCarley), a safety-critical auditory warning should be presented at **SNR ≥ 10–15 dB above ambient** to ensure detectability in a multi-task cockpit. Our computed SNR is **16 dB**, which **meets the lower bound** but is *marginal* for a *safety-critical* alert.

| Criterion | Threshold | Our Value | Verdict |
|---|---|---|---|
| SNR for reliable detection | ≥ 10 dB | 16 dB | ✓ |
| SNR for safety-critical alert | ≥ 15 dB (preferably 20 dB) | 16 dB | ✓ Marginal |
| Frequency content in 1–4 kHz band? | Required | (Assumed) | ✓ if tonal |
| Distinct from other cockpit aural alerts? | Required | (Assumed) | ✓ |

**Recommendation:** The 94 dB SPL level is *just* adequate. For a stall warning or terrain alert, designers should aim for **≥ 100 dB SPL** at the ear (≈ 20 dB SNR) to ensure unmaskability even under worst-case workload and hearing-protection conditions.

---

## 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-yield bullets — read these in the last 15 minutes before the KTU exam.**

### Representational Displays
- ✅ **Definition:** Display where form mimics function (one-to-one mapping).
- ✅ **Aircraft examples:** AI, HSI, RMI, Moving Map.
- ✅ **Display-centered** = *inside-out*; **World-centered** = *outside-in*.
- ✅ The **AI is world-centered**: the aircraft symbol is fixed; the horizon moves.

### Aircraft Bank Angle Displays
- ✅ **Bank angle** = roll about longitudinal axis; measured 0–90°.
- ✅ **AI** = position indicator (pitch + bank).
- ✅ **TC** = rate indicator (turn rate + coordination).
- ✅ **Standard-rate turn** = 3°/s, 360° in 2 min.
- ✅ **Bank index ticks** at 10°, 20°, 30°, 45°, 60°.
- ✅ **Bank equation:** $\tan \phi = V\omega / g$.
- ✅ **Rule of thumb (kts):** bank° ≈ (V_kts / 10) + 7 for standard rate.
- ✅ **Six principles:** Moving Part, Pictorial Realism, Functional Whole, Preattentive, Reference Frame, Stereopsis.

### Process of Hearing
- ✅ **Pathway:** Pinna → Canal → Tympanic Membrane → Ossicles → Cochlea → CN VIII → Cochlear Nucleus → SOC → IC → MGN → Cortex.
- ✅ **Mnemonic:** "**C**ochlear → **S**OC → **I**C → **M**GN → **A**uditory cortex" = **CSIMA**.
- ✅ **Ossicle gain** ≈ **27 dB** (impedance matching).
- ✅ **Ear-canal resonance** ≈ **2.7 kHz**.
- ✅ **Basilar membrane** is **tonotopic** (base = high freq, apex = low freq).
- ✅ **Békésy place theory** of pitch.
- ✅ **Hearing range:** 20 Hz – 20 kHz; **most sensitive** 1–4 kHz.
- ✅ **Localization cues:** ITD (< 1.5 kHz), ILD (> 1.5 kHz), pinna spectral notches (front/back), head motion.

### Decibels & Acoustics
- ✅ **SPL:** $L_p = 20 \log_{10}(p_{rms}/20\mu\text{Pa})$.
- ✅ **SIL:** $L_I = 10 \log_{10}(I/10^{-12}\text{W/m}^2)$.
- ✅ **Impedance of air:** $Z_0 = \rho c \approx 415 \, \text{Rayl}$.
- ✅ **+10 dB** ≈ perceived doubling of loudness; **+20 dB** = 100× intensity.
- ✅ **OSHA limit:** 90 dBA for 8 hours (NIOSH 85 dBA).
- ✅ **Threshold of pain:** 140 dB SPL.
- ✅ **Fletcher-Munson:** ear is most sensitive 1–4 kHz.

### Principles of Auditory Displays
- ✅ **Miller's 5 ± 2 limit** for absolute identification.
- ✅ **Relative identification** (with reference) allows unlimited channels.
- ✅ **Dimension mapping:** Loudness → urgency; Pitch → rate; Timbre → source; Location → spatial; Rhythm → status.
- ✅ **Recommended SNR** ≥ 10–15 dB for safety-critical.
- ✅ **Auditory icons** = real-world sounds; **Earcons** = structured non-verbal tones.
- ✅ **Use auditory** when eyes busy or info is alerting; **use visual** when quantitative or > 7 channels.
- ✅ **3-D audio** uses ITD + ILD + pinna filtering for spatialization.

### Examiner Survival Kit
- ✅ Always state the **reference value** for dB.
- ✅ Always use the **correct multiplier** (20 for SPL, 10 for SIL).
- ✅ Always **justify** your design choice in 14-mark questions.
- ✅ Always include a **numerical example** if the question says "compute" or "derive."
- ✅ Always **cite Miller's Law** when discussing auditory channels.
- ✅ Always **cite Wickens / Boff & Lincoln** by name for the six display principles.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Auditory Displays for Specific Purposes

**Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology):**
An *auditory display* is a human-machine interface element that conveys information, status, alerts, or feedback to a user through the auditory (hearing) channel, exploiting the human auditory system's capabilities and limitations. For *specific purposes*, auditory displays are engineered to deliver targeted information such as warnings, alarms, status confirmations, navigation cues, data sonification, and speech-based communication in operational environments where the visual channel is overloaded, unavailable, or insufficient.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Auditory displays are critical when the visual channel is saturated (e.g., aircraft cockpits, control rooms, vehicle operation) or when rapid alerting is required. They must be designed within the human auditory perception range of **20 Hz – 20,000 Hz**, with peak sensitivity between **2,000 – 4,000 Hz** (the frequency range of the human voice and warning signals).

#### Classification of Auditory Displays

| Type | Description | Example |
|------|-------------|---------|
| **Verbal (Speech) Displays** | Synthesized or recorded human voice | GPS voice navigation, cockpit warnings ("Pull up!") |
| **Non-Verbal (Tonal) Displays** | Tones, beeps, chimes, bells | Heart monitor beep, microwave "ding" |
| **Auditory Icons** | Natural, everyday sounds mapped to events | Paper-shuffling sound for file transfer |
| **Earcons** | Abstract, structured musical motifs | Mobile phone notification jingles |
| **Sonification** | Data mapped to sound parameters | Geiger counter clicks, seismic activity monitor |
| **Auditory Alarms/Warnings** | Urgent attention-demanding signals | Fire alarm, ICU ventilator alarm |

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy — The "Two Senses" Cooking Analogy:**
> Imagine you are cooking a complex meal. Your **eyes** (visual display) watch the stove, recipes, and timers. But when your hands are wet and your eyes are focused on a hot pan, your **ears** (auditory display) take over — the *timer bell* (tonal alarm) tells you the bread is done, your *partner's voice from the doorway* (speech display) warns you that someone is at the door, and the *sizzle becoming a crackle* (auditory icon) warns you the oil is too hot. Each sound has a *specific purpose* — and each is engineered deliberately to grab attention *without* requiring you to look. That is the essence of auditory display design: using the right sound for the right job.

### 1.2 Cutaneous Senses — Tactual (Tactile) Displays

**Formal Definition:**
The *cutaneous senses* (or *somatosensory* system) are the network of receptors in the skin that detect mechanical, thermal, and noxious stimuli. A *tactual display* (or *haptic display*) is a device that stimulates these cutaneous receptors to convey information to a user through touch, vibration, pressure, temperature, or texture. It is the third major sensory channel in human factors engineering, complementing visual and auditory displays.

The skin contains specialized **mechanoreceptors** (touch), **thermoreceptors** (temperature), and **nociceptors** (pain), which are the physiological basis of tactual displays.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The human skin has approximately **5,000,000 touch receptors**, **3,000,000 pressure receptors**, and the highest tactile acuity on the **fingertips, lips, and tongue** (two-point discrimination as low as **1–2 mm**). The back has the poorest tactile acuity (~40–70 mm). This non-uniformity is critical in designing tactual displays.

#### Classification of Cutaneous Senses & Tactual Displays

| Sense Modality | Receptor Type | Typical Use in Displays |
|----------------|---------------|-------------------------|
| **Pressure / Touch** | Merkel cells, Meissner corpuscles | Braille readers, key-press feedback |
| **Vibration** | Pacinian corpuscles, Ruffini endings | Mobile phone buzz, game controllers |
| **Temperature** | Cold & warm thermoreceptors | Surgical training simulators |
| **Pain (Nociception)** | Free nerve endings | Safety alerts (e.g., collision warning) |
| **Kinesthesia (deep)** | Muscle spindles, joint receptors | Force feedback joysticks (often combined with cutaneous) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy — The "Smartphone" Analogy:**
> Hold your smartphone. When it vibrates silently in your pocket (vibrotactile display), you *feel* a call without looking. When you press an on-screen key, the phone gives a small tap (tactile feedback) so you know your touch was registered. When the GPS says "turn left" while in your pocket (auditory), it speaks aloud. When you *type on a laptop's smooth surface*, you feel the lack of click (a missing tactile cue) and may press harder. These are all *tactual displays* — using the sense of touch to communicate information when the eyes and ears are busy. Modern cars use *seat vibration* to warn the driver of lane departure; that is a tactual display in critical safety engineering.

#### Why Combine Auditory and Tactual Displays?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Multimodal Design Principle (KTU 2024 NEP):** Modern human factors engineering promotes *multimodal displays* — using multiple sensory channels (visual + auditory + tactile) — to:
> 1. **Reduce information overload** on a single channel
> 2. **Increase redundancy** for safety-critical alerts
> 3. **Cater for users with sensory impairments** (universal design)
> 4. **Improve situational awareness** in high-stress environments

## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Framework: Auditory Display Design

#### 2.1.1 Human Auditory Perception Parameters

The design of auditory displays is governed by measurable psychophysical parameters of the human ear:

- **Frequency Range:** 20 Hz to 20,000 Hz (degrades with age — *presbycusis*).
- **Loudness Range (Dynamic Range):** From the absolute threshold (~0 dB SPL) to the pain threshold (~120–130 dB SPL).
- **Frequency Discrimination:** Humans can distinguish two tones differing by ~0.3% (about 3 Hz at 1 kHz, the famous *Weber's Law* for pitch).
- **Loudness Discrimination:** Just-Noticeable Difference (JND) in loudness is approximately **1 dB**.
- **Critical Bands:** The ear acts as a bank of bandpass filters (~24 critical bands covering the audible range), each with bandwidth:

$$CB \approx 25 + 75 \left(1 + 1.4 f^2\right)^{0.69} \text{ Hz}$$

where $f$ is the center frequency in kHz. Critical bands explain *auditory masking* — a critical KTU concept.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Auditory Masking:** A louder sound (the *masker*) raises the hearing threshold of a softer sound (the *target*) when their frequencies are within one critical band. **Engineering Implication:** When designing warning alarms in a noisy cockpit, the alarm frequency must lie *outside* the masking bandwidth of background noise (typically above 4 kHz) to be detected.

#### 2.1.2 Types of Auditory Displays — Detailed Design Logic

**(A) Speech (Verbal) Displays**
- Use recorded or **synthesized speech** (Text-To-Speech, TTS).
- Provide explicit, unambiguous information (e.g., "Cabin pressure low").
- **Advantages:** Direct, language-rich, doesn't require learning abstract codes.
- **Disadvantages:** Slower than tones; can be masked by other speech; requires attention.
- **Design Rule (KTU):** Speech messages should be **< 2.5 seconds**, use a *female voice* (more easily distinguished from male background speech in cockpits), and follow the *redundancy principle* — combine with a non-verbal warning tone.

**(B) Non-Speech (Tonal / Auditory Icon / Earcon) Displays**

| Sub-type | Design Principle | KTU Example / Use Case |
|----------|------------------|------------------------|
| **Auditory Icons** | Natural sounds metaphorically mapped to events | File deletion → crumpling paper sound |
| **Earcons** | Abstract short musical motifs (Pitch + Rhythm + Timbre) | Distinct melodies for "New Email," "Low Battery" |
| **Pure Tones** | Single frequency sinusoidal signal | Simple alert beep |
| **Complex Tones** | Multiple harmonics; richer timbre | Telephone ring, alarm bells |

**Earcon Grammar (Blattner, Sumikawa & Greenberg):** Earcons are built from three parameters:
1. **Pitch** (high/low) — encodes priority
2. **Rhythm** (tempo, pattern) — encodes function
3. **Timbre** (instrument quality) — encodes category

**(C) Warning Alarms**

KTU 2024 identifies **three auditory urgency levels** (Edworthy & Adams framework):

| Urgency Level | Recommended Tone Character | Engineering Example |
|---------------|----------------------------|---------------------|
| **Low (Caution)** | Slow pulse, lower frequency, soft | "Door ajar" in car |
| **Medium (Warning)** | Moderate pulse rate, mid frequency | Low fuel warning |
| **High (Critical/Danger)** | Fast pulse, high frequency, loud, irregular | Fire alarm, EKG critical alarm |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Design Guideline (NFPA & IEC 60601-1-8 referenced principles):**
> - The alarm frequency should be in the **500 Hz – 4,000 Hz** range to avoid masking.
> - Pulse repetition rate signals urgency: slow (< 1 Hz) = caution, fast (4–8 Hz) = danger.
> - The number of pulses can encode *type* of hazard (3 pulses = "Fire," 4 pulses = "Evacuate" in some medical standards).
> - Loudness should be at least **15 dB** above ambient noise.

### 2.2 Theoretical Framework: Tactual Display Design

#### 2.2.1 Mechanoreceptor Characteristics

Tactual displays are designed around the response characteristics of cutaneous mechanoreceptors:

| Receptor | Location | Adaptation | Frequency Response | Display Use |
|----------|----------|------------|---------------------|-------------|
| **Merkel cells** | Skin surface (superficial) | Slow (SA-I) | 0–30 Hz | Static pressure, Braille dots |
| **Meissner corpuscles** | Dermal papillae (superficial) | Fast (FA-I) | 20–50 Hz | Low-frequency vibration, slip detection |
| **Ruffini endings** | Deep dermis | Slow (SA-II) | 0–~10 Hz (best for skin stretch) | Directional stretch, shape perception |
| **Pacinian corpuscles** | Deep dermis/subcutaneous | Fast (FA-II) | 200–300 Hz (peak ~250 Hz) | High-frequency vibration, mobile phone buzz |

> [!NOTE]
> **The Pacinian channel is the most exploited in vibrotactile displays** because of its high sensitivity at ~250 Hz, which matches common actuator frequencies (ERM, LRA motors in phones).

#### 2.2.2 Psychophysical Parameters of Tactual Perception

- **Two-Point Discrimination (Acuity):** Minimum distance at which two points are felt as separate.
  - Fingertip: **~1–2 mm**
  - Palm: **~10 mm**
  - Forearm: **~25 mm**
  - Back: **~40–70 mm**

- **Vibration Detection Threshold:** Lowest amplitude detectable at a given frequency. Pacinian threshold curve has a *U-shape* with minimum near **200–300 Hz**.

- **Spatial Summation:** Tactile sensitivity increases with the area of stimulation (up to a few cm²).

- **Temporal Summation:** Detection threshold decreases with stimulus duration (up to ~1 second).

#### 2.2.3 Engineering Parameters of Tactual Displays

| Display Type | Stimulus | Typical Frequency / Force | Application |
|--------------|----------|---------------------------|-------------|
| **Vibrotactile** | Mechanical vibration | 10–500 Hz (peak 250 Hz) | Phones, wearables, game controllers |
| **Electrotactile** | Skin-surface electrical current | 0–500 Hz, low current (< 5 mA) | Sensory substitution for blind (e.g., tongue displays) |
| **Electromechanical (Pin-array)** | Mechanical pins moving up/down | 0.5–2 mm displacement, 1–10 N | Braille, refreshable tactile displays |
| **Force-Feedback (Haptic)** | Resistance/torque from motor | Variable; combined with kinesthesia | Surgical robots, flight simulators |
| **Thermal** | Heating/cooling elements | 5–45 °C range | Surgery simulators, AR/VR |
| **Pneumatic / Air-jet** | Air pressure on skin | 0.1–2 kPa | Novel automotive seating alerts |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Real-World Utility — Modern Applications:**
> 1. **Automotive lane-departure warning:** Seat vibrates on the side of the lane drift (vibrotactile).
> 2. **Aviation:** Stick shaker vibrates control yoke at *stall* (vibrotactile warning).
> 3. **Medical surgery simulators (e.g., da Vinci robot):** Force feedback lets the surgeon *feel* tissue stiffness.
> 4. **Tactile Maps for the Visually Impaired:** Raised-line drawings read by fingers.
> 5. **Smart Watches:** Patterned vibrations distinguish calls, messages, alarms (e.g., Apple Taptic Engine).

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Rule | Units / Notes |
|---------|----------------|---------------|
| Sound Pressure Level | $L_p = 20 \log_{10} \left( \dfrac{p}{p_0} \right)$, $p_0 = 20\,\mu\text{Pa}$ | Decibels (dB SPL) |
| Loudness (Phon) | Equal-loudness contour at 1 kHz | Phon ≈ dB at 1 kHz |
| Loudness (Sone) | $S = 2^{(P-40)/10}$ | Sone; $P$ in phon |
| Pitch (Mel) | $m = 1127 \ln \left( 1 + \dfrac{f}{700} \right)$ | Mel scale; $f$ in Hz |
| Critical Bandwidth | $CB \approx 25 + 75(1 + 1.4 f^2)^{0.69}$ | $f$ in kHz, $CB$ in Hz |
| Weber's Law (Pitch) | $\dfrac{\Delta f}{f} \approx 0.003$ | Just-Noticeable Difference |
| Two-Point Discrimination | Empirical: fingertip 1–2 mm; back 40–70 mm | Depends on receptor density |
| Vibrotactile Peak Sensitivity | $f_{peak} \approx 250$ Hz | Pacinian corpuscle channel |
| Alarm Loudness Rule | $L_{alarm} \geq L_{ambient} + 15$ dB | IEC 60601-1-8 (Medical) |
| Optimal Speech Duration | $\leq 2.5$ seconds | To avoid delay-induced error |
