topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT742 - HUMAN FACTORS IN ENGINEERING AND DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Work Physiology; Measure of Physiological Strain, Physical Workload, Strength & Endurance; Manual Material Handling (MMH), Biomechanical Recommended Limits of MMH; Reducing the Risk of MMH Overexertion; General Principles of Biomechanical Design; Anthropometry

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Work Physiology

**Work Physiology** is the branch of human factors/ergonomics that scientifically studies the **energy transformations, cardiovascular responses, respiratory functions, and muscular metabolism** that occur in the human body during the performance of physical work. It bridges the gap between the *work demanded* by a task and the *work capacity* of the human operator.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Definition:** Work physiology is the systematic study of the physiological responses and adaptations of the human body to physical work, including metabolic, cardiovascular, respiratory, and muscular changes that determine the worker's capacity to perform tasks without undue fatigue or injury.

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
Think of the human body as a hybrid car. Just like a hybrid switches between electric (aerobic) and gasoline (anaerobic) energy depending on the demand, the body switches between **aerobic metabolism** (using oxygen, sustainable, slow) and **anaerobic metabolism** (without oxygen, fast, but builds up lactic acid "battery heat"). Work physiology is the engineering dashboard that tells you *which engine* is running, *how hot* it's getting (strain), and *how much fuel* is left (endurance).

---

## 1.2 Measure of Physiological Strain

**Physiological Strain** is the internal load placed on the body's regulatory systems (heart, lungs, muscles) as a response to a physical workload. It is a *response variable*, while workload is the *stimulus variable*.

The **four principal measures** of physiological strain (with standard units in **bold**) are:

1. **Heart Rate (HR)** — measured in **beats per minute (bpm)**
2. **Oxygen Consumption (VO₂)** — measured in **liters per minute (L/min)** or **mL/kg/min**
3. **Energy Expenditure / Metabolic Rate** — measured in **kilocalories per minute (kcal/min)** or **watts (W)**
4. **Body (Core) Temperature** — measured in **degrees Celsius (°C)**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Acceptable Strain Limits (KTU Board Standard):**
> - Working HR should not exceed **110 bpm** for an 8-hour shift.
> - Peak HR for sustained work: **40 bpm above resting HR**.
> - Maximum energy expenditure for an 8-hour shift: **5.0 kcal/min**.
> - Maximum aerobic capacity (VO₂max) utilization: **35–50%** for an 8-hour work day.

---

## 1.3 Physical Workload

**Physical Workload** is the external demand imposed on a worker by a task. It is the *cause* of physiological strain. Workload can be classified as:

- **Static Workload:** Sustained muscular contraction with little movement (e.g., holding a heavy tool). Causes continuous compression of blood vessels → local fatigue.
- **Dynamic Workload:** Rhythmic muscular contraction with movement (e.g., walking, lifting repetitively). Promotes blood circulation → global aerobic load.
- **Combined Workload:** Both static and dynamic components acting simultaneously.

> [!TIP]
> A simple heuristic: **Static work tires muscles; dynamic work tires the heart.**

---

## 1.4 Strength & Endurance

### 1.4.1 Muscle Strength
**Muscle Strength** is the maximum force a muscle or muscle group can generate at a specified velocity. It is classified as:

- **Static (Isometric) Strength:** Force produced without change in muscle length. Maximum strength is exerted at approximately **mid-range joint angle**.
- **Dynamic (Isotonic/Isokinetic) Strength:** Force produced with movement.
  - **Concentric:** Muscle shortens (lifting phase).
  - **Eccentric:** Muscle lengthens under load (lowering phase — can produce **20–40% more force** than concentric).

### 1.4.2 Endurance
**Endurance** is the ability to sustain a force or work output over time. It is inversely related to the percentage of Maximum Voluntary Contraction (MVC) used:

$$\text{Endurance Time} \propto \frac{1}{\text{Force Level (% MVC)}}$$

**Rohmert's Endurance Curve:** Holding a static posture at **>15–20% MVC** for more than a few minutes produces rapid fatigue.

---

## 1.5 Manual Material Handling (MMH)

**Manual Material Handling (MMH)** refers to any activity that requires a worker to lift, lower, push, pull, carry, or restrain an object. MMH is the **single largest cause** of occupational injuries (especially low-back disorders) in industry.

**Components of MMH:**
1. Lifting
2. Lowering
3. Pushing
4. Pulling
5. Carrying
6. Holding/Restraint
7. Throwing

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Board Pitfall:** Many students confuse MMH with *manual dexterity* (fine motor tasks). MMH is strictly about **whole-body gross motor** transport of loads.

---

## 1.6 Biomechanical Recommended Limits of MMH

The **NIOSH Lifting Equation (NLE)** provides the **Recommended Weight Limit (RWL)** — the load that nearly all healthy workers can lift safely.

The six **multipliers** of the NLE that adjust the **Load Constant (LC = 23 kg)** are:

- **LC** — Load Constant = **23 kg**
- **HM** — Horizontal Multiplier
- **VM** — Vertical Multiplier
- **DM** — Distance Multiplier
- **AM** — Asymmetry Multiplier
- **FM** — Frequency Multiplier
- **CM** — Coupling Multiplier

The **Lifting Index (LI)** is the ratio of actual load lifted to RWL:

$$LI = \frac{\text{Actual Load (L)}}{\text{RWL}}$$

- LI ≤ 1.0 → Safe for most workers.
- LI 1.0–3.0 → Increased risk; administrative controls needed.
- LI > 3.0 → Unacceptable risk; engineering redesign required.

---

## 1.7 Reducing the Risk of MMH Overexertion

The **hierarchy of controls** applied to MMH (in order of effectiveness):

1. **Elimination** — Remove the need to lift (e.g., conveyors).
2. **Engineering Controls** — Redesign workstation, use hoists/dollies, adjust heights.
3. **Administrative Controls** — Job rotation, rest breaks, training in proper technique.
4. **Personal Protective Equipment (PPE)** — Back belts (controversial; not recommended as sole control).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **"Power Zone" Principle:** Always lift within the **zone between the knuckles and the shoulders** — close to the body, with the load between **knuckle height (≈ 71 cm) and shoulder height (≈ 137 cm)**.

---

## 1.8 General Principles of Biomechanical Design

**Biomechanics** applies mechanical principles (force, moment, lever) to the human body. The five design principles are:

1. **Maintain Neutral Postures** — Joints in mid-range; avoid extreme flexion/extension/twisting.
2. **Reduce Excessive Forces** — Use mechanical advantage; keep forces below strength limits.
3. **Perform Work at Optimal Heights** — Elbow height for precision work; knuckle-to-shoulder for heavy work.
4. **Minimize Repetition & Static Loads** — Provide variation, micro-breaks.
5. **Minimize Fatigue** — Reduce sustained contractions; allow recovery time.

**Lever Principle in the Body:**
- **First-class lever:** Triceps extension (fulcrum at elbow).
- **Second-class lever:** Standing on toes (calf raise; fulcrum at toes).
- **Third-class lever (most common in body):** Biceps curl (force between fulcrum and load — **mechanical disadvantage, but favors speed & range of motion**).

---

## 1.9 Anthropometry

**Anthropometry** is the scientific study of the **measurements and proportions of the human body** — lengths, breadths, depths, circumferences, weights, and ranges of motion. It is the foundation for designing equipment, workspaces, controls, and PPE.

**Two design philosophies (critical for KTU):**

| Philosophy | Description | Use Case |
|------------|-------------|----------|
| **Design for the Average** | Design for the 50th percentile (mean) | Inappropriate — excludes ~50% of population |
| **Design for the Range** | Use 5th–95th percentile range with adjustability | KTU-recommended best practice |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Anthropometric Statistical Basis:**
> Human dimensions follow a **Normal (Gaussian) Distribution** with:
> - **5th percentile** = μ − 1.645σ (accommodates short/small users)
> - **50th percentile** = μ (mean)
> - **95th percentile** = μ + 1.645σ (accommodates tall/large users)
> 
> Here, **μ** is the **mean** and **σ** is the **standard deviation**.

**Functional vs. Static Anthropometry:**
- **Static:** Body dimensions at rest (e.g., standing height, sitting height).
- **Functional (Dynamic):** Body dimensions during movement (e.g., reach envelope, legroom when entering a vehicle).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 The Energy Pathway: From Food to Work

The human body converts chemical energy (food) → mechanical work via three energy systems:

### Step 1: ATP-CP System (Phosphagen)
- Stored ATP + Creatine Phosphate.
- Duration: **0–10 seconds**. Anaerobic, alactic (no lactate).
- Use: Sprints, explosive lifts.

### Step 2: Anaerobic Glycolysis (Lactic Acid System)
- Glucose → Pyruvate → Lactate.
- Duration: **10 seconds – 2 minutes**.
- Use: Heavy lifting bouts, sustained high-intensity work.

### Step 3: Aerobic Oxidation
- Glucose + O₂ → CO₂ + H₂O + ATP.
- Duration: **> 2 minutes**, sustainable.
- Use: Prolonged moderate work, walking, cycling.

> **Why this matters:** The choice of energy system dictates **how long a worker can sustain a task** and **what kind of fatigue** (local muscular vs. cardiovascular) they will experience.

---

## 2.2 Cardiovascular Response to Work

As workload increases, **Heart Rate (HR)** rises linearly with oxygen consumption (VO₂) up to a maximum. This is the basis of the **HR–VO₂ Linear Relationship**.

**Karvonen Formula** for Target Heart Rate (used in fitness/work prescription):

$$THR = \left( HR_{max} - HR_{rest} \right) \times \text{Intensity (\%)} + HR_{rest}$$

$$HR_{max} = 220 - \text{Age (years)}$$

**Working HR Allowance** for safe occupational work:

$$HR_{working} \leq HR_{rest} + 40 \text{ bpm}$$

This is the **40 bpm rule** — the KTU-accepted limit for sustained occupational work.

---

## 2.3 Strength Analysis

### 2.1 Static Strength Model

Static strength depends on:
1. **Body posture** (strongest at mid-range joint angles).
2. **Direction of force application** (push > pull in many cases).
3. **Gender** (women have ~60–70% of male static strength on average).
4. **Age** (peaks at 25–30 years, declines thereafter).

### 2.2 Strength-to-Load Ratio

To prevent overexertion, the **Applied Force** must be ≤ **Strength Capacity**:

$$\text{Force Utilization} = \frac{F_{applied}}{F_{max}} \times 100\% \leq 70\%$$

> Acceptable threshold for a one-time exertion: **70% MVC**.
> For repetitive/sustained work: **15–20% MVC** (Rohmert limit).

---

## 2.4 Endurance (Rohmert's Curve)

**Rohmert's Endurance Equation (for static work):**

$$t_{end} = \frac{K_1}{F_{rel} - K_2}$$

Where:
- $t_{end}$ = Endurance time in seconds
- $F_{rel}$ = Force as a fraction of MVC (e.g., 0.5 for 50% MVC)
- $K_1 \approx 18$, $K_2 \approx 0.15$ (empirical constants)

**Worked intuition:** If you hold **50% MVC**, endurance is much shorter than holding **20% MVC** because the (F_rel − K₂) denominator grows, shrinking t_end.

---

## 2.5 NIOSH Lifting Equation — Full Theoretical Decomposition

The NIOSH equation calculates the **Recommended Weight Limit (RWL)**:

$$RWL = LC \times HM \times VM \times DM \times AM \times FM \times CM$$

| Parameter | Meaning | Range | Ideal (Multiplier = 1) |
|-----------|---------|-------|------------------------|
| **LC** | Load Constant (max recommended weight at ideal posture) | — | **23 kg** |
| **HM** | Horizontal distance from spine to hands | 25–63 cm | 25 cm |
| **VM** | Vertical height of hands from floor | 0–175 cm | 76 cm |
| **DM** | Vertical travel distance | 25–175 cm | 25 cm |
| **AM** | Asymmetry angle (twist) | 0–135° | 0° |
| **FM** | Frequency × duration | 0.2–>12 lifts/min | < 0.2 lifts/min |
| **CM** | Coupling (hand-container grip) | Good/Fair/Poor | Good |

**Asymmetry Multiplier formula:**

$$AM = 1 - 0.0032 \times A \quad \text{where } A \text{ is asymmetry in degrees}$$

**Horizontal Multiplier formula:**

$$HM = \frac{25}{H} \quad \text{where } H \text{ is horizontal reach in cm}$$

**Vertical Multiplier formula:**

$$VM = 1 - 0.0032 \times \vert V - 75 \vert$$

**Distance Multiplier formula:**

$$DM = 0.82 + \frac{4.5}{D} \quad \text{where } D \text{ is travel distance in cm}$$

**Frequency Multiplier formula (sample):**

$$FM = \frac{1 - 0.0032 \times f \times T_{avg}}{1} \text{ (interpolated from NIOSH table)}$$

**Coupling Multiplier** is taken from a table: Good = 1.0, Fair = 0.95, Poor = 0.90.

---

## 2.6 Anthropometric Statistical Formulas

For a **Normal Distribution** (true for most human body dimensions):

$$P_x = \mu + z \cdot \sigma$$

Where:
- $P_x$ = the xth percentile dimension
- $\mu$ = population mean
- $\sigma$ = population standard deviation
- $z$ = standard normal deviate (z-score)

| Percentile | z-score |
|------------|---------|
| 1st | −2.326 |
| 2.5th | −1.96 |
| 5th | **−1.645** |
| 10th | −1.28 |
| 25th | −0.674 |
| 50th | 0.00 |
| 75th | +0.674 |
| 90th | +1.28 |
| 95th | **+1.645** |
| 97.5th | +1.96 |
| 99th | +2.326 |

**Design Rule (KTU standard):**
- For **minimum clearance** (e.g., headroom, legroom): use **95th percentile male**.
- For **maximum reach** (e.g., control height): use **5th percentile female**.
- For **seated dimensions**: use 5th–95th percentile of relevant population.

---

## 2.7 KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Units / Notes |
|---|-------------------|------------|---------------|
| 1 | RWL (NIOSH) | $LC \times HM \times VM \times DM \times AM \times FM \times CM$ | kg |
| 2 | Lifting Index | $LI = L / RWL$ | dimensionless |
| 3 | Karvonen THR | $THR = (HR_{max} - HR_{rest})\times I + HR_{rest}$ | bpm |
| 4 | Max HR | $HR_{max} = 220 - Age$ | bpm |
| 5 | Working HR limit | $HR_{rest} + 40$ | bpm |
| 6 | VO₂ max relation | $HR \uparrow \propto VO_2 \uparrow$ (linear) | L/min |
| 7 | Rohmert Endurance | $t_{end} = K_1 / (F_{rel} - K_2)$ | seconds |
| 8 | HM | $25 / H$ | H in cm |
| 9 | VM | $1 - 0.0032 \cdot \vert V - 75 \vert$ | V in cm |
| 10 | DM | $0.82 + 4.5 / D$ | D in cm |
| 11 | AM | $1 - 0.0032 \cdot A$ | A in degrees |
| 12 | Percentile | $P_x = \mu + z\sigma$ | cm, mm, kg |
| 13 | Static strength principle | Max at mid-range joint angle | — |
| 14 | Eccentric vs. concentric strength | Eccentric force = 1.2 to 1.4 × Concentric | ratio |
| 15 | Static load limit for sustained work | ≤ 15–20% MVC | — |
| 16 | Repetitive work limit | ≤ 50% VO₂max for 8-hour shift | — |
| 17 | Power Zone | Knuckle to shoulder height (71–137 cm) | cm |
| 18 | Energy expenditure threshold | ≤ 5.0 kcal/min for 8-hr work | kcal/min |

---

## 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive design:** Driver seat dimensions, pedal placement, headliner height (5th female to 95th male).
- **Industrial workstations:** Conveyor heights set at knuckle/waist height of average worker.
- **Aerospace:** Cockpit anthropometry for ejection seat range, control reach.
- **Healthcare:** Hospital bed dimensions, MRI bore diameter, surgical instrument grips.
- **Apparel/Consumer:** Phone screen reach (5th female thumb-to-index reach), door handle diameter.

> **Why this matters for KTU:** Module 3 is the **"engineering-physiology bridge"** — once you can quantify strain and recommend limits, you can *redesign* jobs, equipment, and environments to fit the human, not the other way around.

## 3.1 Analytical Matrix: How to Use This Section

| Sub-topic | Derivative Generated | Application |
|-----------|---------------------|-------------|
| 3.2 | NIOSH RWL numerical computation | Real lift problem |
| 3.3 | Lifting Index & risk classification | Safety audit |

---

## 3.2 Step-by-Step Derivation: NIOSH RWL Calculation

### Problem Context
A worker lifts a 15 kg box from a shelf located 40 cm in front of the spine, with hands at 50 cm above the floor, lifting to 150 cm above the floor. The lift is straight (no twist), occurs 3 times per minute for 1 hour, and the coupling is "Fair". Compute the **RWL**, the **Lifting Index**, and the **risk classification**.

### Step 1: Identify All Variables
- Actual load, L = 15 kg
- Horizontal distance, H = 40 cm
- Vertical start, V = 50 cm
- Vertical end, V' = 150 cm
- Distance (travel), D = V' − V = 150 − 50 = 100 cm
- Asymmetry, A = 0°
- Frequency, f = 3 lifts/min (for 1 hr) — FM from table ≈ 0.75
- Coupling, C = Fair → CM = 0.95
- Load Constant, LC = 23 kg

### Step 2: Compute HM

$$HM = \frac{25}{H} = \frac{25}{40} = 0.625$$

### Step 3: Compute VM

$$VM = 1 - 0.0032 \cdot \vert V - 75 \vert = 1 - 0.0032 \cdot \vert 50 - 75 \vert$$

$$VM = 1 - 0.0032 \cdot 25 = 1 - 0.08 = 0.92$$

### Step 4: Compute DM

$$DM = 0.82 + \frac{4.5}{D} = 0.82 + \frac{4.5}{100} = 0.82 + 0.045 = 0.865$$

### Step 5: Compute AM

$$AM = 1 - 0.0032 \cdot A = 1 - 0.0032 \cdot 0 = 1.0$$

### Step 6: Compute FM (from NIOSH table for V < 75 cm, f = 3 lifts/min, 1 hr)

$$FM \approx 0.75$$

### Step 7: Compute CM

$$CM = 0.95 \quad \text{(Fair)}$$

### Step 8: Compute RWL

$$RWL = LC \times HM \times VM \times DM \times AM \times FM \times CM$$

$$RWL = 23 \times 0.625 \times 0.92 \times 0.865 \times 1.0 \times 0.75 \times 0.95$$

Multiplying step by step:

$$23 \times 0.625 = 14.375$$

$$14.375 \times 0.92 = 13.225$$

$$13.225 \times 0.865 = 11.4396$$

$$11.4396 \times 1.0 = 11.4396$$

$$11.4396 \times 0.75 = 8.5797$$

$$8.5797 \times 0.95 = 8.1507 \text{ kg}$$

$$\boxed{RWL \approx 8.15 \text{ kg}}$$

### Step 9: Compute Lifting Index

$$LI = \frac{L}{RWL} = \frac{15}{8.15} = 1.84$$

### Step 10: Risk Classification

Since **1.0 < LI ≤ 3.0** → **Increased risk; some workers may be at risk; administrative controls + monitoring recommended.**

> **Engineering insight:** The actual load (15 kg) is well below the "unassisted" 23 kg load constant, but **poor posture, distance, and frequency** cut RWL to ~8 kg. This is why a "feels light" load can still be hazardous.

---

## 3.3 Step-by-Step Derivation: Karvonen Target Heart Rate

### Problem Context
A 35-year-old worker has a resting heart rate of 70 bpm. For a sustained moderate work task, intensity is 50% of HR reserve. Compute the target working HR.

### Step 1: Compute Max HR

$$HR_{max} = 220 - Age = 220 - 35 = 185 \text{ bpm}$$

### Step 2: Compute HR Reserve

$$HR_{reserve} = HR_{max} - HR_{rest} = 185 - 70 = 115 \text{ bpm}$$

### Step 3: Apply Intensity

$$THR = (HR_{reserve} \times I) + HR_{rest} = (115 \times 0.50) + 70$$

$$THR = 57.5 + 70 = 127.5 \text{ bpm}$$

### Step 4: Validate against 40 bpm rule

$$HR_{working} \leq HR_{rest} + 40 = 70 + 40 = 110 \text{ bpm}$$

Since **127.5 > 110 bpm**, this 50% intensity is **too high for sustained 8-hour work**. The intensity should be reduced to ~35% for occupational use.

**Corrected intensity for occupational ceiling:**

$$I_{occ} = \frac{110 - 70}{115} = \frac{40}{115} \approx 0.348 = 34.8\%$$

$$\boxed{I_{occ} \approx 35\% \text{ HR reserve}}$$

---

## 3.4 Step-by-Step Derivation: Rohmert Endurance Time

### Problem Context
A worker holds a tool at 40% of Maximum Voluntary Contraction (MVC). Compute the static endurance time using Rohmert's equation with K₁ = 18, K₂ = 0.15.

### Step 1: Define Relative Force

$$F_{rel} = 0.40 \text{ (i.e., 40% MVC)}$$

### Step 2: Substitute into Rohmert's equation

$$t_{end} = \frac{K_1}{F_{rel} - K_2} = \frac{18}{0.40 - 0.15}$$

$$t_{end} = \frac{18}{0.25} = 72 \text{ seconds}$$

### Step 3: Interpretation

$$\boxed{t_{end} \approx 72 \text{ seconds} = 1.2 \text{ minutes}}$$

> **Design implication:** Any static hold at 40% MVC longer than ~1 minute will cause local muscular fatigue. Therefore, **redesign** by reducing the weight of the tool, adding a counterbalance, or providing a support arm.

---

## 3.5 Step-by-Step Derivation: Anthropometric Percentile

### Problem Context
A population of male workers has a mean **stature (standing height)** of 1710 mm and a standard deviation of 65 mm. Find:
(a) The 5th percentile stature.
(b) The 95th percentile stature.
(c) The eye-height (≈ 93% of stature) for the 5th percentile worker.

### Step 1: Recall the percentile equation

$$P_x = \mu + z \cdot \sigma$$

### Step 2: Compute 5th Percentile (z = −1.645)

$$P_5 = 1710 + (-1.645)(65) = 1710 - 106.925 = 1603.075 \text{ mm}$$

$$P_5 \approx 1603 \text{ mm (≈ 160.3 cm)}$$

### Step 3: Compute 95th Percentile (z = +1.645)

$$P_{95} = 1710 + (1.645)(65) = 1710 + 106.925 = 1816.925 \text{ mm}$$

$$P_{95} \approx 1817 \text{ mm (≈ 181.7 cm)}$$

### Step 4: Compute Eye-Height for 5th Percentile

Eye-height ≈ 0.93 × Stature (anthropometric ratio)

$$EH_5 = 0.93 \times 1603.075 = 1490.86 \text{ mm} \approx 1491 \text{ mm}$$

### Step 5: Design Decision

For a **display panel** the worker should view without raising their head excessively, place the center of the display at the **5th percentile female eye-height** (typically 1430 mm) and not higher than the **95th percentile male eye-height** (1650 mm). This defines the **adjustable mounting range**.

---

## 3.6 Step-by-Step Derivation: Energy Expenditure from VO₂

### Problem Context
A worker has a resting VO₂ of 0.25 L/min and during work the VO₂ is 1.25 L/min. Compute the **net (work-related) energy expenditure** in kcal/min, given the conversion **1 L O₂ ≈ 5.0 kcal**.

### Step 1: Net Oxygen Consumption

$$VO_{2,net} = VO_{2,work} - VO_{2,rest} = 1.25 - 0.25 = 1.00 \text{ L/min}$$

### Step 2: Convert to Energy

$$EE_{net} = VO_{2,net} \times 5.0 \text{ kcal/L} = 1.00 \times 5.0 = 5.0 \text{ kcal/min}$$

### Step 3: Check against KTU occupational limit

The recommended maximum for an 8-hour shift is **5.0 kcal/min**.

$$\boxed{EE_{net} = 5.0 \text{ kcal/min} \rightarrow \text{at the upper limit; not sustainable indefinitely}}$$

**Design action:** Introduce work-rest cycles, mechanize part of the task, or add team rotation.

---

## 3.7 Analytical Summary Table

| Quantity | Formula | Typical Range | KTU Threshold |
|----------|---------|---------------|---------------|
| Static strength | Max at mid-angle | 200–800 N (hand) | — |
| Working HR | $HR_{rest} + 40$ | 100–130 bpm | ≤ 110 bpm |
| VO₂max utilization | VO₂ work / VO₂ max | 20–60% | ≤ 50% (8 hr) |
| Energy expenditure | VO₂ × 5 kcal/L | 2.5–8 kcal/min | ≤ 5 kcal/min |
| RWL (NIOSH) | LC × multipliers | 5–23 kg | ≥ Actual Load |
| Lifting Index | L / RWL | 0.5–3.0 | ≤ 1.0 |
| Static hold limit | 15–20% MVC | — | ≤ 20% MVC |
| 5th percentile | μ − 1.645σ | — | Min clearance |
| 95th percentile | μ + 1.645σ | — | Max clearance |
