# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define Work Physiology. List the four major measures of physiological strain and the recommended occupational limits for each.**

**Model Answer:**

**Work Physiology** is the branch of ergonomics that studies the metabolic, cardiovascular, respiratory, and muscular responses of the human body to physical work, with the goal of matching work demands to human capacity.

**Four major measures of physiological strain (with limits):**

| # | Measure | Unit | 8-hr Occupational Limit |
|---|---------|------|--------------------------|
| 1 | Heart Rate (HR) | bpm | $HR_{rest} + 40$ bpm (≤ 110 bpm) |
| 2 | Oxygen Consumption (VO₂) | L/min or mL/kg/min | ≤ 50% of VO₂max |
| 3 | Energy Expenditure (EE) | kcal/min | ≤ 5.0 kcal/min |
| 4 | Core Body Temperature | °C | ≤ 38.0–38.5 °C sustained |

---

### Question A2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Differentiate between Static Workload and Dynamic Workload. Why is static work more fatiguing than dynamic work at the same force level?**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Static Workload | Dynamic Workload |
|--------|------------------|------------------|
| Muscle behavior | Sustained contraction, no length change | Rhythmic contraction with movement |
| Blood flow | Continuously compressed → ischemia | Pulsatile compression → circulation maintained |
| Energy system | Primarily anaerobic | Primarily aerobic |
| Fatigue type | Local muscular fatigue | Cardiovascular fatigue |
| Recovery | Slow | Fast |
| Example | Holding a tool overhead | Walking, cycling |

**Why static work is more fatiguing:** During static contraction, intramuscular pressure exceeds capillary perfusion pressure, **occluding blood flow**. This prevents delivery of O₂ and nutrients and removal of metabolic by-products (lactate, H⁺, heat), causing rapid accumulation of fatigue metabolites. In dynamic work, the alternating contraction-relaxation cycle acts as a "pump," maintaining circulation.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU Rule:** Each Part B question must have two sub-parts (a) and (b) carrying 7 marks each. **Module-Internal Choice** is provided below — answer **either** Question A **or** Question B.

---

### QUESTION A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 – Module 3 Internal Choice]**

**(a)** [7 Marks] State the NIOSH Lifting Equation and explain all six multipliers used to compute the Recommended Weight Limit (RWL). Briefly discuss how the Lifting Index (LI) is used to classify lifting tasks.

**(b)** [7 Marks] A male worker (age 32, resting HR 72 bpm) performs a manual palletizing task. Compute (i) his **Maximum Heart Rate**, (ii) the **Target Heart Rate at 40% intensity** using the Karvonen formula, and (iii) the **highest sustainable working HR** for an 8-hour shift using the 40 bpm rule. Is the 40% intensity safe? Justify.

---

#### MODEL SOLUTION — (a) NIOSH Lifting Equation

**Statement:**

$$RWL = LC \times HM \times VM \times DM \times AM \times FM \times CM$$

$$LI = \frac{L}{RWL}$$

**Six Multipliers:**

1. **HM (Horizontal Multiplier)** — penalizes lifting with hands far from the spine (leverage increases back moment). $HM = 25/H$, maxes at H = 25 cm.
2. **VM (Vertical Multiplier)** — penalizes lifts not at the optimal 75 cm height. $VM = 1 - 0.0032 \vert V - 75 \vert$.
3. **DM (Distance Multiplier)** — penalizes long vertical travel of the load. $DM = 0.82 + 4.5/D$.
4. **AM (Asymmetry Multiplier)** — penalizes twisting. $AM = 1 - 0.0032A$, where A is the asymmetry angle in degrees (0° = straight, 135° = full twist).
5. **FM (Frequency Multiplier)** — penalizes frequent or prolonged lifting. Selected from a NIOSH table based on lifts/min and work duration.
6. **CM (Coupling Multiplier)** — penalizes poor hand-hold quality. Good = 1.0, Fair = 0.95, Poor = 0.90.

**LI Risk Classification:**

- **LI ≤ 1.0:** Safe for nearly all healthy workers.
- **1.0 < LI ≤ 3.0:** Increased risk; engineering or administrative controls recommended.
- **LI > 3.0:** Unacceptable risk; task redesign mandatory.

---

#### MODEL SOLUTION — (b) Heart Rate Computations

**Given:** Age = 32 years; HR_rest = 72 bpm; Intensity = 40%

**Step 1: Maximum Heart Rate**

$$HR_{max} = 220 - Age = 220 - 32 = 188 \text{ bpm}$$

**Step 2: Heart Rate Reserve**

$$HR_{reserve} = HR_{max} - HR_{rest} = 188 - 72 = 116 \text{ bpm}$$

**Step 3: Target Heart Rate at 40% intensity (Karvonen)**

$$THR = (HR_{reserve} \times I) + HR_{rest}$$

$$THR = (116 \times 0.40) + 72 = 46.4 + 72 = 118.4 \text{ bpm}$$

**Step 4: Maximum sustainable working HR (40 bpm rule)**

$$HR_{working, max} = HR_{rest} + 40 = 72 + 40 = 112 \text{ bpm}$$

**Step 5: Comparison and Conclusion**

$$\text{THR (40%)} = 118.4 \text{ bpm} > HR_{working, max} = 112 \text{ bpm}$$

**Conclusion:** **40% intensity is NOT safe** for an 8-hour shift. The intensity should be reduced.

**Safe Intensity Calculation:**

$$I_{safe} = \frac{112 - 72}{116} = \frac{40}{116} \approx 0.345 = 34.5\%$$

$$\boxed{I_{safe} \leq 34\% \text{ HR reserve for this worker}}$$

**Engineering recommendation:** Provide work-rest cycles, mechanical aids, or reduce load/pace.

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 – Module 3 Internal Choice]**

**(a)** [7 Marks] Define **Anthropometry**. Explain the difference between **Static** and **Functional (Dynamic) Anthropometry** with two examples each. State the statistical basis for designing for a population range, and the **design rule for minimum clearance** versus **maximum reach**.

**(b)** [7 Marks] The **stature** of a population of female workers is normally distributed with **mean μ = 1590 mm** and **standard deviation σ = 60 mm**. Compute:
(i) The **5th and 95th percentiles** of stature.
(ii) The **5th percentile sitting height** (assume sitting height = 0.52 × stature).
(iii) The **5th percentile eye-height (seated)** (assume seated eye-height = 0.79 × stature).
(iv) For an office chair, suggest the **minimum seat-pan height** (assume popliteal height = 0.25 × stature) at the 5th percentile.

---

#### MODEL SOLUTION — (a) Anthropometry

**Definition:** Anthropometry is the quantitative study of human body dimensions — lengths, breadths, depths, circumferences, weights, and ranges of motion — used as the basis for designing equipment, workstations, and environments to fit the user population.

**Static vs. Functional Anthropometry:**

| Type | Description | Example 1 | Example 2 |
|------|-------------|-----------|-----------|
| **Static** | Body measured at rest, in fixed, standardized postures | Standing height (stature) | Shoulder breadth |
| **Functional (Dynamic)** | Body measured during movement or activity | Forward functional reach (seated, gripping) | Legroom needed to enter a car seat |

**Statistical basis:** Most human body dimensions follow a **Normal (Gaussian) Distribution**. The **5th–95th percentile range** covers ~90% of the population, and a 1.645 σ deviation from the mean corresponds to the 5th/95th percentile — the conventional accommodation design range.

**Design Rules:**

- **Minimum clearance (e.g., headroom, legroom, doorway):** Design for the **95th percentile male** (μ + 1.645σ). Otherwise, the tallest user will not fit.
- **Maximum reach (e.g., control height, shelf reach):** Design for the **5th percentile female** (μ − 1.645σ). Otherwise, the shortest user cannot reach.

---

#### MODEL SOLUTION — (b) Anthropometric Calculations

**Given:** μ = 1590 mm, σ = 60 mm; z at 5th = −1.645; z at 95th = +1.645.

**(i) 5th and 95th Percentile Stature**

$$P_5 = \mu + z \cdot \sigma = 1590 + (-1.645)(60) = 1590 - 98.7 = 1491.3 \text{ mm}$$

$$P_{95} = 1590 + (1.645)(60) = 1590 + 98.7 = 1688.7 \text{ mm}$$

$$\boxed{P_5 \approx 1491 \text{ mm}; \quad P_{95} \approx 1689 \text{ mm}}$$

**(ii) 5th Percentile Sitting Height**

$$SH_5 = 0.52 \times P_5 = 0.52 \times 1491.3 = 775.5 \text{ mm}$$

**(iii) 5th Percentile Seated Eye-Height**

$$EH_{seated, 5} = 0.79 \times P_5 = 0.79 \times 1491.3 = 1178.1 \text{ mm}$$

**(iv) Minimum Seat-Pan Height (5th Percentile Popliteal Height)**

$$PH_5 = 0.25 \times P_5 = 0.25 \times 1491.3 = 372.8 \text{ mm}$$

**Design Rule for Seat Height:** Seat pan height should **accommodate the 5th percentile popliteal height** (so the shortest user's feet rest flat on the floor with a small clearance ≈ 25–50 mm).

$$\text{Minimum Seat-Pan Height} \approx 372.8 + 25 \text{ mm clearance} = 397.8 \text{ mm} \approx 400 \text{ mm}$$

$$\boxed{\text{Recommended seat height range: } \approx 400 \text{ mm (low) to } \approx 500 \text{ mm (high for 95th)}}$$

---

## 5.3 KTU EXAMINER'S VALUATION WARNING / PITFALL CALLOUT

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
> 
> 1. **Confusing LC units:** The Load Constant LC = **23 kg**, not 25 kg (which is for HM's optimal reach).
> 2. **Forgetting the 0.0032 constant:** Many students write AM = 1 − A; the correct form is AM = 1 − **0.0032** × A.
> 3. **Using 220 − Age for HR_max but applying it to THR directly:** THR uses **HR reserve**, not HR_max. Always subtract HR_rest.
> 4. **Mixing up clearance vs. reach:** 95th male = clearance; 5th female = reach. Reverse and you lose 1–2 marks.
> 5. **Forgetting to use the z-score table** in percentile problems; some students assume z = 1.0 (which corresponds to the **84th**, not 95th, percentile).
> 6. **Calculating gross VO₂ instead of net VO₂** for energy expenditure; the rest component must be subtracted.
> 7. **Treating MMH and manual dexterity as synonymous** — they are not. MMH is whole-body load handling.

---

## 5.4 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Work Physiology** = study of body's energy/physiological responses to physical work.
- **Workload** = stimulus (cause); **Strain** = response (effect).
- **4 strain measures:** HR, VO₂, EE, Core Temp.
- **Occupational HR ceiling** = HR_rest + **40 bpm**; Max EE = **5 kcal/min** for 8 hr.
- **Static work** occludes blood flow → fast local fatigue; **Dynamic work** allows circulation → sustainable.
- **Static strength** is max at **mid-range joint angle**; **Eccentric** > **Concentric** force (~20–40% higher).
- **Rohmert:** Endurance time $t_{end} = K_1 / (F_{rel} - K_2)$; sustained holds > **15–20% MVC** cause rapid fatigue.
- **NIOSH Equation:** $RWL = 23 \times HM \times VM \times DM \times AM \times FM \times CM$ (all dimensionless except LC and RWL in kg).
- **HM = 25/H; VM = 1 − 0.0032·|V − 75|; DM = 0.82 + 4.5/D; AM = 1 − 0.0032·A**.
- **LI ≤ 1** safe; **1 < LI ≤ 3** increased risk; **LI > 3** unacceptable.
- **MMH controls hierarchy:** Elimination → Engineering → Administrative → PPE (in decreasing effectiveness).
- **Power Zone** = knuckle to shoulder height (~71–137 cm); keep loads close to the body.
- **Biomechanical principles:** Neutral postures, reduce force, optimal heights, minimize repetition/static loads, minimize fatigue.
- **Body's third-class lever** → mechanical disadvantage but favors **speed + range of motion**; external tools provide mechanical advantage.
- **Anthropometry** = study of human body measurements; **Static** (rest) vs. **Functional** (during movement).
- **Normal distribution** applies; **P_x = μ + z·σ**.
- **z = ±1.645** for **5th and 95th percentiles** — KTU's standard accommodation range.
- **5th percentile female** for **max reach**; **95th percentile male** for **min clearance**; **5th–95th** for **adjustable range**.
- **Karvonen THR** uses HR **reserve**, not HR max directly.
- **1 L O₂ ≈ 5 kcal** for energy conversion; use **net** VO₂ for work-specific energy expenditure.
- **Always state units** (kg, bpm, kcal/min, mm) — KTU examiners deduct marks for missing units.
- **Subtract HR_rest** in Karvonen equation — common error.
- **Use 0.0032** (not 0.32 or 3.2) in NIOSH AM/VM equations.

> **Final Tip:** In KTU Board exams, structure every MMH/NLE problem in **6 clear steps**: (1) List variables, (2) Compute each multiplier, (3) Multiply sequentially, (4) Compute RWL, (5) Compute LI, (6) Conclude with risk class and design action. This 6-step format is what toppers use.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Anthropometric Design Principles; Static and Dynamic Anthropometry; Case Study of Anthropometric Design
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEEBT742 - HUMAN FACTORS IN ENGINEERING AND DESIGN > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Anthropometric Design Principles; Static and Dynamic Anthropometry; Case Study of Anthropometric Design # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Anthropometry — Formal KTU 2024 Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Anthropometry** is the scientific study of the measurements and proportions of the human body. In the context of engineering design, anthropometry provides the quantitative **dimensional database** of human body sizes, strengths, ranges of motion, and working postures that engineers use to design tools, equipment, workstations, vehicles, and environments to fit the human operator. Within the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PEEBT742 – Human Factors in Engineering and Design)**, anthropometry is classified as a foundational sub-discipline of **biomechanics and ergonomics**, dealing specifically with the **geometric compatibility** between the human body and the man-machine interface. **Two principal branches** are defined by the syllabus: | Branch | Alternative Name | What it Measures | |---|---|---| | **Static Anthropometry** | Structural / Structural Dimensions | Body dimensions measured when the body is held in a fixed, standardized, motionless posture | | **Dynamic Anthropometry** | Functional / Kinesiological | Body dimensions, reach, and clearance measured **during motion** or under load | ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy — The "T-Shirt Size" Problem** > Imagine you are a garment manufacturer producing **shirts for an entire country**. If you make all shirts in **one size**, roughly 90% of the population will be unhappy — too tight for some, too loose for others. The smart solution is to study the **distribution of shoulder widths, chest circumferences, and arm lengths** across the population, and then produce shirts in **Small, Medium, Large, XL**, etc., so that 95% of people get a comfortable fit. > > **Anthropometry is exactly this idea, applied to engineering design.** Instead of shirts, we design **cars, chairs, control panels, helmets, and doorways**. Instead of shoulder width, we measure **stature, eye height, reach distance, knee height, grip span**, etc. The data is then used so that 5%–95% of the user population (the "comfortable-fit middle band") can operate the product safely and comfortably. ## 1.3 Why Anthropometric Data Matters in Engineering > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Approximately **8–10% of the human population falls outside the "average" range** for any single body dimension. This is the foundation of all anthropometric design reasoning — designers **must consciously decide** which segment of the population their product will accommodate. **Three principal sources of human variability** that anthropometric data must address: 1. **Intra-individual variability** — the same person differs in size between morning and evening (≈ **1–2% change in stature**), between winter and summer, and across age. 2. **Inter-individual variability** — differences between different people (the dominant source of variation). 3. **Secular variability** — generational changes in body size due to nutrition, health, and lifestyle (modern generations are typically larger than their grandparents). ## 1.4 The Central Concept — Percentiles > [!NOTE] > A **percentile** is a statistical measure that indicates the value below which a given percentage of observations fall. In anthropometry, the **5th and 95th percentiles** are the two most important design boundaries. **Interpretation in plain language:** - The **5th-percentile** value of a body dimension is the value that is **larger than only 5% of the population** and **smaller than 95% of the population**. Used for **minimum-design** constraints (e.g., door heights, legroom, reach for a tall person — actually here, it is the small person whose needs we serve). - The **95th-percentile** value is **larger than 95%** of the population. Used for **maximum-design** constraints. - The **50th-percentile** is the **median** (the "average" person). - The **5th–95th percentile range** covers **90% of the user population**. > [!TIP] > **Mnemonic for the exam:** *"5% are smaller, 95% are smaller — design for both extremes to cover the middle 90%."* ## 1.5 Design Philosophy — The Three Golden Rules **KTU 2024 lists three classical anthropometric design strategies** (also called McFarland's design principles, or Grandjean's design rules): 1. **Design for the Average (Mean / 50th Percentile)** — Acceptable only when the consequence of misfit is trivial (e.g., decorative items, packaging). Risks excluding 50% of users. 2. **Design for the Range (5th–95th Percentile / Adjustable Design)** — The **preferred engineering approach**. The product is adjustable so that it can be set to fit anywhere from the 5th to the 95th percentile user. Example: car seat with sliding adjustment, office chair with pneumatic height adjustment, monitor arm. 3. **Design for the Extreme (One Extreme Only)** — Used only when adjustability is impossible and the dimension is critical for safety. Example: **emergency egress door widths, aircraft cockpit ejection seat clearances, minimum overhead clearance in tunnels.** --- # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Static (Structural) Anthropometry — The Theory > [!IMPORTANT] > **Definition (KTU 2024):** Static anthropometry deals with body dimensions measured when the subject is **stationary**, in **standardized reference postures** (e.g., standing erect, sitting erect, supine, prone, hands at sides, etc.), without any significant movement. ### 2.1.1 Standard Reference Postures (KTU High-Yield) | Posture | Description | Common Measurements | |---|---|---| | **Standing Erect** | Upright, head in Frankfurt plane, feet together, arms at sides | Stature, eye height, shoulder height, elbow height, knuckle height, fingertip height | | **Sitting Erect** | Seated on a flat surface, thighs parallel to floor, back unsupported | Sitting height, eye height (seated), shoulder height (seated), elbow height (seated), knee height | | **Hands-Functional** | Hand held in functional grasp position | Grip diameter, hand breadth, hand length | ### 2.1.2 Major Static Anthropometric Dimensions (Exhaustive List for KTU) **Lower-case abbreviations used by KTU:** Dimensions are typically given as a **mean ± standard deviation** and as **5th, 50th, and 95th percentiles** for the working population (often stratified by gender). 1. **Stature (Body Height, Vertex-to-Floor)** — most fundamental dimension 2. **Eye Height (Standing and Seated)** — critical for sightline and display visibility 3. **Shoulder Height / Shoulder Breadth (Bi-deltoid)** 4. **Elbow Height (Standing and Seated)** — for armrest and work-surface height 5. **Elbow-to-Fingertip Length (Functional Reach)** 6. **Hip Breadth (Sitting)** 7. **Knee Height (Sitting)** 8. **Popliteal Length (Sitting, back of knee to underside of thigh)** — for seat depth 9. **Buttock-to-Knee Length (Thigh Length)** — for seat depth 10. **Buttock-to-Popliteal Length** — for seat pan length 11. **Hand Length, Hand Breadth** 12. **Foot Length, Foot Breadth** 13. **Head Length, Head Breadth, Head Circumference** 14. **Sitting Height (Erect)** ## 2.2 Dynamic (Functional) Anthropometry — The Theory > [!IMPORTANT] > **Definition (KTU 2024):** Dynamic anthropometry deals with measurements of the body **in motion** — during reach, lift, push, pull, twist, walk, climb, or work activity. It also includes measurements of **muscle strength, range of joint motion, and the body dimensions under load (compressed soft tissue).** ### 2.2.1 Why Static Data is Insufficient > [!NOTE] > A famous case (often cited in KTU textbooks): a tall person may have a **long arm reach** (good for reaching overhead) but a **short leg** (so a long staircase tread is uncomfortable). Static measurements alone cannot predict how a person will fit in a **moving** workspace such as a truck cabin, a fighter-jet cockpit, or an assembly line. **Dynamic anthropometry captures the additional variables that emerge only during movement.** ### 2.2.2 Categories of Dynamic Measurements 1. **Reach Envelopes** — the **three-dimensional surface** in space traced by the hand/fingertip during maximum reach. 2. **Functional Reach (Forward, Lateral, Overhead)** — measured with subject seated or standing, body twisted/bent. 3. **Clearance Dimensions** — space required for body to **pass through** an opening while moving (walking, crouching, climbing). 4. **Body Motion Range (Joint Articulation)** — angles of rotation at shoulder, elbow, wrist, hip, knee, ankle, neck, trunk. 5. **Strength Measurements (Isometric, Isotonic, Isokinetic)** — grip strength, push/pull strength, lifting strength, torque strength. 6. **Working Posture Dimensions** — body dimensions when bent forward, kneeling, squatting, or lying down. ## 2.3 The KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!TIP] > **Cheat-Sheet Box — Master These for the Exam** ### 2.3.1 Statistical Formulas (Percentile and Range Estimation) | # | Formula | Variables & Units | Use | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $P_k = \mu + z_k \cdot \sigma$ | $P_k$ = k-th percentile value (mm)
$\mu$ = mean (mm)
$z_k$ = standard normal deviate (z-score) for k-th percentile
$\sigma$ = standard deviation (mm) | Estimate a specific percentile value from mean and standard deviation | | 2 | $R_{90} = P_{95} - P_{5}$ | $R_{90}$ = 5th–95th percentile range (mm)
$P_{95}$ = 95th percentile
$P_{5}$ = 5th percentile | Quantify the population range a design must accommodate | | 3 | $P_{50} \approx \mu$ (for normal distribution) | — | Median ≈ Mean for symmetric data | | 4 | $z_k$ values (KEEP ON FINGERTIP):
$z_{5} = -1.645$
$z_{50} = 0.000$
$z_{95} = +1.645$ | Standard normal deviates for the 5th, 50th, 95th percentiles | Direct lookup table for KTU problems | ### 2.3.2 Design Boundary Selection (CRITICAL FOR EXAM) | Design Type | Lower Boundary | Upper Boundary | When Used | |---|---|---|---| | **Clearance (must pass through / over)** | 95th percentile (large person) | — | Doorways, tunnels, escape hatches, overhead clearance | | **Reach (must reach an object)** | 5th percentile (small person, short arm) | — | Light switches, emergency controls, work-piece placement | | **Reach (must NOT reach, for safety)** | 95th percentile (long reach) | — | Guards, safety barriers, machine enclosures | | **Adjustable Design (seat, armrest, work surface)** | 5th percentile | 95th percentile | Vehicle seats, office furniture, hospital beds, control panels | | **Strength-based (must operate a control)** | 5th percentile (weak) for *operate*; 95th for *resist* | — | Pedals, levers, latches, fire alarms | | **Visibility / Sightline (must see over / past)** | 95th percentile eye height (seated) | — | Cockpit canopies, vehicle A-pillars, dashboard hood lines | ### 2.3.3 Adjustability Range Formula For an adjustable product (e.g., office chair): $$ L_{adj} = P_{95} - P_{5} $$ where $L_{adj}$ is the **adjustment range** required. If the product's adjustment is centred at the 50th percentile: $$ L_{adj} = P_{95} - P_{5} = 2 \times 1.645 \times \sigma \approx 3.29 \, \sigma $$ ### 2.3.4 Body-Surface-Area Approximation (used in some KTU problems) $$ A_{body} = 0.007184 \cdot W^{0.425} \cdot H^{0.725} $$ where $A_{body}$ is in m², $W$ is body weight in kg, $H$ is stature in cm. (DuBois formula.) ### 2.3.5 Functional-Reach Correction (for tight spaces) $$ R_{eff} = R_{static} \cdot k_{clothes} - k_{posture} $$ where $R_{eff}$ is effective reach, $R_{static}$ is the static reach, $k_{clothes}$ accounts for bulky clothing (≈ **0.95** for normal, **0.85** for arctic clothing), and $k_{posture}$ accounts for postural restriction (≈ **50–100 mm** for seated reach). ## 2.4 Why This Matters in Engineering — Real-World Utility | Application Domain | Anthropometric Variable Used | Design Decision | |---|---|---| | **Automotive — Driver Cabin** | Sitting eye height, hip-to-pedal, shoulder room, headroom | Seat track range, steering wheel reach, roof height | | **Aircraft Cockpit** | Sitting height, eye height, arm reach, leg length | Throttle quadrant placement, canopy height, instrument panel angle | | **Office Furniture** | Popliteal height, sitting eye height, elbow height | Chair height range, desk height, monitor arm reach | | **Personal Protective Equipment (Helmets, Gloves, Coveralls)** | Head circumference, hand length, torso length | Sizing matrix (S/M/L/XL), adjustable straps | | **Public Infrastructure** | Stature, shoulder breadth, hand reach | Door widths, ATM screen height, ticket-counter heights | | **Hospital Beds / OT Tables** | Stature, weight, limb lengths | Length, width, load capacity, articulation range | | **Defence — Tank Crewstation** | Seated eye height, functional reach, leg volume | Hatch opening, periscope placement, pedal layout | | **Industrial Workstations** | Elbow height, forward reach, shoulder breadth | Bench height, conveyor position, tool placement | --- # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Computing Percentile Boundaries from Mean and SD > [!NOTE] > **Problem:** For a population of Indian industrial workers, the **stature (standing height)** is found to follow a normal distribution with **mean $\mu = 1670$ mm** and **standard deviation $\sigma = 70$ mm**. Determine the **5th, 50th, and 95th percentile** values of stature. ### Solution — Step-by-Step (each step shown explicitly, no skipping) **Step 1 — Recall the percentile formula:** $$ P_k = \mu + z_k \cdot \sigma $$ **Step 2 — Look up the standard normal deviates (z-scores):** For the 5th percentile: $z_{5} = -1.645$ For the 50th percentile: $z_{50} = 0.000$ For the 95th percentile: $z_{95} = +1.645$ **Step 3 — Compute $P_{5}$:** $$ P_{5} = 1670 + (-1.645) \cdot 70 $$ $$ P_{5} = 1670 - 115.15 $$ $$ P_{5} = 1554.85 \text{ mm} \approx 1555 \text{ mm} $$ **Step 4 — Compute $P_{50}$:** $$ P_{50} = 1670 + 0.000 \cdot 70 = 1670 \text{ mm} $$ **Step 5 — Compute $P_{95}$:** $$ P_{95} = 1670 + (+1.645) \cdot 70 $$ $$ P_{95} = 1670 + 115.15 $$ $$ P_{95} = 1785.15 \text{ mm} \approx 1785 \text{ mm} $$ **Step 6 — Interpretation (this is what KTU examiners look for):** > "**5% of the population is shorter than 1555 mm, and 5% is taller than 1785 mm.** A design that must accommodate **90% of users** should have an adjustment range of at least $R_{90} = P_{95} - P_{5} = 230$ mm." --- ## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Designing an Adjustable Office Chair Seat Height > [!NOTE] > **Problem:** Design the **height range** of a seat pan of an office chair intended to fit 90% of the working population. The population data for **popliteal height (PH)** — the distance from the underside of the foot to the underside of the thigh — is given as: **mean $\mu_{PH} = 445$ mm**, **$\sigma_{PH} = 25$ mm**. ### Solution — Step-by-Step **Step 1 — Identify the design intent.** Seat pan height should match the **popliteal height** of the user so that the **thighs are horizontal** and the **feet are flat on the floor**. **Step 2 — Identify which percentile boundaries to use.** To cover 90% of users, the chair must adjust from $P_{5}$ to $P_{95}$ of popliteal height. **Step 3 — Calculate $P_{5}$:** $$ P_{5} = 445 + (-1.645) \cdot 25 = 445 - 41.125 = 403.9 \text{ mm} $$ **Step 4 — Calculate $P_{95}$:** $$ P_{95} = 445 + (+1.645) \cdot 25 = 445 + 41.125 = 486.1 \text{ mm} $$ **Step 5 — Calculate the required adjustment range:** $$ L_{adj} = P_{95} - P_{5} = 486.1 - 403.9 = 82.2 \text{ mm} $$ **Step 6 — Specify the design with a 5% engineering safety margin:** $$ L_{adj, design} = 82.2 + (0.05 \times 82.2) = 82.2 + 4.1 = 86.3 \text{ mm} $$ **Step 7 — Final design specification:** > "The seat pan height shall be **adjustable from 404 mm to 487 mm** (≈ 410 mm – 490 mm rounded to ISO standards), giving a total adjustment of **~83 mm** which covers **90% of the target population**." **Step 8 — Add a small clearance (15–20 mm) for shoes:** $$ H_{chair, min} = P_5 + \text{heel height} = 404 + 20 = 424 \text{ mm} $$ $$ H_{chair, max} = P_{95} + \text{heel height} = 487 + 20 = 507 \text{ mm} $$ --- ## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Doorway Clearance for a Helmeted Worker (Dynamic Case) > [!NOTE] > **Problem:** A doorway in a confined industrial plant must allow passage of workers **wearing hard hats and carrying tools**. Static stature is $\mu = 1670$ mm, $\sigma = 70$ mm. The hard-hat adds 100 mm, the posture effect (head bowed slightly) reduces effective height by 30 mm, and an engineering margin of 50 mm is required. Determine the **minimum door height** that will allow 95% of workers to pass without ducking excessively. ### Solution — Step-by-Step **Step 1 — Use the 95th percentile (tall person = design-critical for clearance):** $$ P_{95, stature} = 1670 + 1.645 \cdot 70 = 1785 \text{ mm} $$ **Step 2 — Add hard-hat height:** $$ H_{hatted} = 1785 + 100 = 1885 \text{ mm} $$ **Step 3 — Subtract head-bow clearance loss:** $$ H_{effective} = 1885 - 30 = 1855 \text{ mm} $$ **Step 4 — Add engineering margin:** $$ H_{door, min} = 1855 + 50 = 1905 \text{ mm} $$ **Step 5 — Final specification:** **Round up to the next standard value, 2000 mm (2.0 m)** — which matches the standard industrial door height. --- ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Selecting the Correct Anthropometric Strategy > [!TIP] > **Master this matrix — KTU questions often ask "what percentile would you use for X"** | Design Situation | Critical User | Use Percentile | Justification | |---|---|---|---| | Minimum **legroom** in a car | Tallest person (long legs) | $P_{95}$ | The 95th-percentile leg length must fit | | Minimum **stature clearance** in a doorway | Tallest person | $P_{95}$ | Tallest person must not hit head | | Maximum **reach** to a fire alarm pull-station | Smallest person (short arm) | $P_{5}$ | The 5th-percentile arm must still reach | | Maximum **reach to a guarded area** (must NOT be reachable) | Largest person (long arm) | $P_{95}$ | The longest arm must not reach into danger | | **Seat pan height** for an office chair | Popliteal height | $P_{5}$ to $P_{95}$ | Adjustable range must cover both | | **Eye-line over a dashboard / hood** | Seated eye height | $P_{95}$ | Tallest driver must see over the hood | | **Pedal force** on a brake pedal | Weakest operator (or design for $P_{5}$ strength) | $P_{5}$ strength | 5% of population should still be able to brake | | **Width of an airline seat** | Broadest shoulder | $P_{95}$ | Tallest/broadest should fit (or be excluded) | | **Bicycle frame size** | Inseam length | $P_{5}$ to $P_{95}$ | Must fit both small and large riders | | **Helmet inner circumference** | Head circumference | $P_{5}$ to $P_{95}$ | Adjustable strap covers range | --- ## 3.5 Summary of the Mathematical Reasoning The fundamental statistical assumption used throughout anthropometric design is that **human body dimensions are approximately normally distributed within a homogeneous population** (same age group, same gender, same ethnicity, same occupational group). The percentile formula $$ P_k = \mu + z_k \cdot \sigma $$ is therefore the workhorse of all anthropometric calculations. The choice of $z_k$ depends on whether the design must accommodate a **lower** boundary ($z_5 = -1.645$), the **middle** ($z_{50} = 0$), or the **upper** ($z_{95} = +1.645$) of the population. The 90% coverage range is then $$ R_{90} = P_{95} - P_{5} = 2 \times 1.645 \times \sigma = 3.29 \, \sigma $$ and the design must provide an **adjustment range of at least $3.29 \sigma$** to cover 90% of the population. --- # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Flow Diagram — The Anthropometric Design Process ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Identify the User Population] --> B[Define the Critical Body Dimension] B --> C[Obtain Anthropometric Database] C --> D{Is Data Available?} D -- Yes --> E[Extract mean and standard deviation] D -- No --> F[Conduct Anthropometric Survey] F --> E E --> G[Compute 5th and 95th Percentile values] G --> H{Is Adjustable Design Feasible?} H -- Yes --> I[Design for Range 5th to 95th Percentile] H -- No --> J{Is it a Clearance or Reach Problem?} J -- Clearance --> K[Design for 95th Percentile extreme] J -- Reach --> L[Design for 5th Percentile extreme] I --> M[Define Adjustment Range R90 = P95 minus P5] K --> N[Add Engineering Margin] L --> N M --> N N --> O[Prototype and User-Test] O --> P[Final Anthropometric Design] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the diagram:** The flow begins with identifying who will use the product, then asks "is adjustability possible?". If **yes**, the design is sized for the **5th–95th percentile range** (covering 90% of users). If **no**, the designer must choose either a **clearance** criterion (use $P_{95}$) or a **reach** criterion (use $P_{5}$) and add an engineering safety margin. --- ## 4.2 Block Diagram — Branches of Anthropometry ```mermaid graph TD A[Anthropometry in Engineering Design] A --> B[Static Structural] A --> C[Dynamic Functional] B --> B1[Stature and Stature-related] B --> B2[Sitting Dimensions] B --> B3[Hand and Foot Dimensions] B --> B4[Head Dimensions] C --> C1[Reach Envelopes] C --> C2[Joint Range of Motion] C --> C3[Functional Clearances] C --> C4[Strength Measurements] C --> C5[Postural Dimensions] ``` --- ## 4.3 Schematic — The Seated Reference Posture (Block-Level Functional Architecture) The following Mermaid block diagram shows the **functional reference architecture** of a seated worker, used in KTU exam answers to label anthropometric landmarks: ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph SeatedReferencePosture SH[Sitting Height] --> EH[Eye Height Seated] SH --> SHO[Shoulder Height Seated] EH --> ST[Stature Trunk Axis] SHO --> AB[Acromion to Backrest] AB --> SEAT[Seat Pan Surface] SEAT --> PL[Popliteal Length] SEAT --> BK[Buttock to Knee Length] SEAT --> FT[Foot to Floor] SEAT --> TH[Thigh Clearance] SHO --> EF[Elbow to Fingertip] SHO --> EL[Elbow Height Seated] EL --> WS[Work Surface Reference Line] end ``` > [!NOTE] > **How to use this diagram in the exam:** Draw a simplified version, label each dimension with its KTU-standard name and abbreviation, and then list the **5th and 95th percentile values** in a small table beside the diagram. KTU examiners reward students who can annotate a drawing with the **mean and standard deviation** of each dimension. --- ## 4.4 Comparative Schematic — Static vs Dynamic Anthropometry ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Anthropometric Measurement] --> B{Is Subject Moving?} B -- No --> S[Static Anthropometry] B -- Yes --> D[Dynamic Anthropometry] S --> S1[Stature] S --> S2[Sitting Height] S --> S3[Hand Length] S --> S4[Head Circumference] D --> D1[Functional Reach Envelope] D --> D2[Joint Range of Motion] D --> D3[Body Volume in Motion] D --> D4[Clearance in Motion] D --> D5[Muscle Strength] ``` --- ## 4.5 Concept Map — Percentile-Based Design Decisions ```mermaid flowchart TD P[Percentile Design Decision] P --> P5[Use 5th Percentile] P --> P50[Use 50th Percentile] P --> P95[Use 95th Percentile] P5 --> P5A[Smallest person must reach] P5 --> P5B[Smallest person must fit through] P5 --> P5C[Smallest person can operate the control] P50 --> P50A[Average-size design - acceptable for non-critical items only] P95 --> P95A[Tallest person must fit through] P95 --> P95B[Longest arm must NOT reach into danger] P95 --> P95C[Strongest person must be able to resist accidental activation] ``` --- # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > [!NOTE] > **Format:** KTU Part A expects a **definition in 2–3 lines** + a **one-sentence application** + (where applicable) a **formula**. ### Question 1 — Define Static and Dynamic Anthropometry. Give two examples of dimensions measured under each. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model QP]` **Model Answer:** **Static Anthropometry** is the measurement of the human body in **fixed, standardised, motionless postures** (e.g., standing erect or sitting erect). Examples: (i) **stature** (vertex-to-floor height), (ii) **sitting height** (vertex-to-seat surface), (iii) **elbow height when seated**, (iv) **hand length**, (v) **head circumference**. **Dynamic Anthropometry** is the measurement of the human body **during motion, work, or under load**. Examples: (i) **functional forward reach** of the hand while seated, (ii) **squatting height** (vertical clearance required to pass through a hatch), (iii) **grip strength**, (iv) **joint range of motion** (e.g., shoulder flexion angle), (v) **body depth in a driving posture**. **Application:** Static data is used for designing **fixed-size furniture and clearances**; dynamic data is essential for **operator cabins, escape routes, and equipment that the body must move within**. --- ### Question 2 — Explain the significance of the 5th and 95th percentiles in anthropometric design with one example each. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **Model Answer:** The **5th percentile** ($z = -1.645$) is the value below which only **5% of the population** lies. It is used as the **design boundary for reach and minimum-strength** requirements — i.e., the **smallest/weakest** user must be able to reach a critical control. *Example:* The 5th-percentile **functional reach** sets the maximum horizontal distance of an emergency stop button from a seated operator. The **95th percentile** ($z = +1.645$) is the value below which **95% of the population** lies. It is used as the **design boundary for clearance and maximum-strength** requirements — i.e., the **largest/strongest** user must fit through an opening or not accidentally activate a control. *Example:* The 95th-percentile **seated eye height** sets the minimum height of a vehicle's windscreen header so that the tallest driver can see the road clearly. The **range $P_{95} - P_{5}$** covers the **middle 90% of the user population** and is the recommended accommodation target in KTU design philosophy. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** The most common marking loss in Module 3 answers is **failing to justify the choice of percentile** with the correct reason. A statement like "use the 95th percentile for door height" without saying *"because the tallest person is the design-critical case for vertical clearance"* will lose **1–2 marks** immediately. Always justify with the **"tallest/shortest/strongest/weakest"** reasoning. --- ### Question A (14 Marks) — Full-Length Model Question > **"[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model QP]"** > > **(a) [7 marks]** Define **anthropometry** and explain with sketches the difference between **static and dynamic anthropometry**. List **at least six** body dimensions used in static anthropometry, and **at least four** body dimensions used in dynamic anthropometry. State clearly **which engineering design problem** each dimension is used to solve. > > **(b) [7 marks]** For a population of industrial workers, the **functional forward reach** (seated) is normally distributed with **mean $\mu = 750$ mm** and **standard deviation $\sigma = 50$ mm**. An emergency stop button is to be placed on a fixed console at a horizontal distance of $D$ mm from the operator's shoulder. The design must be usable by **90% of the population** while seated. Calculate: **(i)** the **maximum distance $D_{max}$** at which the button can be placed so that the **smallest 5% of the population** can still reach it, **(ii)** the **minimum distance $D_{min}$** such that the **largest 5% of the population** cannot accidentally activate it, and **(iii)** the required **adjustment range** of the button if the console is repositionable. State all design assumptions. --- ### Model Answer — Question A #### Part (a) — 7 marks **Definition (1.5 marks):** Anthropometry is the science of measuring human body dimensions, strengths, and ranges of motion to provide the **quantitative data base** for ergonomic design of tools, equipment, workstations, and environments. **Static vs Dynamic (2.5 marks):** | Aspect | Static (Structural) Anthropometry | Dynamic (Functional) Anthropometry | |---|---|---| | Posture | Body held in fixed, standardised pose | Body in motion or under load | | Quantity measured | Lengths, breadths, depths, circumferences | Reach envelopes, joint angles, body clearance in motion, strengths | | Standardisation | High (ISO 7250, IS 9474) | Lower (depends on task) | | Example | Stature, sitting height, hand length | Functional reach, shoulder flexion angle, grip strength | **Six static dimensions (1.5 marks):** 1. **Stature** — used for **doorway height, vehicle roof height, escape-hatch clearance**. 2. **Sitting height** — used for **seat-back height, cabin headroom**. 3. **Elbow height (seated)** — used for **work-surface and armrest height**. 4. **Popliteal length** — used for **chair seat-pan height**. 5. **Hand length** — used for **glove sizing, handle length**. 6. **Head circumference** — used for **helmet sizing, hat sizing**. **Four dynamic dimensions (1.5 marks):** 1. **Functional forward reach (seated)** — used for **maximum reach of controls in a fixed workstation**. 2. **Shoulder flexion angle (max)** — used for **overhead reach and overhead guard clearance**. 3. **Squatting height** — used for **tunnel and crawl-space minimum height**. 4. **Grip strength** — used for **maximum allowable force on hand-tools to prevent RSI**. --- #### Part (b) — 7 marks **Assumptions (0.5 marks):** - Distribution is **normal**. - Population is **homogeneous** (one occupational group, one gender or mixed). - The reach is measured from the **shoulder**; the button is at **shoulder height** (no vertical offset). - 90% accommodation is targeted. **Given:** $\mu = 750$ mm, $\sigma = 50$ mm, $z_5 = -1.645$, $z_{95} = +1.645$. **(i) Maximum distance $D_{max}$ for the smallest 5% to reach — Use $P_5$ (1.5 marks):** $$ D_{max} = P_5 = \mu + z_5 \cdot \sigma = 750 + (-1.645)(50) $$ $$ D_{max} = 750 - 82.25 = 667.75 \text{ mm} $$ **Conclusion:** The button must be placed **no farther than ≈ 668 mm from the operator's shoulder** to be reachable by the 5th-percentile (shortest reach) worker. **(ii) Minimum distance $D_{min}$ so that the largest 5% cannot accidentally reach it — Use $P_{95}$ (1.5 marks):** $$ D_{min} = P_{95} = \mu + z_{95} \cdot \sigma = 750 + (+1.645)(50) $$ $$ D_{min} = 750 + 82.25 = 832.25 \text{ mm} $$ **Conclusion:** The button must be placed **at least ≈ 832 mm from the operator's shoulder** so that the 5th-percentile (longest reach) worker cannot accidentally activate it. **(iii) Required adjustment range (1.5 marks):** $$ L_{adj} = P_{95} - P_{5} = 832.25 - 667.75 = 164.5 \text{ mm} $$ **Or, equivalently:** $$ L_{adj} = 3.29 \cdot \sigma = 3.29 \cdot 50 = 164.5 \text{ mm} $$ **Final design recommendation (2 marks):** - If the button is at a **fixed position**, place it at **$D \le 668$ mm** from the shoulder (using $P_5$ reach) so the smallest user can reach. - If the button can be **repositioned for different users**, provide an **adjustment range of at least 165 mm** centred on the 50th percentile (≈ 668 mm to 833 mm), and a safety margin of **10–20 mm** at each end. - Add a **vertical offset tolerance of ± 50 mm** for seated-operator posture variation. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — Internal-Choice Alternative > **"[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model QP]"** > > **(a) [7 marks]** With the help of a **neat labelled sketch**, describe the **principles of anthropometric design** as applied to an **operator workstation / control desk**. Explain the **three classical design strategies** (design for average, design for range, design for extreme) and state, with **two examples each**, when each is preferred. > > **(b) [7 marks]** A public-transport **bus doorway** is to be designed for the **boarding population**. The stature of the user population is normally distributed with **mean $\mu = 1650$ mm** and **standard deviation $\sigma = 75$ mm**. **(i)** Compute the **5th and 95th percentile** of stature. **(ii)** What **minimum door height** will allow 95% of the population to pass without contact, given a **footwear allowance of 30 mm** and an **engineering safety margin of 50 mm**? **(iii)** Comment on whether the design should be **adjustable or fixed** for a public bus, and justify your answer in **two lines**. --- ### Model Answer — Question B (Brief) **(a) [7 marks]:** Discuss McFarland / Grandjean's three strategies with examples (office chair = adjust; door = extreme; cap = average). Sketch a workstation with **stature, eye height, elbow height, reach envelope** annotated. **(b) (i) [2 marks]:** $$ P_5 = 1650 + (-1.645)(75) = 1650 - 123.4 = 1526.6 \text{ mm} $$ $$ P_{95} = 1650 + (+1.645)(75) = 1650 + 123.4 = 1773.4 \text{ mm} $$ **(b) (ii) [3 marks]:** Use 95th percentile as the design-critical case (tallest person). $$ H_{door,min} = P_{95} + \text{footwear} + \text{margin} $$ $$ H_{door,min} = 1773.4 + 30 + 50 = 1853.4 \text{ mm} $$ Round up to **standard 1900 mm** (matches the standard **IS 14684 / AIS-052** public-bus doorway dimension). **(b) (iii) [2 marks]:** The design is **fixed** (not adjustable) for a public bus because **(a)** buses must be **mass-produced at low cost**, **(b)** doorways cannot be adjusted per passenger, and **(c)** the consequence of slight discomfort is trivial. The fixed dimension covers the **5th–95th percentile** (90% of passengers) — the remaining 10% are accepted as marginal users (slightly duck or tilt head). --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > [!TIP] > **Print this section and read it 5 times the night before the exam.** - [ ] **Anthropometry** = science of measuring body dimensions; **static** = still body, **dynamic** = body in motion. - [ ] **Static dimensions** (use in KTU): stature, sitting height, eye height, shoulder height, elbow height, popliteal length, hand length, head circumference. - [ ] **Dynamic dimensions**: functional reach envelope, joint range of motion, body clearance in motion, muscle strength, working posture dimensions. - [ ] **Percentile formula** (most important equation in the module): $\,P_k = \mu + z_k \cdot \sigma\,$. - [ ] **z-values to memorise**: $z_5 = -1.645$, $z_{50} = 0.000$, $z_{95} = +1.645$. - [ ] **90% range formula**: $\,R_{90} = P_{95} - P_5 = 3.29 \, \sigma\,$. - [ ] **Three design strategies**: design for **average** (mean), design for **range** (5th–95th, adjustable), design for **extreme** (one percentile only). - [ ] **5th percentile** → for **reach and minimum-strength** problems (smallest/weakest user). - [ ] **95th percentile** → for **clearance, maximum-strength, and accidental-activation** problems (largest/strongest user). - [ ] **Adjustable design** is always preferred in KTU when feasible. - [ ] **Indian / KTU population data** typically uses $\sigma \approx 5$–$8$% of the mean for body dimensions. - [ ] **Adjustability range** = $P_{95} - P_5$; **centred design** places midpoint at $P_{50}$. - [ ] **Engineering margin** of $5$–$10$% is added to all final dimensions. - [ ] **Standard reference posture** for static data: standing erect with head in the **Frankfurt plane** (orbitomeatal line horizontal). - [ ] **Case study** in KTU typically uses: office chair, driver cabin, bus doorway, operator console, or escape hatch. - [ ] **Common KTU pitfall**: using the **wrong percentile**. Always ask: *"Who is the design-critical user?"* — the **smallest** (for reach) or the **largest** (for clearance)? ---

topics.title: Viewing Angle Determination of Cardiac Output Monitor; Work Place and Seating Design
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT742 - HUMAN FACTORS IN ENGINEERING AND DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Viewing Angle Determination of Cardiac Output Monitor; Work Place and Seating Design

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Viewing Angle Determination – Cardiac Output Monitor (COM)

**Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
The **Viewing Angle** of a Cardiac Output Monitor (COM) is the angle subtended at the observer's eye between the *line of sight to the centre of the display* and a *reference datum* (usually the horizontal eye level or the normal to the display surface). In clinical human-factors engineering, this angle is *deliberately designed* so that clinicians (nurses, anaesthetists, intensivists) can read numerical waveforms — such as cardiac output (CO), stroke volume (SV), systemic vascular resistance (SVR) and central venous pressure (CVP) — without inducing **musculoskeletal fatigue, ocular strain, or alarm-fatigue errors**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Cardiac Output Monitors are *critical-care displays* (IEC 60601-1-6 / ISO 9241-300 family of ergonomics standards). They are not ordinary computer monitors. Their viewing angle is determined by the **combined geometric relationship between the operator's eye, the display panel, and the patient's bedside environment** — *not* by the LCD/LED panel's own wide-view property.

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
Imagine you are reading a book on a desk. If the book is placed flat on the table, you have to bend your neck down 60° — your neck aches within minutes. If you tilt the book 30° toward you (using a book stand), the reading becomes effortless. Now imagine the "book" is a *life-critical monitor* showing a patient's heart output in L/min — a wrong reading could cause a wrong drug dosage. The "tilt angle" you choose is the **viewing angle**. For a COM, the angle is engineered not only for comfort but for **error-free, instantaneous waveform and numeric recognition** during high-stress situations.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Reference Values in KTU Context (in bold):**
> - Recommended **viewing distance** for medical displays: **50 cm – 70 cm** (≈ 20–28 inches).
> - Recommended **vertical viewing angle** (line of sight below horizontal): **15° – 30°** for seated viewing; **0° – 15°** for standing.
> - Recommended **horizontal viewing angle** (azimuth from normal to screen): within **± 30°** for primary use, **± 50°** for ambient glances.
> - **Preferred line-of-sight (ANSI/HFES 100):** **15° below horizontal eye level** (preferred visual axis).

---

### 1.2 Workplace and Seating Design

**Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
**Workplace Design** is the systematic application of *anthropometric, biomechanical, cognitive, and environmental* data to configure the physical layout of a workstation — its **seat, work surface, foot-rest, monitor, keyboard, lighting, and clearance envelope** — so that the operator can sustain *productive, safe, and healthy* postures across the working population (typically 5th–95th percentile users).

**Seating Design** is the *sub-discipline* that engineers the **seat pan, backrest, armrests, base, and adjustment mechanisms** of a chair to support the **neutral spine posture**, distribute **interface pressure** on buttocks and thighs, and provide **postural stability** over prolonged sitting (≥ 2 hours continuous).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** "Design for the *middle*, accommodate the *extremes*." Workplace dimensions are set using the **50th percentile (median)** anthropometry, with adjustability ranges covering the **5th to 95th percentile** population. Seats are *not* one-size-fits-all.

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
Think of a classroom bench. If it is built for the *tallest* student, the shortest student dangles his legs, putting pressure on the back of the thighs and cutting blood flow. If it is built for the *shortest* student, the tallest student's knees bang the underside of the desk. A **properly designed seat is a *compromise* — the seat pan height is set at the *5th-percentile popliteal height + shoe heel* (so short users' feet touch the floor), and the work-surface height is set at the *95th-percentile elbow height* (so tall users don't have to shrug their shoulders).** That is the **"5th–95th accommodation rule"** of ergonomic seating.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Anthropometric Anchors (in bold):**
> - **Popliteal Height** (5th–95th %ile, Indian male): **≈ 40 cm – 51 cm** — sets seat height.
> - **Buttock-Popliteal Length** (seat depth reference): **≈ 44 cm – 52 cm**.
> - **Elbow Height (sitting):** **≈ 60 cm – 75 cm** — sets work-surface height.
> - **Eye Height (sitting):** **≈ 72 cm – 86 cm** — sets monitor top edge.
> - **Lumbar Lordosis Location:** **≈ 20 cm – 25 cm** above the seat pan — sets lumbar support.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Framework – Viewing Angle of a Cardiac Output Monitor

The viewing angle of a COM is determined by solving a **simple plane-geometry problem** with three reference points:

1. **E** — the observer's eye (cyclopean eye at the bridge of the nose).
2. **D** — the centre of the display (the "primary viewing target").
3. **H** — the horizontal datum passing through E, parallel to the floor.

The angles computed are:

| Angle | Symbol | Definition | Typical KTU Design Value |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Vertical Viewing Angle** | $\theta_v$ | Angle between line $ED$ and horizontal $EH$ (downward) | **15° – 30°** |
| **Horizontal Viewing Angle** | $\theta_h$ | Angle between line $ED$ and the normal to display surface | **≤ 30°** (primary) |
| **Tilt Angle of Display** | $\phi$ | Angle of display panel from vertical | **≈ 0° – 15°** backward |
| **Line-of-Sight Angle** | $\alpha$ | Angle of *primary line of sight* below horizontal | **≈ 15°** (preferred) |

**Operational Logic (Why & How):**

- **Why 15° below horizontal?** The eye's *preferred line of sight* (when the head is held in a neutral, balanced posture on the cervical spine) is naturally **15° below the true horizontal** — this is the *gravitational resting position* of the extraocular muscles.
- **Why ± 30° horizontal limit?** Beyond ± 30° azimuth from the display normal, the **luminance contrast** of standard LCD/LED panels drops by ~50%, causing **display-induced ambiguity in numerical readings** — unacceptable in critical care.
- **Why a backward tilt (φ > 0°)?** Tilting the top of the monitor *away* from the operator makes the *line $ED$* more nearly *perpendicular* to the screen, improving legibility, reducing specular glare from ceiling lights, and lowering the required vertical viewing angle $\theta_v$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** The same geometric principle is used to design **aircraft cockpit displays, railway LDC driver desks, vehicle infotainment screens, and process-control DCS panels** — making this a *transferable* human-factors skill.

---

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING RULE:** All absolute-value and similar notations use `\vert` or `\mid` — never the raw pipe `|` — to preserve markdown table integrity.

| # | Concept | Formula / Relation | Variables & Units | KTU Boundary / Typical Value |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Vertical Viewing Angle** | $\theta_v = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{H_e - H_d}{D_{ed}}\right)$ | $H_e$ = eye height above floor (m); $H_d$ = display-centre height above floor (m); $D_{ed}$ = horizontal eye-to-display distance (m) | $\theta_v = 15° \text{ to } 30°$ |
| 2 | **Horizontal Viewing Angle** | $\theta_h = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{L_{off}}{D_{ed}}\right)$ | $L_{off}$ = lateral offset of operator from display-normal (m) | $\theta_h \le 30°$ (primary) |
| 3 | **Effective Line-of-Sight Distance** | $D_{los} = \sqrt{(H_e - H_d)^2 + D_{ed}^2 + L_{off}^2}$ | All distances in metres (m) | $D_{los} = 0.5 \text{ m – } 0.7 \text{ m}$ (medical) |
| 4 | **Display Tilt Compensation** | $\phi = \theta_v - \theta_{pref}$ | $\theta_{pref}$ = preferred line-of-sight angle = **15°** | $\phi = 0° \text{ to } 15°$ |
| 5 | **Seat Height (Popliteal-based)** | $H_{seat} = H_{popliteal,5\%} + h_{shoe}$ | $H_{popliteal,5\%}$ = 5th-percentile popliteal height; $h_{shoe}$ = heel allowance (≈ 2.5 cm) | Indian male: **40.0 cm – 51.0 cm** |
| 6 | **Seat Depth (Pan Depth)** | $D_{seat} = L_{buttock\text{-}popliteal,95\%} - \text{clearance}$ | Clearance from front edge to popliteal fossa ≈ **2 cm – 5 cm** | **38 cm – 46 cm** |
| 7 | **Seat Width** | $W_{seat} = B_{hip,95\%} + \text{lateral clearance}$ | Lateral clearance ≈ **2.5 cm per side** | **≥ 45 cm** |
| 8 | **Work-Surface Height (Standing)** | $H_{ws,stand} = H_{elbow,95\%,stand} - h_{clearance}$ | $h_{clearance}$ = **5 cm – 10 cm** for clearance over work surface | **≈ 100 cm – 110 cm** |
| 9 | **Work-Surface Height (Sitting)** | $H_{ws,sit} = H_{elbow,50\%,sit} + h_{work}$ | $h_{work}$ = allowance for task type: **precision** → 5 cm; **light** → 0; **heavy** → 5–10 cm *below* elbow | **≈ 70 cm – 75 cm** |
| 10 | **Backrest Height (Lumbar Top)** | $H_{lumbar,top} = 0.20 \text{ to } 0.25 \cdot H_{seat} \text{ above seat pan}$ | Lumbar pad height above seat pan | **20 cm – 25 cm** |
| 11 | **Backrest Inclination** | $\beta = 95° \text{ to } 110°$ (recline from vertical) | $\beta$ = angle between seat pan and backrest | **≈ 105°** (task) ; **110° – 115°** (relax) |
| 12 | **Seat-Pan Tilt** | $\gamma = 0° \text{ to } 10°$ (rearward) | Positive γ = front of seat higher | **≈ 5°** rearward (anti-slide) |
| 13 | **Monitor Top Edge Height (Sitting)** | $H_{mon,top} \le H_{eye,95\%,sit} - 0$ | Display top at or just below 95th-percentile eye height | **≤ 86 cm** (Indian) |
| 14 | **Critical Display Luminance** | $L_d = L_{amb} \cdot R_{CR}$ | $R_{CR}$ = contrast ratio (min **3:1** for waveforms, **7:1** for text) | **ANSI/AAMI HE75** |
| 15 | **Vertical Eye–Display Offset (for COM)** | $\Delta H = H_e - H_d$ | Must be positive (display below eye) for $\theta_v > 0$ | **≈ 10 cm – 25 cm** |

**Real-World Engineering Utility:**

- These equations are used in **anaesthesia workstation layout** (Drager, GE Aisys, Mindray A-series), **ICU pendant-arm positioning** (Hillrom, Stryker), **Cockpit display placement** (Boeing 787, Airbus A350), **CNC operator console design**, and **BPO workstation certification** (ISO 9241-5).
- A **mis-designed viewing angle** causes the "ICU nurse syndrome" — chronic neck flexion, trapezius myalgia, and *display-misread medication errors* documented in JCAHO root-cause analyses.

---

### 2.3 Theoretical Framework – Workplace and Seating Design Principles

A properly designed chair and workstation must satisfy **five simultaneous ergonomic requirements**:

1. **Static Anthropometric Fit** — dimensions accommodate the 5th–95th percentile population.
2. **Dynamic Postural Support** — chair permits *postural variation* (micro-movements every 7–10 minutes) to prevent **static-load back pain**.
3. **Pressure Distribution** — interface pressure on the **ischial tuberosities (IT)** and **posterior thighs** must be **< 4.5 kPa** to prevent capillary occlusion.
4. **Stability & Reach** — the **functional reach envelope** (≈ 60 cm radius for women, 65 cm for men) must lie entirely within the work-surface boundary.
5. **Adjustability** — at least **3 dimensions** of adjustment: **seat height, backrest height, backrest tilt** (minimum per ISO 9241-5).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Important Note:** The exam frequently tests the *relationship between seat-pan tilt and lumbar lordosis*. A **horizontal or forward-tilted seat pan (γ < 0°)** is recommended for **forward-tilted task work** (e.g., microscope use, dental work) because it preserves lumbar lordosis. A **rearward-tilted pan (γ > 0°)** is preferred for **reclined task work** (e.g., control-room monitoring — *exactly the cardiac-output-monitor case!*).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Vertical Viewing Angle of a Cardiac Output Monitor

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Setup (Typical KTU Numerical):** A cardiac anaesthetist is seated in the ICU at an eye height of $H_e = 125$ cm from the floor. The COM is mounted on a wall bracket such that the centre of the screen is at $H_d = 110$ cm from the floor. The horizontal distance from the anaesthetist's eye to the wall (and hence to the display) is $D_{ed} = 90$ cm. Determine (a) the vertical viewing angle $\theta_v$, and (b) the recommended tilt of the monitor $\phi$ for optimal viewing.

#### Step 1 — Write the geometric definition

The vertical viewing angle is defined as the angle subtended between the **horizontal datum through the eye** and the **line of sight to the display centre**, measured in the vertical plane.

$$
\theta_v = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{\Delta H}{D_{ed}}\right)
$$

where $\Delta H = H_e - H_d$ is the vertical drop from the eye to the display centre.

#### Step 2 — Substitute the numerical values

$$
\Delta H = H_e - H_d = 125 \text{ cm} - 110 \text{ cm} = 15 \text{ cm}
$$

$$
D_{ed} = 90 \text{ cm}
$$

$$
\theta_v = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{15 \text{ cm}}{90 \text{ cm}}\right) = \tan^{-1}(0.1667)
$$

#### Step 3 — Evaluate the inverse tangent

$$
\theta_v = 9.46° \approx 9.5°
$$

#### Step 4 — Compare with the preferred line-of-sight

The preferred (ANSI/HFES 100) line-of-sight is **15° below horizontal**. The *required* tilt correction is:

$$
\phi = \theta_{pref} - \theta_v = 15° - 9.46° = 5.54° \approx 5.5°
$$

The display must be tilted **backward by ≈ 5.5°** (top of monitor away from operator) so that the *actual* line of sight to the centre of the screen equals the 15° preferred angle.

#### Step 5 — Engineering interpretation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The current configuration puts the screen at 9.5° below the eye — *too high* (too close to eye level). The anaesthetist would have to lift his gaze, causing **cervical extension** and eye strain. Tilting the monitor backward by 5.5° *geometrically lowers* the screen relative to the eye's preferred line of sight, restoring comfort.

**Analytical Decision Matrix:**

| Computed $\theta_v$ | Status | Recommended Action |
|---|---|---|
| $\theta_v < 10°$ | Display too high | Tilt monitor back by $\phi = 15° - \theta_v$ |
| $10° \le \theta_v \le 30°$ | **Optimum** | No tilt needed |
| $\theta_v > 30°$ | Display too low (severe neck flexion) | Raise mount by $(\theta_v - 15°)\cdot D_{ed}$ |

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Required Monitor Lift to Achieve $\theta_v = 20°$ (Best Cardiac-Output-Monitor Practice)

#### Step 1 — Re-state the formula, solve for $\Delta H$

$$
\theta_v = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{\Delta H}{D_{ed}}\right) \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \Delta H = D_{ed} \cdot \tan(\theta_v)
$$

#### Step 2 — Substitute the target angle and known distance

Let the target be $\theta_v = 20°$ (good practice for sustained monitoring) and $D_{ed} = 80$ cm (closer ICU position).

$$
\Delta H = 80 \text{ cm} \cdot \tan(20°)
$$

#### Step 3 — Evaluate

$$
\tan(20°) = 0.3640
$$

$$
\Delta H = 80 \cdot 0.3640 = 29.12 \text{ cm}
$$

#### Step 4 — Compute the required display-centre height

If the seated eye height is $H_e = 122$ cm, then:

$$
H_d = H_e - \Delta H = 122 - 29.12 = 92.88 \text{ cm} \approx 93 \text{ cm}
$$

#### Step 5 — Sanity check

$$
\theta_v = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{122 - 93}{80}\right) = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{29}{80}\right) = \tan^{-1}(0.3625) = 19.94° \approx 20° \;\; \checkmark
$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Seat Height Using 5th-Percentile Popliteal Height (Indian Anthropometry, NFHS-5 / IS 5950)

#### Step 1 — State the relationship

$$
H_{seat} = H_{popliteal,5\%} + h_{shoe}
$$

#### Step 2 — Plug in standard Indian male 5th-percentile value

For Indian male adults (5th percentile): $H_{popliteal,5\%} = 40.0$ cm. Standard shoe-heel allowance $h_{shoe} = 2.5$ cm.

$$
H_{seat} = 40.0 + 2.5 = 42.5 \text{ cm}
$$

#### Step 3 — Adjustment range for 95th percentile

For Indian male 95th percentile: $H_{popliteal,95\%} = 51.0$ cm. The same chair with the same seat pan must *clear* this dimension — the chair must offer a *pneumatic height adjustment* of at least:

$$
\Delta H_{adj} = 51.0 - 40.0 = 11.0 \text{ cm}
$$

plus a small overshoot, say **15 cm of total adjustment range** (from 38 cm to 53 cm) — to accommodate both genders and shoe variations.

#### Step 4 — Seat depth

For Indian female 95th percentile buttock-popliteal length ≈ 53 cm. Subtract a 5 cm popliteal clearance:

$$
D_{seat} = 53 - 5 = 48 \text{ cm}
$$

But most office chairs use **38 cm – 46 cm** as the practical depth range — this *shortens* the seat by ~5 cm to avoid pressure behind the knees of shorter users.

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Work-Surface Height for Cardiac ICU Nurse (Sitting, Light-precision Task)

#### Step 1 — Choose the population reference

Use **50th-percentile sitting elbow height** for Indian female (the more frequent nursing demographic in KTU case studies): $H_{elbow,50\%,sit} = 63$ cm.

#### Step 2 — Add task allowance

For *light-precision* task (reading COM waveforms, adjusting drips): $h_{work} = +2$ cm to +5 cm above elbow.

$$
H_{ws} = 63 + 3 = 66 \text{ cm (lower bound)} \;\;\text{to}\;\; 63 + 5 = 68 \text{ cm (upper bound)}
$$

#### Step 3 — Adjustability requirement

The 5th-percentile Indian female elbow height (sitting) is ≈ 58 cm; the 95th-percentile is ≈ 70 cm. Required range:

$$
\Delta H_{ws,adj} = 70 - 58 = 12 \text{ cm} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \text{Provide at least 15 cm of adjustment}
$$

#### Step 4 — Cross-check against the monitor location

Given the work surface at 66 cm and the seated eye at 122 cm, the COM is mounted on an *overhead arm* so that the **display centre** is at 122 − 29 ≈ 93 cm (from Derivation 2). This positions the COM *27 cm above* the work surface — a safe clearance for keyboard and IV-pole shadows.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **Answer Style:** 1–2 line crisp definition + 1 bullet of applied/clinical value. KTU valuation gives 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for supporting fact, 1 mark for *application* (e.g., a standard or example).

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model QP Set B]**
Define the term **viewing angle** as applied to a Cardiac Output Monitor. State the recommended range of vertical viewing angle for sustained critical-care monitoring.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- The **viewing angle** of a COM is the angle between the observer's **line of sight to the centre of the display** and a reference datum, usually the horizontal through the eye (or the normal to the display). *(1 mark)*
- The recommended **vertical viewing angle** for sustained monitoring is **15° – 30° below the horizontal eye level** *(1 mark)* — this aligns with the **preferred line of sight (15°)** from ANSI/HFES 100 and accommodates operator postural variation up to 30° without inducing neck flexion injury. *(1 mark)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Supplementary QP]**
List **any three** key anthropometric dimensions used in workplace and seating design, and state the typical Indian 5th-percentile value for each.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Popliteal Height** (Indian male 5th %ile) = **≈ 40.0 cm** — sets *minimum seat height*. *(1 mark)*
- **Sitting Elbow Height** (Indian female 5th %ile) = **≈ 58 cm** — sets *minimum work-surface height*. *(1 mark)*
- **Buttock-Popliteal Length** (Indian female 5th %ile) = **≈ 44 cm** — sets *maximum seat depth*. *(1 mark)*
- (Any equivalent 3 dimensions accepted, e.g., hip breadth, eye height, lumbar location.)

---

### 5.2 Part B — Essay / Numerical Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Part B = 14 marks; sub-parts typically (a) = 7 marks + (b) = 7 marks. Students answer **either OR** (Choice).

---

#### **Question A (14 marks) — Viewing Angle of a Cardiac Output Monitor**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Modified Past Paper Pattern]**

> An ICU anaesthetist is seated in front of a Cardiac Output Monitor (COM) mounted on a swing-arm bracket at the bedside of a post-operative cardiac surgery patient. The following geometric data is recorded by the hospital's human-factors audit team:
>
> - Eye height of the seated anaesthetist, $H_e = 124$ cm above the floor.
> - Vertical height of the display centre, $H_d = 108$ cm above the floor.
> - Horizontal distance from the anaesthetist's eye to the wall (and hence the display), $D_{ed} = 100$ cm.
> - Lateral offset of the anaesthetist from the display-normal, $L_{off} = 35$ cm.
>
> **Answer the following:**

**(a)** Calculate the **vertical viewing angle** $\theta_v$ and the **horizontal viewing angle** $\theta_h$. Comment on whether the current setup satisfies the KTU/ANSI recommended ranges. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** If the vertical viewing angle must be brought to the **preferred value of 20°** for sustained monitoring, calculate (i) the **required vertical drop** $\Delta H$ from eye to display centre, and (ii) the **new display-centre height** $H_d^{new}$. Also compute the **line-of-sight distance** $D_{los}$ in the corrected setup. *(7 marks)*

---

**Model Solution (Question A):**

**Part (a) — Calculation of $\theta_v$ and $\theta_h$**

**Step 1 — Vertical drop calculation:**

$$
\Delta H = H_e - H_d = 124 - 108 = 16 \text{ cm}
$$

**Step 2 — Vertical viewing angle:**

$$
\theta_v = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{\Delta H}{D_{ed}}\right) = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{16}{100}\right) = \tan^{-1}(0.16) = 9.09° \approx 9.1°
$$

**Step 3 — Horizontal viewing angle:**

$$
\theta_h = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{L_{off}}{D_{ed}}\right) = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{35}{100}\right) = \tan^{-1}(0.35) = 19.29° \approx 19.3°
$$

**Step 4 — Comparison with standards:**

| Angle | Computed | Recommended (KTU/ANSI) | Status |
|---|---|---|---|
| $\theta_v$ | **9.1°** | 15° – 30° | ❌ **Too small** (display too close to eye level) |
| $\theta_h$ | **19.3°** | ≤ 30° (primary) | ✅ **Acceptable** |

**Conclusion for (a):** The horizontal angle is within tolerance but the vertical angle is *below* the recommended range — the anaesthetist will experience **cervical extension (head tipped back)**. *Recommended correction follows in part (b).* *(2 marks for tabular analysis + 1 mark for conclusion)*

---

**Part (b) — Correcting $\theta_v$ to 20° and computing $D_{los}$**

**Step 1 — Required vertical drop for $\theta_v = 20°$:**

$$
\Delta H_{req} = D_{ed} \cdot \tan(20°) = 100 \cdot 0.3640 = 36.40 \text{ cm}
$$

**Step 2 — New display-centre height:**

$$
H_d^{new} = H_e - \Delta H_{req} = 124 - 36.40 = 87.60 \text{ cm}
$$

**Step 3 — Physical interpretation:**

The display centre must be **lowered by 108 − 87.6 = 20.4 cm** — achievable by either (i) re-mounting the swing-arm 20.4 cm lower, or (ii) tilting the panel backward by $\phi = 20° - 9.1° = 10.9° \approx 11°$ (acceptable range 0°–15°).

**Step 4 — Line-of-sight distance in the corrected setup:**

$$
D_{los} = \sqrt{(\Delta H_{req})^2 + D_{ed}^2 + L_{off}^2}
$$

$$
D_{los} = \sqrt{(36.40)^2 + (100)^2 + (35)^2} \text{ cm}
$$

$$
D_{los} = \sqrt{1324.96 + 10000 + 1225} = \sqrt{12549.96}
$$

$$
D_{los} = 112.03 \text{ cm} \approx 1.12 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 5 — Sanity check:** Medical-display recommended viewing distance is **50 – 70 cm** for primary reading; the computed $D_{los} = 112$ cm is *outside* this range, suggesting the anaesthetist should **move the chair 12 cm closer** to bring $D_{los}$ into the recommended band. *(1 mark for the trade-off note)*

---

#### **Question B (14 marks) — Workplace and Seating Design**

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Modified Past Paper Pattern]**

> A new 24-station **Cardiac Monitoring Control Room** is being designed for a tertiary-care hospital. The central monitoring nurse will be seated for 8-hour shifts, viewing up to 24 bedside Cardiac Output Monitors displayed on a large video wall. The human-factors consultant has obtained the following **Indian 5th–95th percentile anthropometric data** for the nursing population (mixed gender):
>
> | Dimension | 5th %ile (cm) | 95th %ile (cm) |
> |---|---|---|
> | Popliteal Height (sitting) | 38.0 | 50.0 |
> | Buttock-Popliteal Length | 42.0 | 52.0 |
> | Hip Breadth (sitting) | 36.0 | 48.0 |
> | Elbow Height (sitting) | 58.0 | 70.0 |
> | Eye Height (sitting) | 70.0 | 84.0 |
> | Thigh Clearance (sitting) | 13.0 | 17.0 |
>
> **Answer the following:**

**(a)** Design the **seat dimensions** — namely (i) seat height, (ii) seat depth, (iii) seat width — including the required **adjustment range** for each. Justify each choice with the relevant anthropometric anchor. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Design the **work surface and backrest geometry**, including (i) work-surface height for sitting, (ii) backrest lumbar support height, (iii) backrest inclination, (iv) seat-pan tilt. State the **minimum adjustability** of the workstation. *(7 marks)*

---

**Model Solution (Question B):**

**Part (a) — Seat Dimensions**

**(i) Seat Height:**

$$
H_{seat} = H_{popliteal,5\%} + h_{shoe} = 38.0 + 2.5 = 40.5 \text{ cm}
$$

For the 95th percentile (50.0 cm popliteal), the chair must adjust *up* to **50.0 + 2.5 = 52.5 cm**. So the **adjustment range** is **40.5 cm – 52.5 cm**, or a **12 cm travel** (round up to **15 cm**). *(2 marks)*

**(ii) Seat Depth:**

The seat must *not* press into the popliteal fossa. Use 95th-percentile buttock-popliteal length minus 5 cm clearance:

$$
D_{seat} = 52.0 - 5.0 = 47.0 \text{ cm} \;\; \text{(upper practical limit for the largest user)}
$$

For the 5th percentile, this 47 cm would be too deep (knees cannot bend). Therefore provide **seat-depth adjustment** (sliding seat pan) of:

$$
D_{seat,adj} = 42.0 - 5.0 = 37.0 \text{ cm} \;\;\text{to}\;\; 47.0 \text{ cm}
$$

i.e., a **10 cm depth-adjustment range**. *(2 marks)*

**(iii) Seat Width:**

$$
W_{seat} = B_{hip,95\%} + 2 \times 2.5 \text{ cm (lateral clearance)} = 48.0 + 5.0 = 53.0 \text{ cm}
$$

A standard **≥ 50 cm** chair is acceptable. Most commercial office chairs use 48 – 52 cm seat widths, satisfying this. *(1 mark for calculation + 1 mark for acceptance statement)*

**Summary Table for (a):**

| Dimension | Designed Value | Adjustment Range | Anchor |
|---|---|---|---|
| Seat Height | 40.5 cm | 40.5 – 52.5 cm | 5th %ile popliteal + shoe |
| Seat Depth | 42 cm (median) | 37 – 47 cm | 5th–95th %ile buttock-popliteal − 5 cm |
| Seat Width | ≥ 50 cm | (fixed) | 95th %ile hip + 5 cm clearance |

---

**Part (b) — Work Surface and Backrest Geometry**

**(i) Work-Surface Height (Sitting):**

Use the **50th-percentile elbow height** with task allowance $h_{work} = +5$ cm (light-precision, e.g., chart documentation):

$$
H_{ws} = H_{elbow,50\%} + h_{work} = 64.0 + 5.0 = 69.0 \text{ cm} \;\; (\text{approx.})
$$

The 5th–95th range is 58 – 70 cm, so **provide ± 7 cm adjustability** = **14 cm total** to cover the full population. *(2 marks)*

**(ii) Backrest Lumbar Support Height:**

The lumbar pad must be located at the **lumbar lordosis apex** — approximately **20 – 25 cm above the seat pan** (Indian nursing data). The backrest *height* must be adjustable from **40 cm (lumbar-only) to 65 cm (full thoracic support)** to allow individual variation. *(2 marks)*

**(iii) Backrest Inclination:**

For a *task* posture (control-room monitoring, leaning slightly forward to read waveforms), use:

$$
\beta = 95° \text{ to } 100° \;\; \text{(recline from vertical)}
$$

For a *relaxed* posture (between alarms), $\beta = 105°$ to $110°$ is acceptable. Provide a **multi-position tilt lock** so the nurse can shift from 95° to 110°. *(1.5 marks)*

**(iv) Seat-Pan Tilt:**

For a *control-room seated task* with *rearward-leaning* monitor viewing, a slight rearward pan tilt of:

$$
\gamma = +3° \text{ to } +5° \text{ (rearward)}
$$

is recommended to prevent the user from sliding forward. *(1.5 marks)*

**Minimum Workstation Adjustability (KTU/ISO 9241-5):**

1. **Seat-height** adjustment ≥ **12 cm** (40.5 – 52.5 cm).
2. **Seat-depth** adjustment ≥ **10 cm** (37 – 47 cm).
3. **Backrest-height** adjustment ≥ **10 cm**.
4. **Backrest-tilt** adjustment from **95° to 110°**.
5. **Lumbar-depth** (convexity) adjustment ≥ **2 cm**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do not** use the *95th-percentile* value as the design target for *seat height* — this is the **most common mistake**. Seat height is anchored on the **5th percentile (shortest user)** to ensure *every user's feet touch the floor*. A 95th-percentile seat height would leave short users *dangling*, causing posterior-thigh pressure and **Deep Vein Thrombosis (DVT) risk**.
> 2. **Do not** confuse *sitting elbow height* with *standing elbow height*. Indian sitting elbow height is **≈ 60–70 cm**, while standing is **≈ 100–110 cm** — mixing these gives nonsensical work-surface heights.
> 3. **Do not** compute seat depth from the 5th percentile buttock-popliteal length — the seat must be *short enough* for the smallest user (use 5th percentile) but must also *not be too deep* for them, so subtract the popliteal clearance of **2–5 cm**.
> 4. **Do not** assume a single "average" angle for $\theta_v$ — the **15° preferred line of sight** is the *minimum* ergonomic target; **20°–30°** is the *practical* sustained-monitoring range. The KTU marking scheme gives 2 marks for explicitly stating the *range* and *source standard*.
> 5. **Do not** omit units in your final answers. Marks are routinely deducted for numerical answers without cm / m / degrees.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Memorize the bulleted facts below; each one is a likely 1-mark recall item.**

**Viewing Angle of a Cardiac Output Monitor:**

- ☐ The **vertical viewing angle** is measured from the **horizontal through the eye** to the **line of sight to the display centre**.
- ☐ KTU / ANSI recommended range: **15° – 30°** below horizontal.
- ☐ **Preferred** line of sight: **15° below horizontal** (HFES 100).
- ☐ Formula: $\theta_v = \tan^{-1}(\Delta H / D_{ed})$.
- ☐ **Horizontal viewing angle** must be within **± 30°** of display-normal for primary critical-care reading.
- ☐ Medical display **viewing distance**: **50 – 70 cm**.
- ☐ **Display tilt** $\phi = 0° – 15°$ backward, used to compensate when $\theta_v$ is too small.
- ☐ Critical care displays must have a **contrast ratio ≥ 3:1** for waveforms and **≥ 7:1** for text (ANSI/AAMI HE75).
- ☐ A **mismatch of viewing angle** is a root cause of *medication errors*, *alarm-fatigue*, and *work-related musculoskeletal disorders (WMSD)* in ICUs.

**Workplace and Seating Design:**

- ☐ **Seat height = 5th-percentile popliteal height + shoe-heel allowance (≈ 2.5 cm)**.
- ☐ **Seat depth = 95th-percentile buttock-popliteal length − 2 to 5 cm popliteal clearance**.
- ☐ **Seat width = 95th-percentile hip breadth + 5 cm lateral clearance**.
- ☐ **Work-surface height (sitting) = elbow height + task allowance (precision +5 cm; light 0 cm; heavy −5 to −10 cm)**.
- ☐ **Work-surface height (standing) = 95th-percentile standing elbow height − 5 to 10 cm clearance**.
- ☐ **Backrest lumbar support = 20 – 25 cm above seat pan**; backrest inclines **95° – 110°** from the seat pan.
