> 2. **Confusing kVp with keV:** kVp is *peak kilovoltage applied across the tube*; X-ray photon energies span a spectrum up to that peak.
> 3. **Omitting the "design implication" paragraph:** KTU examiners reward the *translation* of physics into human-factors design — a numerical answer without an engineering comment earns partial credit only.
> 4. **Anthropometric mis-quoting:** The correct range is **5th %ile female to 95th %ile male**, not 50th to 95th. Reversing the bounds breaks the 95% population coverage claim.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **🚀 Rapid-Revision Checklist**

- ☐ A **portable X-ray machine** integrates generator, tube, collimator, console, and detector on a wheeled chassis for bedside imaging.
- ☐ The **three competing HF constraints** are: operator ergonomics, patient safety, and diagnostic fidelity.
- ☐ **Push-handle height range** = **5th %ile female elbow (95 cm) to 95th %ile male shoulder (160 cm)** → 95% population coverage.
- ☐ **NIOSH push-force limit:** $F_p \leq 0.35 \cdot m \cdot g$.
- ☐ **Turning radius:** $R_{min} \approx L_w / \sin\delta_{max}$.
- ☐ **Inverse Square Law:** $D_2 = D_1 (d_1/d_2)^2$ — doubling distance quarters the dose.
- ☐ **HVL formula:** $I(x) = I_0 \cdot 2^{-x/\text{HVL}}$ — each HVL thickness halves intensity.
- ☐ **X-ray intensity:** $I_{X-ray} \propto Z \cdot I \cdot V^2$ (tungsten target, $Z = 74$).
- ☐ **Exposure:** $X = I \times t$ in mAs.
- ☐ **Geometric unsharpness:** $U_g = F \cdot d_{obj}/d_{focus}$.
- ☐ **Magnification:** $M = d_{focus}/d_{obj}$.
- ☐ **Display contrast ratio** ≥ **7:1** (ISO 9241-303).
- ☐ **ALARA** — As Low As Reasonably Achievable — governing philosophy.
- ☐ **APR presets** reduce cognitive load via Hick's Law minimization.
- ☐ **Standoff markers at 1.5 m and 2 m** translate inverse-square physics into operator behavior.
- ☐ **Two-stage dead-man switch** prevents accidental firing.
- ☐ **AERB / FDA 21 CFR 1020** — compliance frameworks for portable diagnostic X-ray.
- ☐ **IEC 60601-1-6** — usability engineering standard for medical electrical equipment.
- ☐ **Lead apron sweet spot:** **0.5 mm Pb-equivalent wraparound + standoff distance** — multi-barrier strategy.
- ☐ **Always quote the *design implication* alongside any numerical answer** to score full KTU marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Portable Ultrasound Device
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT742 - HUMAN FACTORS IN ENGINEERING AND DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Portable Ultrasound Device

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU 2024 Definition:**
> A **Portable Ultrasound Device** is a compact, handheld or cart-based diagnostic medical imaging instrument that uses high-frequency sound waves (**typically 2–18 MHz**) to generate real-time visual representations of internal body structures. From a *Human Factors and Ergonomics* perspective, it represents a benchmark case study in the application of user-centered design principles to medical devices, integrating anthropometric data, cognitive ergonomics, human–system interaction (HSI), and task-specific workflow optimization into a constrained form factor.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Relevance:**
> The Portable Ultrasound Device is included in Module 4 as a real-world case study demonstrating how ergonomic design principles, usability heuristics, anthropometric adaptability, and human–machine interface (HMI) considerations converge in the design of modern medical equipment. It bridges theoretical ergonomic principles with practical product design.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine holding a **smartphone-sized stethoscope** that doesn't just listen to your heart but *shows* it on a screen in real time. A traditional ultrasound machine is like a bulky desktop computer on wheels — large, intimidating, and tied to a single hospital room. A portable ultrasound device, by contrast, is like converting that desktop into a sleek tablet: same diagnostic power, but it fits in a doctor's coat pocket, works in an ambulance, and can be operated one-handed during an emergency.

**The human factors challenge:** How do you shrink a complex diagnostic tool without shrinking the *usability*? The answer lies in intelligent design — relocating controls to touchscreens, using voice commands, designing grips that fit 5th–95th percentile hand dimensions, and creating displays readable in dim field-hospital lighting.

### Core Human Factors Domains Applied

| Domain | Application in Portable Ultrasound |
|---|---|
| **Physical Ergonomics** | Grip design, weight distribution (typically **< 1.5 kg** handheld) |
| **Cognitive Ergonomics** | Reducing mental workload through icon-based interfaces |
| **Anthropometry** | Accommodating hand sizes from 5th female to 95th male percentile |
| **Usability Engineering** | One-handed operation, glove-compatible touchscreens |
| **Environmental Ergonomics** | Sunlight-readable displays, dust/water resistance (**IP54** rating) |
| **Safety Engineering** | Fail-safe probe connectors, acoustic output limits (**MI < 1.9, TI < 3.0**) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Standard Metrics to Remember:**
> - Operating frequency range: **2 MHz to 18 MHz**
> - Typical handheld weight: **0.5 kg to 1.5 kg**
> - Display luminance for outdoor readability: **≥ 1000 nits**
> - Battery operation time: **≥ 2 hours continuous scanning**
> - FDA acoustic output limits: **Mechanical Index (MI) < 1.9**, **Thermal Index (TI) < 3.0**

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Human Factors Design Framework for Portable Ultrasound

The design of a portable ultrasound device follows a structured **user-centered design (UCD) cycle** that integrates multiple ergonomic sub-disciplines:

**Step 1: User Analysis**
- Identify primary users: *sonographers, emergency physicians, paramedics, rural healthcare workers*
- Define user characteristics: training level, dominant hand, vision acuity, environmental context
- Apply **Population Stereotype Theory** for icon and control design

**Step 2: Task Analysis**
- **Hierarchical Task Analysis (HTA)** breaks scanning procedure into:
  - Power on → Select probe → Apply gel → Position probe → Adjust settings → Capture image → Save/Transmit
- Identify critical tasks where errors cause patient harm

**Step 3: Anthropometric Accommodation**

The device grip must accommodate hand dimensions across the user population. The **5th–95th percentile** accommodation principle applies:

$$D_{grip} = P_{50} \pm 1.645 \cdot \sigma_{hand}$$

where:
- $D_{grip}$ = Acceptable grip diameter range (mm)
- $P_{50}$ = 50th percentile hand breadth (typically **21 mm** for adult females, **24 mm** for adult males)
- $\sigma_{hand}$ = Standard deviation of hand breadth (typically **1.5–2.0 mm**)
- **1.645** = Z-score corresponding to 5th–95th percentile coverage (**90% of population**)

**Step 4: Cognitive Workload Analysis**

Using the **NASA-TLX (Task Load Index)** framework, the mental demand of operating a portable ultrasound must be evaluated across six subscales: Mental Demand, Physical Demand, Temporal Demand, Performance, Effort, Frustration.

**Step 5: Usability Testing**
- **Effectiveness:** Task completion rate (target **≥ 95%**)
- **Efficiency:** Time to complete standard scan (target **≤ 5 minutes** for FAST exam)
- **Satisfaction:** System Usability Scale (**SUS**) score (target **≥ 68** for "good")

**Step 6: Iterative Refinement**
- Apply Nielsen's **10 Usability Heuristics** for interface design
- Conduct contextual inquiry in actual use environments (ambulance, ER, rural clinic)

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Parameter | Expression | Application |
|---|---|---|
| Grip Diameter Range | $D_{grip} = P_{50} \pm 1.645\sigma$ | Anthropometric grip design |
| Reach Envelope (5th %ile female) | $R_{5} = \mu_R - 1.645\sigma_R$ | Minimum reach accommodation |
| Optimal Reach (for repeated tasks) | $R_{opt} = P_{5}$ | Primary control placement |
| Maximum Reach (emergency) | $R_{max} = P_{95}$ | Secondary control boundary |
| Display Viewing Distance | $V = 25 \text{ to } 40 \text{ cm}$ | Portable device screen placement |
| Minimum Character Height | $H_c = \frac{V \cdot \theta}{3438}$ (arcmin) | Legibility on small displays |
| Two-Handed Coupling Force | $F_{couple} = \mu \cdot N$ | Probe grip stability |
| Battery Run-Time | $T_{bat} = \frac{C_{Ah} \cdot V_{bat}}{P_{system}}$ | Continuous operation planning |
| Acoustic Power Limit (FDA) | $MI < 1.9$ and $TI < 3.0$ | Patient safety constraint |
| Usability Score Target | $SUS \geq 68$ | Acceptance threshold |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:**
> These formulas are used by medical device companies (Philips Lumify, GE Vscan, Butterfly iQ, Clarius) during the FDA 510(k) submission process, where human factors validation testing is **mandatory** per **IEC 62366-1:2015** (Medical devices — Application of usability engineering).

### 2.3 Critical Human Factors Issues in Portable Ultrasound

1. **Display Visibility Trade-off:** Larger screens improve readability but reduce portability. Solution: **Folding/tilting displays** with adaptive brightness.
2. **One-Handed Operation:** Probe must be held and manipulated with one hand; controls must be reachable with the other or via touch-screen gestures.
3. **Sterile Field Maintenance:** Device must be easy to clean; **IP54** rating minimum; crevice-free housing.
4. **Training Transfer:** Rural health workers with minimal training must operate safely; **guided workflow** modes reduce cognitive load.
5. **Gloved Operation:** Touchscreen must respond to nitrile/latex gloves (**≥ 2 mm thick**).
6. **Fatigue Prevention:** Device weight must allow **15-minute scan sessions** without wrist strain (keep below **1.2 kg** for handheld units).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Optimal Display Size for Portable Ultrasound

**Problem Statement:**
A handheld portable ultrasound device has a screen that the operator views at a working distance $V$. Determine the minimum character height required for legibility given a visual acuity requirement of $\theta$ arc-minutes.

**Step 1: Define the geometric relationship**

The minimum character height $H_c$ that can be resolved by the human eye at a given viewing distance is governed by the **angular resolution** of the eye.

The angle subtended by a character of height $H_c$ at distance $V$ is:

$$\theta = \frac{H_c}{V} \text{ (in radians)}$$

**Step 2: Convert radians to arc-minutes for practical use**

Since $1 \text{ radian} = \frac{180}{\pi} \text{ degrees} = \frac{180 \times 60}{\pi} \text{ arc-minutes} = \frac{10800}{\pi} \text{ arc-minutes}$

Therefore:

$$\theta_{\text{arcmin}} = \frac{H_c}{V} \cdot \frac{10800}{\pi}$$

**Step 3: Solve for the minimum character height**

Rearranging for $H_c$:

$$H_c = \frac{\theta_{\text{arcmin}} \cdot V \cdot \pi}{10800}$$

**Step 4: Introduce the standard visual acuity factor**

Standard human visual acuity is **1 arc-minute (Snellen 20/20)**. For medical/ultrasound displays where small annotations (depth markers, patient IDs) must be read, a more conservative **2× acuity** is used (i.e., $\theta = 2$ arc-minutes) to account for:
- Vibration in ambulance use
- Operator fatigue
- Glare and ambient lighting variation

Substituting $\theta = 2$ arc-minutes:

$$H_c = \frac{2 \cdot V \cdot \pi}{10800} = \frac{V \cdot \pi}{5400}$$

**Step 5: Apply to a typical portable ultrasound**

For a typical viewing distance $V = 35 \text{ cm} = 350 \text{ mm}$:

$$H_c = \frac{350 \cdot \pi}{5400} \approx \frac{1099.6}{5400} \approx 0.204 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 6: Convert to display pixel requirement**

For a typical smartphone-class LCD with pixel density of **400 PPI (pixels per inch)**:

$$\text{Pixel size} = \frac{25.4 \text{ mm}}{400} = 0.0635 \text{ mm}$$

$$\text{Minimum character pixels} = \frac{0.204}{0.0635} \approx 3.2 \text{ pixels per arc-minute}$$

Therefore, the **minimum character height** should be at least **4 pixels** at 400 PPI to ensure legibility for 20/20 vision users under field conditions.

> [!NOTE]
> **Design Implication:**
> This is why modern portable ultrasounds (e.g., Butterfly iQ+) use **high-resolution OLED displays (≥ 300 PPI)** — to ensure critical information remains readable in field conditions where lighting and vibration degrade visual performance.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Human Factors vs. Design Trade-offs

| Design Parameter | Human Factors Requirement | Trade-off Cost | Recommended Resolution |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Screen size** | ≥ 5 inches for image quality | Increases device size | Foldable / articulating screen |
| **Device weight** | ≤ 1.5 kg for handheld use | Limits battery capacity | Li-ion with fast-swap cells |
| **Button count** | Minimize for one-hand use | Reduces tactile feedback | Capacitive touch + haptic feedback |
| **Battery life** | ≥ 2 hours continuous | Adds weight | Wireless charging + hot-swap |
| **Probe frequency range** | 2–18 MHz multi-mode | Increases cost | Multi-frequency single crystal (CMUT) |
| **Sterilization** | Wipe-down with 70% IPA | Limits port types | Sealed USB-C, wireless data transfer |
| **Glove operation** | Touch works through 2 mm nitrile | Reduces accuracy | Glove-mode with sensitivity boost |

### 3.3 Worked Example: Grip Diameter for Portable Probe

**Given:**
- Mean adult hand breadth (across metacarpals): $\mu = 22.5 \text{ mm}$
- Standard deviation: $\sigma = 1.8 \text{ mm}$
- Target accommodation: 5th to 95th percentile

**Find:** Acceptable grip diameter range $D_{grip}$

**Solution:**

$$\begin{aligned}
D_{\min} &= \mu - 1.645\sigma \\
&= 22.5 - 1.645(1.8) \\
&= 22.5 - 2.961 \\
&= 19.54 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
D_{\max} &= \mu + 1.645\sigma \\
&= 22.5 + 1.645(1.8) \\
&= 22.5 + 2.961 \\
&= 25.46 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}$$

**Result:** The probe handle diameter should be between **19.5 mm and 25.5 mm** to accommodate 90% of the adult user population.

**Interpretation:** This is why most ultrasound probes have a cylindrical grip section with a diameter of approximately **20–30 mm**, often with a soft overmold to allow slight compression and improve grip security during long scanning sessions.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]**
*List any three anthropometric considerations that influence the ergonomic design of a handheld portable ultrasound probe.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Grip Diameter:** The probe handle diameter must accommodate the 5th–95th percentile hand breadth of the user population, typically **20–30 mm**, to ensure a secure, fatigue-free grip during extended scanning.

2. **Probe Length and Balance:** The device length must be balanced about the grip point so the **center of mass falls within the palm**, reducing wrist torque. Typical length: **15–20 cm**.

3. **Glove Compatibility:** The control buttons/touch surface must remain operable through **2 mm nitrile gloves**, requiring capacitive sensors tuned for reduced dielectric sensitivity.

*(Each point carries 1 mark. Full marks require clear linkage to anthropometric data.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Explain the significance of the FDA Mechanical Index (MI) and Thermal Index (TI) limits in portable ultrasound device design from a human factors perspective.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Mechanical Index (MI)** quantifies the risk of non-thermal bioeffects (cavitation) from acoustic pressure, while the **Thermal Index (TI)** quantifies the risk of tissue heating.

**FDA limits:** **MI < 1.9** and **TI < 3.0** for general-purpose use.

**Human factors significance:**
- The device must **continuously display both MI and TI values** on-screen (per FDA Track 3 requirements) so the operator can monitor exposure in real time.
- The display must be **legible at the operator's working distance** (25–40 cm) even under poor lighting.
- **Audible/visual alerts** must trigger when indices approach limits, requiring intuitive alert design that does not add cognitive load during critical scanning.

*(1 mark each for stating the limits, explaining their meaning, and linking to human factors interface design.)*

---

### Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme Pattern]**

**(a)** Discuss the **user-centered design (UCD) process** as applied to a portable ultrasound device. Identify the primary user groups and explain how task analysis (HTA) is performed for the standard FAST examination workflow. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A handheld ultrasound probe has an average adult hand breadth of **22.5 mm** with a standard deviation of **1.8 mm**. Calculate the grip diameter range to accommodate 90% of the user population. State two design modifications to the probe grip that would improve usability for users outside this range. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question A

**(a) User-Centered Design Process for Portable Ultrasound (7 marks)**

The **ISO 9241-210 UCD process** consists of four main phases:

**Phase 1 — Context of Use Analysis (2 marks):**
- **Primary users:** Emergency physicians, sonographers, paramedics, rural health workers, military medics.
- **User characteristics:** Varying training levels (from board-certified radiologist to minimally trained rural health worker), dominant hand preference, vision acuity, working in stressful/emergency conditions.
- **Environment:** Hospital, ambulance (vibration, noise), rural clinic (limited power, sunlight), battlefield (extreme conditions).
- **Tasks:** Scanning, image capture, diagnosis, data transmission.

**Phase 2 — User Requirements Specification (2 marks):**
- Functional: Multi-mode imaging (B-mode, M-mode, color Doppler), wireless data transfer, ≥ 2 hours battery.
- Usability: One-handed operation, glove-compatible touch, readable in 100,000 lux sunlight.
- Safety: Comply with IEC 60601-1, FDA acoustic limits, IP54 ingress protection.

**Phase 3 — Design Solutions (2 marks):**
- Produce prototypes (paper sketches → 3D printed models → functional units).
- Apply **Nielsen's 10 Heuristics**: visibility of system status, match between system and real world, user control and freedom, consistency, error prevention.
- Use **iterative prototyping** with user feedback at each stage.

**Phase 4 — Evaluation Against Requirements (1 mark):**
- Conduct summative usability testing per **IEC 62366-1**.
- Measure: Task completion rate (≥ 95%), time-on-task, error rate, SUS score (≥ 68), NASA-TLX workload.

**Hierarchical Task Analysis for FAST Exam (within Phase 1):**

1. Power on device → 1.1. Press power button → 1.2. Wait for boot (≤ 15 s)
2. Select FAST preset → 2.1. Tap "FAST" icon → 2.2. Confirm probe selection
3. Apply gel to probe head
4. Position probe on patient (4 sub-views: RUQ, LUQ, pelvic, subxiphoid) → 4.1. RUQ view → 4.2. LUQ view → 4.3. Pelvic view → 4.4. Subxiphoid view
5. Adjust image (gain, depth) → 5.1. Auto-optimize button → 5.2. Manual fine-tune if needed
6. Capture image → 6.1. Press freeze → 6.2. Press save
7. Clean probe and device

Each sub-task is evaluated for **error potential**, **frequency**, and **consequence of failure** (per FMEA principles).

---

**(b) Grip Diameter Calculation (7 marks)**

**Given:**
- Mean hand breadth: $\mu = 22.5 \text{ mm}$
- Standard deviation: $\sigma = 1.8 \text{ mm}$
- Coverage required: 90% (5th to 95th percentile, Z = 1.645)

**Calculation:**

$$\begin{aligned}
D_{\min} &= \mu - 1.645 \cdot \sigma \\
&= 22.5 - 1.645 \times 1.8 \\
&= 22.5 - 2.961 \\
&= 19.539 \text{ mm} \\
&\approx 19.5 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
D_{\max} &= \mu + 1.645 \cdot \sigma \\
&= 22.5 + 1.645 \times 1.8 \\
&= 22.5 + 2.961 \\
&= 25.461 \text{ mm} \\
&\approx 25.5 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}$$

**Result:** The grip diameter should lie between **19.5 mm and 25.5 mm** to accommodate 90% of adult users. **(3 marks for correct calculation, 1 mark for stating final answer with units)**

**Design Modifications for Out-of-Range Users (2 design modifications × 1.5 marks each = 3 marks):**

**Modification 1: Soft Overmold Grip with Variable Compliance**
- Use a **silicone or thermoplastic elastomer (TPE) overmold** of 2–3 mm thickness over a rigid core.
- The compression of the overmold allows the effective grip diameter to flex by **±2 mm**, extending accommodation to roughly **17.5 mm – 27.5 mm**.
- *Benefit:* Accommodates users with smaller hands (e.g., 5th percentile females) and larger hands (95th percentile males) without requiring multiple probe variants.

**Modification 2: Adjustable Strap or Contoured Grip Inserts**
- Provide **interchangeable grip inserts** (small/medium/large) that snap onto the probe handle.
- Or include an **elastic wrist strap** that lets the user secure the probe passively, reducing the grip force requirement for users with limited hand strength (e.g., elderly physicians, those with arthritis).
- *Benefit:* Extends usability to users with **reduced grip strength (< 200 N)** or those performing scans > 15 minutes.

**Bonus Concept (extra credit):** The grip should also include a **textured surface (knurled or ribbed pattern)** with a **surface roughness Ra between 0.8 and 1.6 μm** to ensure slip resistance even when the probe is wet from ultrasound gel.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme Pattern]**

**(a)** Explain **Nielsen's 10 Usability Heuristics** and discuss how each is applied in the touchscreen interface design of a portable ultrasound device. Give at least **five specific interface examples**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the **environmental human factors considerations** for a portable ultrasound device used in (i) an **ambulance during patient transport** and (ii) an **outdoor rural clinic in bright sunlight**. For each scenario, identify at least three ergonomic design requirements and justify them. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question B

**(a) Nielsen's 10 Heuristics Applied to Portable Ultrasound Interface (7 marks)**

Nielsen's heuristics are a set of **10 broad principles of user interface design** that serve as a framework for usability evaluation. Each is applied below to the portable ultrasound interface:

**1. Visibility of System Status (1 mark)**
- The system must always keep users informed about what is going on through timely feedback.
- *Application:* A **status bar at the top of the screen** showing battery level, probe connection state, current exam preset, time, and remaining storage. **Color-coded indicators** (green = ready, yellow = caution, red = fault).

**2. Match Between System and the Real World (1 mark)**
- The system should speak the user's language with words, phrases, and concepts familiar to the user.
- *Application:* Use **anatomical icons** (heart, liver, kidney) for organ-specific presets rather than abstract symbols. Use "Freeze" instead of "Halt Acquisition."

**3. User Control and Freedom (1 mark)**
- Users need a clearly marked "emergency exit" to leave unwanted states.
- *Application:* A **prominent "Back" button** and **undo function** for image annotations. Easy way to exit a sub-menu and return to the main scanning view with one tap.

**4. Consistency and Standards (1 mark)**
- Follow platform conventions.
- *Application:* Use the **same gesture vocabulary** as the operating system (swipe to delete, pinch to zoom on saved images). Consistent color coding across all modes (e.g., blue always means measurement).

**5. Error Prevention (1 mark)**
- Prevent problems from occurring in the first place.
- *Application:* **Confirmation dialog** before deleting patient data. Auto-save every 30 seconds to prevent data loss if battery dies. Probe must be physically locked into the connector to prevent detachment during scanning.

**6. Recognition Rather than Recall (1 mark)**
- Minimize the user's memory load by making objects, actions, and options visible.
- *Application:* **Dropdown menus with thumbnails** of saved patient studies (showing small preview image) rather than requiring the operator to remember patient IDs. All adjustment sliders (gain, depth) visible on the main screen with their numeric values.

**7. Flexibility and Efficiency of Use (1 mark)**
- Accelerators (unseen by the novice) speed up performance for the expert.
- *Application:* **Customizable preset buttons** on the home screen (Favorites). **Voice command** ("Freeze image," "Save to Patient John") for expert users. **Programmable physical buttons** on the probe for one-touch capture.

**8. Aesthetic and Minimalist Design (1 mark)**
- Dialogs should not contain information that is irrelevant or rarely needed.
- *Application:* The **main scanning screen shows only the ultrasound image, MI/TI values, and 3–4 essential controls**. Advanced settings are hidden in a "More" menu accessible by swipe.

**9. Help Users Recognize, Diagnose, and Recover from Errors (1 mark)**
- Error messages should be expressed in plain language, precisely indicate the problem, and constructively suggest a solution.
- *Application:* Instead of "Error 0x4F2A," show **"Probe disconnected. Please reconnect the probe to the bottom of the device."** with a **small illustration** of correct probe insertion.

**10. Help and Documentation (1 mark)**
- Provide easily searchable, task-focused help.
- *Application:* Built-in **tutorial videos** accessible from a "?" icon. Context-sensitive help that appears when the operator pauses on a screen for > 5 seconds.

*(For full 7 marks: 5 specific interface examples with clear heuristic linkage = 5 marks + 2 marks for overall explanation of the heuristic framework.)*

---

**(b) Environmental Human Factors for Two Scenarios (7 marks)**

### Scenario (i): Ambulance During Patient Transport (3.5 marks)

The ambulance environment imposes severe constraints: **vibration (ISO 2631 limits)**, **noise (60–80 dB)**, **limited space (≈ 1 m² workspace)**, **varying lighting (strobe effects from emergency lights)**, and **motion-induced instability**.

**Three Ergonomic Requirements:**

1. **Vibration-Damped Probe Holder & Anti-Slip Surface (1 mark + justification):**
   - The probe must be stored in a **foam-lined holder** that prevents it from becoming a projectile during sudden braking.
   - The device body must have a **high-friction silicone base** to prevent sliding on the ambulance shelf.
   - *Justification:* SAE J3045 and ambulance safety standards require equipment to withstand **5g acceleration** during emergency braking.

2. **High-Luminance Display with Anti-Glare Coating (1 mark + justification):**
   - Display must be readable under **strobe lighting** (5–15 Hz flicker) and direct sunlight through ambulance windows.
   - **≥ 1000 nits** brightness with **anti-reflective (AR) coating** and **optical bonding** to reduce internal reflections.
   - *Justification:* SAE J1757 requires emergency vehicle displays to maintain legibility under vibration + glare conditions.

3. **Latched Trigger Mechanism on Probe (1 mark + justification):**
   - The probe must have a **mechanical latch or magnetic detent** that holds the operator's hand position stable during imaging.
   - An optional **two-handed stabilization strap** to anchor the operator's hand against the patient's body.
   - *Justification:* Reduces the **NASA-TLX Physical Demand** subscale rating, allowing the operator to focus on image interpretation rather than probe stability.

4. **(Bonus) Audio Feedback Channel:** Since visual attention must stay on the patient, **voice-guided instructions** and **beep-coded warnings** (different tones for low battery, high MI, probe disconnect) must be available.

### Scenario (ii): Outdoor Rural Clinic in Bright Sunlight (3.5 marks)

Outdoor rural settings introduce: **direct sunlight (up to 100,000 lux)**, **dust/sand**, **temperature extremes (5–45 °C)**, **humidity (0–95 % RH)**, **unstable power supply**, and **minimally trained operators**.

**Three Ergonomic Requirements:**

1. **Sunlight-Readable Transflective Display (1 mark + justification):**
   - Use a **transflective LCD or high-brightness OLED (≥ 1500 nits)** that combines transmissive and reflective modes.
   - Automatic brightness adjustment via **ambient light sensor**.
   - *Justification:* Standard displays become unreadable above 50,000 lux; transflective technology ensures legibility in **direct tropical sunlight** (relevant for Kerala's coastal rural clinics).

2. **IP54-Rated Sealed Enclosure (1 mark + justification):**
   - The device must be **dust-protected and splash-resistant** (IP54 per IEC 60529) to survive dusty field conditions and accidental water/spill exposure.
   - **Sealed capacitive touch** with no physical seams where gel or dust can accumulate.
   - *Justification:* Medical devices used in resource-limited settings must be **wipeable with 70% IPA** for infection control, requiring a fully sealed housing.

3. **Guided Workflow Mode for Novice Operators (1 mark + justification):**
   - A **step-by-step "wizard" mode** with on-screen pictograms guides rural health workers through the FAST exam with minimal training.
   - Each step is locked until the previous one is completed, preventing user errors.
   - **Voice prompts** in local language (e.g., Malayalam, Hindi) available.
   - *Justification:* Per **WHO guidelines for task-shifting in ultrasound**, devices used by non-experts must include **cognitive forcing functions** to prevent diagnostic errors.

4. **(Bonus) Long-Battery-Life with Solar/Hand-Crank Backup (extra credit):**
   - Devices for rural deployment may require **8–12 hours of operation** without grid power, necessitating swappable battery packs or auxiliary solar charging.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Do NOT** list requirements without **justification** linking them to human factors theory (e.g., reference to NASA-TLX, Nielsen heuristics, anthropometric data, or specific ISO/IEC standards). Generic statements like "it should be lightweight" without data will receive **partial credit only**.
> - **Do NOT** confuse *usability heuristics* with *ergonomic guidelines*. Heuristics are **interface principles** (Nielsen), while ergonomics deals with **physical/cognitive fit** between user and system. Examiners specifically test this distinction.
> - **Do NOT** forget to mention **standards compliance** (IEC 62366-1, IEC 60601-1, FDA HF Guidance) when discussing medical device human factors — this is a frequent differentiator between 12-mark and 14-mark answers.
> - **Calculation errors** in percentile problems (using 1.96 instead of 1.645 for 90% coverage) are the **most common reason** for losing 2–3 marks in numerical sub-parts. Always specify which percentile range you are solving for.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- A **Portable Ultrasound Device** is a compact medical imaging tool used as a case study to apply human factors and ergonomic principles.
- The **primary ergonomic goal** is to preserve diagnostic capability while enabling **one-handed, portable, glove-compatible** operation across diverse users.
- **Key anthropometric design parameter:** Grip diameter = $P_{50} \pm 1.645\sigma$ (5th–95th percentile accommodation).
- **Display design rule:** Minimum character height = $\frac{V \cdot \pi \cdot \theta}{10800}$ where $V$ is in mm and $\theta$ in arc-minutes.
- **FDA acoustic safety limits:** **MI < 1.9** and **TI < 3.0** must be continuously displayed to the operator.
- **Nielsen's 10 Heuristics** must be applied systematically to the touchscreen interface.
- **Environmental human factors:** Vibration (ambulance), sunlight readability (outdoor), dust/water resistance (IP54), temperature extremes.
- **Usability acceptance thresholds:** Task completion **≥ 95%**, time for FAST exam **≤ 5 min**, **SUS score ≥ 68**.
- **Applicable standards:** IEC 62366-1 (Usability Engineering), IEC 60601-1 (Medical Electrical Equipment Safety), ISO 9241-210 (UCD), FDA HF Guidance.
- **Cognitive ergonomics:** Apply **NASA-TLX** to assess workload; use **guided workflow modes** for novice operators.
- **Hygiene and maintenance:** Device must be **wipeable with 70% IPA** and survive **≥ 1000 wipe cycles** without degradation.
- **Connectivity human factors:** Wireless data transfer (Wi-Fi 6, Bluetooth 5) must be **secure (HIPAA/GDPR compliant)** and **one-touch activated**.
- **Probe frequency range:** **2–18 MHz** — lower for deep abdominal imaging, higher for vascular/superficial structures.
- **Battery requirement:** **≥ 2 hours** continuous scanning for emergency use; hot-swap capability preferred.
- **Rapid-fire memory aid:** **"A HAND"** — **A**nthropometry, **H**euristics, **A**coustic safety, **N**ASA-TLX, **D**isplay legibility.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Transport Ventilator
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT742 - HUMAN FACTORS IN ENGINEERING AND DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Transport Ventilator

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Transport Ventilator** (also known as a **Transport Exhaust System** or **Local Exhaust Ventilation - Transport System**) is a mechanical or natural ventilation system designed to capture, convey, and discharge airborne contaminants (dusts, fumes, vapors, mists, or gases) from the point of generation to a safe discharge location outside the occupational environment. It is a critical engineering control used in industrial hygiene and occupational safety to protect workers from hazardous atmospheric exposures.

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PEEBT742 – Human Factors in Engineering and Design, Module 4)**, the transport ventilator is studied under the broader domain of **Environmental Ergonomics and Industrial Ventilation**, where it serves as a primary engineering control to maintain acceptable indoor air quality, thermal comfort, and worker safety.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):** Transport ventilators form a sub-topic under *Industrial Ventilation and Air Quality Control*. Students must understand their classification, design parameters, duct flow equations, fan laws, and human-factor considerations such as noise, draft, and operator interference.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine your home's **kitchen exhaust hood**. When you are frying food, smoke and oil vapors are generated on the stove. The exhaust hood *captures* the rising plume, *transports* it through a duct (chimney), and *discharges* it outside your home. This is exactly what a transport ventilator does in an industrial setting — only the contaminants are far more hazardous (e.g., silica dust, lead fumes, solvent vapors), the airflow rates are much higher, and the system must be engineered to capture the contaminant *before* it reaches the worker's breathing zone.

**Three core stages of a transport ventilator:**

1. **Capture (Hood):** The contaminant is caught at or very near its source.
2. **Transport (Ducts):** The contaminated air is conveyed through a network of ducts.
3. **Discharge (Stack/Fan):** The air is released safely into the atmosphere, often after filtration.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metric:** The performance of a transport ventilator is universally expressed in **cubic meters per second (m³/s)** or **cubic feet per minute (CFM)**. A typical industrial transport system handles airflows ranging from **0.1 m³/s to 50 m³/s**.

## 1.3 Classification of Transport Ventilators

Transport ventilators are broadly classified into the following categories:

| Type | Description | Typical Application |
|------|-------------|---------------------|
| **Local Exhaust Ventilation (LEV)** | Captures contaminant at source via a hood | Welding, grinding, soldering, painting booths |
| **General (Dilution) Ventilation** | Dilutes contaminants with fresh air in the entire room | Warehouses, garages, battery charging rooms |
| **Push-Pull Ventilation** | Combination of supply (push) and exhaust (pull) air jets | Open-surface tanks, large painting operations |
| **Receiving (Make-up Air) Ventilation** | Supplies clean air to replace exhausted air | Compensation air for LEV systems |

> [!NOTE]
> **Human Factors Relevance:** From an ergonomic standpoint, transport ventilators must be designed such that the worker is not subjected to excessive **drafts (> 0.5 m/s in cold environments)**, **noise (> 85 dBA at the operator position)**, or **cold downdrafts** that cause discomfort and reduce productivity.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Principles — Step-by-Step Logic

The functioning of a transport ventilator can be decomposed into **five sequential engineering principles**:

### Step 1 — Contaminant Generation Identification
- **Why:** Before designing any system, the type, rate, and toxicity of contaminant generation must be quantified.
- **How:** Industrial hygienists measure **generation rate (G)** in **mg/s** or **g/hr** using source emission testing (e.g., EPA Method 5 for particulates).

### Step 2 — Capture Velocity Selection
- **Why:** A minimum air velocity at the hood opening is required to overcome the contaminant plume's buoyancy and momentum.
- **How:** Refer to **ACGIH Industrial Ventilation Manual** which prescribes capture velocities:

| Contaminant Type | Capture Velocity (m/s) |
|------------------|------------------------|
| **Vapors (low velocity)** | 0.25 – 0.50 |
| **Welding fumes** | 0.50 – 1.00 |
| **Hot dusts** | 1.00 – 2.50 |
| **Very hot materials** | 2.50 – 10.0 |

### Step 3 — Hood Entry Loss (K-entry)
- **Why:** Real hoods do not behave as ideal orifices; they have an entry loss coefficient.
- **How:** The static pressure loss at the hood entry is:
$$P_{entry} = K_h \cdot \frac{\rho v^2}{2}$$
where $K_h$ is the hood loss coefficient (typically **0.25 for a flanged hood**, **0.93 for an unflanged hood**), $\rho$ is air density (**1.2 kg/m³ at 20 °C**), and $v$ is the duct velocity.

### Step 4 — Duct Transport
- **Why:** The duct must convey the contaminated air with minimum loss while maintaining transport velocity to prevent particle settling.
- **How:** The **transport velocity** is the minimum duct velocity required to keep particulates suspended:

| Contaminant | Transport Velocity (m/s) |
|-------------|--------------------------|
| **Vapors, gases** | 5 – 10 |
| **Light dusts** | 10 – 13 |
| **Heavy dusts** | 13 – 18 |
| **Large particles** | 18 – 25 |

### Step 5 — Fan Selection & Discharge
- **Why:** The fan must overcome total system resistance (static pressure) and deliver the required airflow.
- **How:** Fans are selected based on the **system curve** intersecting the **fan curve**, ensuring operation at the **best efficiency point (BEP)**.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **definitive formula bank** for solving any KTU University Exam question on Transport Ventilators. Memorize these equations, their units, and boundary conditions.

| # | Formula | Description | Units | Typical KTU Application |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|--------------------------|
| 1 | $Q = v \cdot A$ | Volumetric flow rate = velocity × cross-sectional area | m³/s | Q1: *Basic flow calculations* |
| 2 | $v = \dfrac{Q}{A} = \dfrac{4Q}{\pi D^2}$ | Velocity in a circular duct | m/s | Q2: *Duct sizing problems* |
| 3 | $D = \sqrt{\dfrac{4Q}{\pi v}}$ | Required duct diameter for given Q and v | m | Q3: *Design of duct networks* |
| 4 | $\Delta P_f = f \cdot \dfrac{L}{D} \cdot \dfrac{\rho v^2}{2}$ | Friction loss in straight duct (Darcy-Weisbach) | Pa | Q4: *Duct pressure loss* |
| 5 | $f = 0.0791 \cdot Re^{-0.25}$ | Blasius equation for smooth ducts (Re < 10⁵) | dimensionless | Q4: *Friction factor computation* |
| 6 | $P_{entry} = K_h \cdot \dfrac{\rho v^2}{2}$ | Hood entry loss | Pa | Q5: *Hood design* |
| 7 | $\Delta P_{total} = \sum \Delta P_{friction} + \sum \Delta P_{entry} + \sum \Delta P_{fitting}$ | Total system static pressure | Pa | Q6: *Fan selection* |
| 8 | $\dfrac{Q_2}{Q_1} = \dfrac{N_2}{N_1}$ | Fan Law 1: Flow ∝ Speed | – | Q7: *Fan speed scaling* |
| 9 | $\dfrac{P_2}{P_1} = \left(\dfrac{N_2}{N_1}\right)^2 \cdot \left(\dfrac{\rho_2}{\rho_1}\right)$ | Fan Law 2: Pressure ∝ Speed² × Density | – | Q7: *Fan scaling* |
| 10 | $\dfrac{HP_2}{HP_1} = \left(\dfrac{N_2}{N_1}\right)^3 \cdot \left(\dfrac{\rho_2}{\rho_1}\right)$ | Fan Law 3: Power ∝ Speed³ × Density | – | Q7: *Power scaling* |
| 11 | $TL = 10 \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{Q_1}{Q_2}\right) + 20 \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{L_1}{L_2}\right)$ | Transmission loss in ducts (noise) | dB | Q8: *Acoustic design* |
| 12 | $C = \dfrac{G}{Q \cdot K}$ | Concentration after ventilation (mixing model) | mg/m³ | Q9: *Dilution ventilation* |

> **Notation key:** $Q$ = flow rate, $v$ = velocity, $A$ = area, $D$ = duct diameter, $L$ = duct length, $f$ = friction factor, $Re$ = Reynolds number, $K_h$ = hood loss coefficient, $N$ = fan rotational speed (RPM), $G$ = contaminant generation rate, $C$ = concentration, $K$ = mixing factor.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

Transport ventilators are deployed in:

- **Foundries and metal casting shops** — capture of silica dust and metal fumes
- **Paint spray booths** — control of VOCs and isocyanate aerosols
- **Pharmaceutical manufacturing** — containment of potent compounds
- **Welding bays** — extraction of manganese, ozone, and nitrogen oxides
- **Asbestos abatement sites** — negative-pressure transport to HEPA filters
- **Battery rooms** — dilution of hydrogen gas below LEL (Lower Explosive Limit)

From a **human factors perspective**, the design must ensure that workers are not exposed to:
1. **Excessive noise** from the fan/duct (use of silencers, lined ducts)
2. **Cold drafts** near workstations (use of flaps, push-pull systems)
3. **Visual obstruction** from hoods (transparent enclosures, strategic hood placement)
4. **Interference with work tasks** (ergonomic hood design, articulating arms)

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Duct Diameter from Flow and Velocity Constraints

### Problem Setup
A transport ventilator handles **$Q = 0.50 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$** of contaminated air. The minimum transport velocity for the heavy dust handled is **$v = 15 \text{ m/s}$**. Determine the required duct diameter.

### Step 1 — State the governing relation
For a circular duct, the cross-sectional area is:
$$A = \frac{\pi D^2}{4}$$

### Step 2 — Relate area, velocity, and flow rate
The continuity equation for incompressible flow gives:
$$Q = v \cdot A = v \cdot \frac{\pi D^2}{4}$$

### Step 3 — Solve for $D$
Rearranging for the diameter:
$$\frac{\pi D^2}{4} = \frac{Q}{v}$$

$$D^2 = \frac{4Q}{\pi v}$$

$$D = \sqrt{\frac{4Q}{\pi v}}$$

### Step 4 — Substitute numerical values
$$D = \sqrt{\frac{4 \times 0.50}{\pi \times 15}}$$

$$D = \sqrt{\frac{2.00}{47.124}}$$

$$D = \sqrt{0.04244}$$

$$D = 0.206 \text{ m}$$

### Step 5 — Convert to standard duct size
$$\boxed{D \approx 206 \text{ mm} \;\; (\text{use standard 200 mm duct})}$$

> **Engineering judgment:** Always select the next available **standard duct size** (typically available in 50 mm increments: 100, 150, 200, 250, 300 mm). If 206 mm is required, select 200 mm and **recompute the actual velocity** to ensure it remains above the transport velocity threshold.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Friction Loss in a Straight Galvanized Duct

### Problem Setup
Air at **20 °C** ($\rho = 1.20 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $\mu = 1.81 \times 10^{-5} \text{ Pa·s}$) flows through a **20 m long** horizontal circular duct of diameter **$D = 0.20 \text{ m}$** at a velocity of **$v = 15 \text{ m/s}$**. Compute the friction pressure loss.

### Step 1 — Calculate the Reynolds number
$$Re = \frac{\rho v D}{\mu} = \frac{1.20 \times 15 \times 0.20}{1.81 \times 10^{-5}}$$

$$Re = \frac{3.60}{1.81 \times 10^{-5}} = 198{,}895$$

### Step 2 — Determine the friction factor
Since $Re < 10^5$ (turbulent flow regime boundary, but here $Re$ is just below $2 \times 10^5$ which is still in the transition region; for KTU exam purposes, we use Blasius for smooth ducts up to $10^5$ and Colebrook for higher values). For this problem, applying Blasius (valid up to $Re = 10^5$):

> **Note:** Since our $Re = 198{,}895$ slightly exceeds Blasius's upper limit ($10^5$), for KTU board exam accuracy we apply the **Colebrook equation** in its explicit form (Swamee-Jain) for galvanized steel roughness $\varepsilon = 0.15 \text{ mm}$:

$$f = \frac{0.25}{\left[\log_{10}\left(\frac{\varepsilon/D}{3.7} + \frac{5.74}{Re^{0.9}}\right)\right]^2}$$

$$\frac{\varepsilon}{D} = \frac{0.00015}{0.20} = 0.00075$$

$$\frac{5.74}{Re^{0.9}} = \frac{5.74}{(198{,}895)^{0.9}} = \frac{5.74}{74{,}165} = 7.74 \times 10^{-5}$$

$$\frac{\varepsilon/D}{3.7} = \frac{0.00075}{3.7} = 2.03 \times 10^{-4}$$

Sum inside the log:
$$2.03 \times 10^{-4} + 7.74 \times 10^{-5} = 2.80 \times 10^{-4}$$

$$\log_{10}(2.80 \times 10^{-4}) = -3.553$$

$$f = \frac{0.25}{(-3.553)^2} = \frac{0.25}{12.62} = 0.0198$$

### Step 3 — Compute the dynamic pressure term
$$\frac{\rho v^2}{2} = \frac{1.20 \times (15)^2}{2} = \frac{1.20 \times 225}{2} = 135 \text{ Pa}$$

### Step 4 — Apply the Darcy-Weisbach equation
$$\Delta P_f = f \cdot \frac{L}{D} \cdot \frac{\rho v^2}{2}$$

$$\Delta P_f = 0.0198 \times \frac{20}{0.20} \times 135$$

$$\Delta P_f = 0.0198 \times 100 \times 135$$

$$\Delta P_f = 267.3 \text{ Pa}$$

### Final Result
$$\boxed{\Delta P_f \approx 267 \text{ Pa} \;\; (\text{for a 20 m straight duct run})}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Fan Sizing Using the System Curve

### Problem Setup
A transport ventilator has the following components:

| Component | Loss Coefficient (K) | Quantity |
|-----------|---------------------|----------|
| Flanged hood entry | 0.25 | 1 |
| 90° elbow (smooth) | 0.30 | 3 |
| 45° elbow | 0.20 | 2 |
| Branch tee | 0.50 | 1 |
| Straight duct friction | (as computed above) | 20 m |

Total duct length with all fittings: $L_{eq} = L_{straight} + \sum K \cdot \frac{D}{f}$ (equivalent length method).

### Step 1 — Compute equivalent length of fittings
For each fitting, equivalent length:
$$L_{eq,fitting} = K \cdot \frac{D}{f} = K \cdot \frac{0.20}{0.0198} = K \cdot 10.10 \text{ m}$$

| Fitting | K | $L_{eq}$ (m) |
|---------|---|--------------|
| Hood | 0.25 | 2.53 |
| 90° elbows (×3) | 0.30 × 3 = 0.90 | 9.09 |
| 45° elbows (×2) | 0.20 × 2 = 0.40 | 4.04 |
| Branch tee | 0.50 | 5.05 |
| **Subtotal fittings** | **2.05** | **20.71** |
| Straight duct | – | 20.00 |
| **Total equivalent length** | – | **40.71 m** |

### Step 2 — Compute total system pressure at design flow
Using the simplified equivalent-length method:
$$\Delta P_{system} = f \cdot \frac{L_{eq,total}}{D} \cdot \frac{\rho v^2}{2}$$

$$\Delta P_{system} = 0.0198 \times \frac{40.71}{0.20} \times 135$$

$$\Delta P_{system} = 0.0198 \times 203.55 \times 135$$

$$\Delta P_{system} = 544.1 \text{ Pa}$$

### Step 3 — Fan selection summary
$$\boxed{Q_{fan} = 0.50 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}, \quad \Delta P_{fan} = 545 \text{ Pa} \;\; (\text{plus 10–15\% safety margin} \Rightarrow 625 \text{ Pa})}$$

The fan should be selected to deliver at least **$0.50 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$** at **$625 \text{ Pa}$ static pressure**, typically operating near its **best efficiency point (BEP)**.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Effect of Human Factors on Ventilator Design

| Human Factor | Ventilator Design Implication | Mitigation Strategy |
|--------------|-------------------------------|---------------------|
| **Worker proximity to hood** | Higher capture velocity needed | Flanged hoods, enclosing hoods |
| **Draft sensitivity** | Cold downdrafts cause complaints | Low-velocity push-pull, slot hoods |
| **Noise exposure** | Fan noise can exceed 85 dBA | Silencers, lined ducts, vibration mounts |
| **Visual interference** | Opaque hoods block vision | Transparent enclosures, low-profile hoods |
| **Work posture variability** | Fixed hoods may miss emissions | Articulating arms, flexible extraction arms |
| **Maintenance access** | Clogged ducts reduce performance | Easy-access cleanouts, blast gates |
| **Heat stress (foundries)** | Hot air rises against exhaust | Canopy hoods, high-volume LEV |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Transport Ventilator

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    src[Contaminant Source] --> capture[Hood Capture Stage]
    capture --> duct1[Transport Ductwork Network]
    duct1 --> branch[Duct Branches and Fittings]
    branch --> duct2[Main Trunk Duct]
    duct2 --> damper[Dampers and Balancing Valves]
    damper --> cleaner[Air Cleaning Device]
    cleaner --> filter[Baghouse HEPA or Carbon Filter]
    filter --> fan[Centrifugal Exhaust Fan]
    fan --> silencer[Noise Attenuator Silencer]
    silencer --> stack[Discharge Stack to Atmosphere]
    stack --> atmos[Safe Atmospheric Discharge]
    make[Make up Air Unit] -.Replacement Air.-> room[Workroom Environment]
    room -.Induces.-> src
    human[Human Operator] -.Exposed.-> room
    control[Control Panel VFD Sensors] -.Monitors.-> fan
    control -.Adjusts.-> damper
```

## 4.2 Detailed Component Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph CAPT[Capture Subsystem]
        H1[Flanged Hood]
        H2[Canopy Hood]
        H3[Slot Hood]
        H4[Booth Enclosure]
    end
    subgraph TRANS[Transport Subsystem]
        D1[Straight Duct Sections]
        D2[Elbows and Bends]
        D3[Reducers and Enlargers]
        D4[Dampers and Blast Gates]
    end
    subgraph CLEAN[Cleaning Subsystem]
        F1[Cyclone Separator]
        F2[Fabric Filter Baghouse]
        F3[HEPA Final Filter]
        F4[Activated Carbon Adsorber]
    end
    subgraph DRIVE[Drive and Discharge Subsystem]
        FN[Centrifugal Fan with VFD]
        SI[Acoustic Silencer]
        ST[Discharge Stack with Rain Cap]
    end
    H1 --> D1
    H2 --> D1
    H3 --> D1
    H4 --> D1
    D1 --> D2 --> D3 --> D4 --> F1
    F1 --> F2 --> F3 --> F4 --> FN --> SI --> ST
```

## 4.3 Airflow Path and Pressure Drop Profile

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Ambient Pressure 0 Pa] -->|Hood Entry Loss 50 Pa| P2[After Hood 50 Pa]
    P2 -->|Duct Friction 270 Pa| P3[After Duct 320 Pa]
    P3 -->|Fitting Losses 130 Pa| P4[Before Filter 450 Pa]
    P4 -->|Filter Resistance 250 Pa| P5[After Filter 700 Pa]
    P5 -->|Fan Adds 800 Pa| P6[After Fan 100 Pa above atmosphere]
    P6 -->|Silencer 60 Pa| P7[Discharge 40 Pa exit velocity pressure]
```

## 4.4 Human-in-the-Loop Ergonomic Integration

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Worker] -->|Performs Task| B[Process Operation]
    B -->|Emits Contaminant| C[Capture Zone]
    C -->|Extracted Air| D[Ventilator System]
    D -->|Feedback Noise Vibration| A
    D -->|Visual Indicator| A
    E[Industrial Hygienist] -->|Monitors Air Sampling| F[Compliance Check]
    F -->|Adjusts Setpoints| D
    G[Ergonomist] -->|Assesses Worker Comfort| A
    G -->|Recommends Hood Repositioning| D
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper 2]`
**Define a transport ventilator. List the four main components of a typical industrial local exhaust ventilation system.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **transport ventilator** is a mechanical ventilation system used to capture, convey, and discharge airborne contaminants from the workplace to a safe location, thereby protecting worker health and maintaining indoor air quality.

**Four main components:**
1. **Hood (Capture device)** – placed at or near the contaminant source to capture the pollutant.
2. **Ductwork (Transport network)** – conveys the contaminated air with sufficient transport velocity to prevent settling.
3. **Air cleaning device (Filter/Cyclone)** – removes contaminants before discharge to meet environmental regulations.
4. **Fan (Prime mover)** – provides the energy to overcome system resistance and maintain required airflow.
5. *(Optional 5th – discharge stack)* for safe atmospheric release.

*(1 mark for definition + 2 marks for correctly listing components)*

---

### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Supplementary]`
**What is meant by "transport velocity" in a duct? Why is it important in the design of a transport ventilator for heavy dusts?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Transport velocity** is the minimum air velocity that must be maintained inside a duct to keep the conveyed particulates suspended in the airstream and prevent their settling, which would otherwise cause duct blockage, fire hazard (for combustible dusts), and loss of system efficiency.

**Importance for heavy dusts:**
- Heavy dusts have high terminal settling velocities; insufficient duct velocity leads to particle deposition on duct walls.
- This causes progressive blockage, increased pressure drop, fan overloading, and ultimately complete system failure.
- For heavy dusts (e.g., metal oxides, sand, foundry dust), transport velocities of **13–18 m/s** are recommended per the ACGIH Industrial Ventilation Manual.

*(1 mark for definition + 2 marks for importance and typical values)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

### **Question Choice A** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 4]`

**(a)** With a neat sketch, describe the working principle of a **Local Exhaust Ventilation (LEV)** system used in a welding shop. Explain the role of capture velocity and transport velocity in its design. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An industrial exhaust system handles **$Q = 0.80 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$** of air contaminated with wood dust. The duct is circular, galvanized steel ($\varepsilon = 0.15 \text{ mm}$), of length **$L = 30 \text{ m}$**, with diameter **$D = 0.25 \text{ m}$**. The air temperature is 30 °C ($\rho = 1.16 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $\mu = 1.85 \times 10^{-5} \text{ Pa·s}$). Compute:
1. The actual duct velocity.
2. The Reynolds number and friction factor (Swamee-Jain).
3. The friction pressure loss using the Darcy-Weisbach equation. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution (a) — 7 marks**

**Working Principle of LEV in Welding Shop:**

A Local Exhaust Ventilation system in a welding shop captures welding fumes (containing manganese, chromium, nickel, ozone, NOₓ) at the source using a **fume extraction hood** (either fixed extraction arm or a moveable on-torch extractor). The contaminated air is then **transported** through a duct network at sufficient velocity to prevent particle settling, **cleaned** using a fabric filter or electrostatic precipitator, and finally **discharged** through a stack into the atmosphere.

**Key design parameters:**

- **Capture velocity** is the air velocity required at the point of contaminant generation to overcome the buoyant and momentum forces of the rising fume plume. For welding fumes, ACGIH recommends a capture velocity of **0.5 to 1.0 m/s** at the hood face. Insufficient capture velocity allows the plume to escape into the worker's breathing zone.

- **Transport velocity** is the minimum air velocity inside the duct (typically **10–13 m/s** for welding fumes) required to keep particulates suspended and prevent duct deposition. The duct must be sized such that the actual velocity always exceeds this transport velocity under all operating conditions (including partial loading of multiple branches).

**Block diagram of the system:**

| Stage | Component | Function |
|-------|-----------|----------|
| 1 | Welding torch + extraction hood | Source capture |
| 2 | Flexible duct / arm | Local transport |
| 3 | Rigid metal duct | Main transport |
| 4 | Fabric filter | Particulate removal |
| 5 | Centrifugal fan | Air mover |
| 6 | Discharge stack | Atmospheric release |

*(Award: 3 marks for description + 2 marks for capture velocity + 2 marks for transport velocity)*

---

#### **Model Solution (b) — 7 marks**

**Given:**
$$Q = 0.80 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}, \quad D = 0.25 \text{ m}, \quad L = 30 \text{ m}, \quad \rho = 1.16 \text{ kg/m}^3, \quad \mu = 1.85 \times 10^{-5} \text{ Pa·s}, \quad \varepsilon = 0.15 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 1 — Compute the actual duct velocity**

The cross-sectional area of the duct:
$$A = \frac{\pi D^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \times (0.25)^2}{4} = \frac{0.1963}{4} = 0.04909 \text{ m}^2$$

The velocity is:
$$v = \frac{Q}{A} = \frac{0.80}{0.04909} = 16.30 \text{ m/s}$$

Since this exceeds the transport velocity for wood dust (typically 10–13 m/s), the design is acceptable.

**Step 2 — Compute the Reynolds number**

$$Re = \frac{\rho v D}{\mu} = \frac{1.16 \times 16.30 \times 0.25}{1.85 \times 10^{-5}}$$

$$Re = \frac{4.727}{1.85 \times 10^{-5}} = 2.555 \times 10^{5}$$

This is **turbulent flow** ($Re > 4000$).

**Step 3 — Compute the friction factor (Swamee-Jain equation)**

The relative roughness:
$$\frac{\varepsilon}{D} = \frac{0.15 \times 10^{-3}}{0.25} = 6.0 \times 10^{-4}$$

The Swamee-Jain equation:
$$f = \frac{0.25}{\left[\log_{10}\left(\frac{\varepsilon/D}{3.7} + \frac{5.74}{Re^{0.9}}\right)\right]^2}$$

Computing each term:
$$\frac{\varepsilon/D}{3.7} = \frac{6.0 \times 10^{-4}}{3.7} = 1.622 \times 10^{-4}$$

$$\frac{5.74}{Re^{0.9}} = \frac{5.74}{(2.555 \times 10^5)^{0.9}} = \frac{5.74}{84{,}520} = 6.79 \times 10^{-5}$$

Sum:
$$1.622 \times 10^{-4} + 6.79 \times 10^{-5} = 2.301 \times 10^{-4}$$

Logarithm:
$$\log_{10}(2.301 \times 10^{-4}) = -3.638$$

Squared:
$$(-3.638)^2 = 13.23$$

Friction factor:
$$f = \frac{0.25}{13.23} = 0.0189$$

**Step 4 — Compute friction pressure loss (Darcy-Weisbach)**

Dynamic pressure:
$$\frac{\rho v^2}{2} = \frac{1.16 \times (16.30)^2}{2} = \frac{1.16 \times 265.7}{2} = 154.1 \text{ Pa}$$

Friction loss:
$$\Delta P_f = f \cdot \frac{L}{D} \cdot \frac{\rho v^2}{2}$$

$$\Delta P_f = 0.0189 \times \frac{30}{0.25} \times 154.1$$

$$\Delta P_f = 0.0189 \times 120 \times 154.1$$

$$\Delta P_f = 349.5 \text{ Pa}$$

**Final Answer:**
$$\boxed{v = 16.3 \text{ m/s}, \quad Re = 2.56 \times 10^5, \quad f = 0.0189, \quad \Delta P_f \approx 350 \text{ Pa}}$$

*(Award: 2 marks velocity, 2 marks Reynolds + f, 3 marks pressure loss)*

### **Question Choice B** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Regular]`

**(a)** Differentiate between **Local Exhaust Ventilation (LEV)** and **General (Dilution) Ventilation**. State the situations in which each is preferred, with one industrial example each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A centrifugal fan operating at **$N_1 = 1450 \text{ RPM}$** delivers **$Q_1 = 1.2 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$** against a static pressure of **$\Delta P_1 = 600 \text{ Pa}$** when handling air of density **$\rho_1 = 1.20 \text{ kg/m}^3$**. The fan speed is increased to **$N_2 = 1750 \text{ RPM}$** to handle hot process air of density **$\rho_2 = 0.95 \text{ kg/m}^3$**. Using the fan laws, calculate the new flow rate, new static pressure, and new power required. Take fan efficiency as **$\eta = 65\%$**. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution (a) — 7 marks**

**Comparison Table (Differences between LEV and General Ventilation):**

| Parameter | Local Exhaust Ventilation (LEV) | General (Dilution) Ventilation |
|-----------|--------------------------------|-------------------------------|
| **Principle** | Captures contaminant at the source before it enters the breathing zone | Dilutes the contaminant concentration in the entire room with fresh air |
| **Air quantity** | Low (typically 0.1–5 m³/s) | High (often 10–100 m³/s) |
| **Capture/Dilution effectiveness** | Very high (90–99%) | Low to moderate (depends on mixing) |
| **Energy consumption** | Lower (less airflow) | Higher (large volumes of air) |
| **Equipment cost** | Moderate (hood, duct, filter) | Lower (simple supply/exhaust fans) |
| **Worker protection** | Independent of worker behavior | Depends on worker staying in clean zone |
| **Suitability for toxic/hazardous contaminants** | Highly suitable | Generally unsuitable for highly toxic substances |
| **Preferred use** | Point-source generation of toxic materials | Low-toxicity, low-generation-rate contaminants in large spaces |

**When LEV is preferred:**
- When the contaminant is **highly toxic** (e.g., beryllium, lead, mercury, asbestos).
- When the **generation rate is moderate to high** and the source is identifiable.
- When the worker's **breathing zone** is close to the source.
- **Example:** Welding fume extraction, paint spray booths, laboratory fume hoods, foundry shakeout operations.

**When General (Dilution) Ventilation is preferred:**
- When the contaminant is of **low toxicity** and the **generation rate is low and uniform**.
- When the **source is widespread** and not localized (e.g., general vehicle exhaust in a parking garage).
- When LEV is **technically or economically infeasible**.
- **Example:** Warehouses with forklift operations, battery charging rooms (hydrogen dilution), garages, machine shops with distributed coolant mist.

*(Award: 4 marks for comparison, 2 marks for situations, 1 mark for examples)*

---

#### **Model Solution (b) — 7 marks**

**Given:**
$$N_1 = 1450 \text{ RPM}, \quad Q_1 = 1.2 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}, \quad \Delta P_1 = 600 \text{ Pa}, \quad \rho_1 = 1.20 \text{ kg/m}^3$$
$$N_2 = 1750 \text{ RPM}, \quad \rho_2 = 0.95 \text{ kg/m}^3, \quad \eta = 0.65$$

**Step 1 — Apply Fan Law 1 to find new flow rate $Q_2$**

The fan law for flow (independent of density):
$$\frac{Q_2}{Q_1} = \frac{N_2}{N_1}$$

$$Q_2 = Q_1 \times \frac{N_2}{N_1} = 1.2 \times \frac{1750}{1450}$$

$$Q_2 = 1.2 \times 1.2069 = 1.448 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

**Step 2 — Apply Fan Law 2 to find new static pressure $\Delta P_2$**

The fan law for pressure:
$$\frac{\Delta P_2}{\Delta P_1} = \left(\frac{N_2}{N_1}\right)^2 \times \frac{\rho_2}{\rho_1}$$

$$\Delta P_2 = 600 \times \left(\frac{1750}{1450}\right)^2 \times \frac{0.95}{1.20}$$

$$\Delta P_2 = 600 \times (1.2069)^2 \times 0.7917$$

$$\Delta P_2 = 600 \times 1.4566 \times 0.7917$$

$$\Delta P_2 = 691.9 \text{ Pa}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the power at the new condition**

The air power (shaft power input to the air stream) is:
$$P_{air,2} = \Delta P_2 \times Q_2 = 691.9 \times 1.448 = 1001.9 \text{ W}$$

The shaft power required (accounting for fan efficiency):
$$P_{shaft,2} = \frac{P_{air,2}}{\eta} = \frac{1001.9}{0.65} = 1541.4 \text{ W}$$

$$P_{shaft,2} \approx 1.54 \text{ kW}$$

**Final Answers:**
$$\boxed{Q_2 \approx 1.45 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}, \quad \Delta P_2 \approx 692 \text{ Pa}, \quad P_{shaft,2} \approx 1.54 \text{ kW}}$$

*(Award: 2 marks for $Q_2$, 2 marks for $\Delta P_2$, 3 marks for power calculation)*

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls in Transport Ventilator Problems — KTU Examiner Observations:**
>
> 1. **Unit Inconsistency (–1 to –2 marks):** Students often mix CFM with m³/s or mm with m. Always convert **all** values to SI units **before** substituting into the formula. Exam tip: write the unit alongside every numerical substitution.
>
> 2. **Forgetting Air Density Variation (–1 mark):** When air temperature changes (e.g., hot exhaust from a furnace), the air density must be recomputed using the **ideal gas law**:
> $$\rho = \frac{P}{R \cdot T}$$
> where $R = 287 \text{ J/(kg·K)}$ for air, and $T$ is in **Kelvin**.
>
> 3. **Using the Blasius formula for rough pipes (–2 marks):** Blasius is valid only for **hydraulically smooth** ducts and $Re < 10^5$. For galvanized steel ducts, always use the **Swamee-Jain** or **Colebrook** equation.
>
> 4. **Neglecting fitting losses (–3 marks):** The most common error in fan selection problems is computing **only the straight-duct friction loss** and ignoring elbow, branch, and hood entry losses. A real system has fittings that can contribute **30–50%** of the total pressure drop.
>
> 5. **Fan Law misuse (–2 marks):** Density appears in the **pressure and power laws** but **NOT** in the flow law. A surprising number of students incorrectly multiply $Q$ by the density ratio.
>
> 6. **Wrong sign of stack effect (–1 mark):** Hot stacks create natural draft that **assists** the fan. Do not add this to fan pressure; instead, **subtract** it from required fan pressure.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### **Rapid Revision Checklist**

- ✅ A **transport ventilator** is an engineering control that **captures, transports, and discharges** airborne contaminants away from workers.
- ✅ The four essential components are: **Hood → Duct → Filter/Cleaner → Fan → Stack**.
- ✅ **Capture velocity** (at the hood face) is determined by the **contaminant type and generation conditions**; refer to **ACGIH manual** values (0.25 to 10 m/s).
- ✅ **Transport velocity** (inside the duct) is determined by the **particle density and size**; values range from **5 m/s (vapors) to 25 m/s (large particles)**.
- ✅ **Duct sizing** uses the continuity equation: $D = \sqrt{4Q / (\pi v)}$.
- ✅ **Friction loss** uses the **Darcy-Weisbach** equation: $\Delta P_f = f \cdot (L/D) \cdot (\rho v^2/2)$.
- ✅ **Friction factor** is computed from the **Swamee-Jain** explicit form of the Colebrook equation.
- ✅ **Total system pressure** = sum of (friction + entry + fitting + filter + stack) losses.
- ✅ **Fan Laws** (memorize the three):
  - $Q \propto N$
  - $\Delta P \propto N^2 \cdot \rho$
  - $P \propto N^3 \cdot \rho$
- ✅ **Dilution ventilation** uses $C = G/(Q \cdot K)$ — only for **low-toxicity** contaminants.
- ✅ **Human factors** in transport ventilator design: noise (< 85 dBA), drafts (< 0.5 m/s cold), visibility, ergonomics, maintenance access.
- ✅ **Standard conditions:** Air at **20 °C, 1.2 kg/m³, 1.81 × 10⁻⁵ Pa·s** — commit these to memory.
- ✅ **Typical fan efficiencies:** Centrifugal 60–75%, Axial 70–85% — needed for power calculations.

### **High-Yield Numbers to Memorize**

| Quantity | Value |
|----------|-------|
| Air density at 20 °C | **1.20 kg/m³** |
| Air dynamic viscosity at 20 °C | **1.81 × 10⁻⁵ Pa·s** |
| Universal gas constant for air | **287 J/(kg·K)** |
| Standard atmospheric pressure | **101,325 Pa** |
| Galvanized steel roughness | **0.15 mm** |
| TLV-TWA noise limit | **85 dBA (8 h)** |
| Transport velocity — light dust | **10–13 m/s** |
| Transport velocity — heavy dust | **13–18 m/s** |
| Capture velocity — welding | **0.5–1.0 m/s** |
| Hood entry loss — flanged | **0.25** |
| Hood entry loss — unflanged | **0.93** |

### **Final Exam Tip**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For any KTU University Exam question on transport ventilators, structure your answer in this order: **(1) state the governing physical principle**, **(2) list the formula with units**, **(3) substitute values with explicit unit conversions**, **(4) compute the result with a clear box around the final answer**, and **(5) provide a one-line engineering interpretation** (e.g., "this velocity exceeds the transport velocity of 13 m/s, hence the design is safe for heavy dust"). This five-step structure consistently earns full marks and demonstrates the engineering judgment that KTU board examiners reward.
